discussion_feedback: De discussie koppelt de uitkomsten terug aan de RQ/hypothesen en bespreekt VRET als werkzamer dan iVET/wachtlijst, met een theoretische interpretatie via habituation/inhibitory learning. Er is ook een kritische reflectie met twee beperkingen (individualisering scenario’s en zelfrapportage) en toekomstig onderzoek (afstemming scenario’s, aanvullende meetmethoden, follow-up). Echter: (1) de kritiek is deels generiek en sommige redeneringen zijn zwak gegrond in de eigen data (bijv. claims over ‘gouden standaard’ iVET en waarom inhibitory learning niet op gang kwam worden besproken zonder dat er procesdata/adherence/therapietrouw of maat voor veiligheids-/vermijdingsgedrag is), (2) de verwachte effecten voor iVET vs wachtlijst worden aangeduid als ‘onverwacht’ maar er is geen solide alternatieve interpretatie op basis van beperkingen van design/metingen/sterkte van statistische power, (3) er worden een paar niet-dubbelzinnige statements gedaan zonder harde data onderbouwing (zoals de timing/‘tussen post en follow-up’, die niet in resultaten bestaat), en (4) de link naar eerdere studies is aanwezig maar minder kritisch in waarom juist adolescenten uitkomsten afwijkend kunnen tonen (bv. dosis, inhoud angsthiërarchie, compliance). Daardoor eerder ‘adequate/developing’ dan ‘good’.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion: De rubriek verwacht dat de redenering naar de main conclusion correct en compleet is én dat kritische reflectie relevant en overtuigend is. Om hoger te gaan: koppel elk belangrijk interpretatiestuk explicieter aan (a) eigen data (effectgroottes/contrast tussen condities, patterns) en (b) de theorie uit de intro. Voeg bovendien beperkingen toe die direct raken aan de onverwachte uitkomst voor iVET/wachtlijst (bv. power, dosis/hoe volledig exposure daadwerkelijk was, procesmaten). Eindig met concrete, data-gedreven implicaties en toekomstig onderzoek die echt volgen uit de gevonden effecten/tegen-effecten.
lang_style_feedback: Taal is overwegend wetenschappelijk en begrijpelijk, met duidelijke opbouw en meestal passende terminologie. Er zijn echter meerdere puntjes die stijl/AP A raken: inconsistenties en kleine fouten in formulering (o.a. enkele onnauwkeurige zinsdelen, een paar typefouten/rommelige overgangen), het resultaten-gedeelte bevat ook opvallende formulering rond post-hoc en ANOVA die de helderheid schaadt. De APA-verwijzingen lijken grotendeels aanwezig in de literatuurlijst en in tekst, maar er zijn ook verwijzingen/formatproblemen in de lijst (o.a. sommige gespelde/verkeerd gecodeerde termen zoals ‘Indiidual’ en inconsistenties in styling). Dat houdt het op ‘adequate’ niveau i.p.v. ‘good’.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style: Het criterium focust op wetenschappelijke taal, heldere structuur (single focus/topic sentences), en beperkte fouten in grammatica/APA/referenties. Om naar ‘good/excellent’ te gaan: (1) minimaliseer type-/spelfouten en inconsistenties in termen, (2) maak statistische zinnen foutloos en eenduidig (bv. p-waardes, df’s, en wat precies wordt geconcludeerd), (3) check APA 7 consequentie in referenties (spelling namen, journal/hoofdletters/diacritica, consistent formaat), en (4) voorkom inhoudelijk verwarrende formuleringen die de lezer tot verkeerde interpretatie kunnen leiden.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a reasonably clear topic, societal rationale (waitlists, burden, economic framing), and a coherent conceptual line leading from inhibitory learning/exposure principles to variability and safety signals, culminating in a clear comparative expectation (therapist-led > self-led; both > waitlist). However, the theoretical link is sometimes asserted rather than argued step-by-step, key definitions (e.g., “safety signals”) are incomplete, and the positioning/punchline for the self-led vs therapist-led comparison is somewhat repetitive and late. Prior research is used as building blocks, but there are notable mismatches with the course’s required “core articles” set-up (theoretical framework and prior research aren’t tightly integrated into a structured research gap for the exact design).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasis is on: (1) clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis culminating line of reasoning; (2) theoretical framework used consistently; (3) responsible use of existing empirical/theoretical work. Strengths: societal relevance and a plausible inhibitory learning-based rationale for variability/safety-signal differences; a comparative hypothesis is stated. Gaps to improve rubric-adjacent performance: tighten the conceptual definitions (especially “safety signals”), ensure every move from theory→design→hypothesis is explicitly argued, and ensure the prior-research section is organized as building blocks that logically lead to the specific RQ without repetition or late payoff.
methods_feedback: Methods include participants recruitment, screening/exclusion criteria, allocation to conditions, and a general description of the interventions and timing. Still, several required specifics are missing or unclear for replication and for judging whether the design matches the claims: (1) the randomization unit/timing is described but not the actual unit for allocation (after screening yes, but not whether any stratification occurred), (2) key details of intervention standardization are largely absent (e.g., therapist-provided content/structure differences, exposure hierarchy/number of scenes, fidelity monitoring), (3) waitlist ethics/controls are unclear beyond “assigned to VRET after 8 weeks” (what happens during the 8 weeks besides assessments?), (4) the statistical analysis plan is not sufficiently specified (no ANOVA model details beyond a vague statement, no handling of outliers beyond “not excluded,” no missingness handling). Some measure details are provided, but not enough on scoring/interpretation and procedural-material separation.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasis is on: study design coherence with RQ/hypotheses and completeness/correctness of participants, materials, procedures. Strengths: inclusion/exclusion criteria, recruitment sources, allocation to three conditions, general timing (8 weeks; pre/post). Gaps: insufficient standardization/fidelity details of the VRET protocols, unclear waitlist control activity, missing key replication details (e.g., therapist-led vs self-led differences beyond “oversight”), and no clear statistical analysis plan/specification. To reach ‘Good’, methods must be complete and correct enough that a reader could replicate the design and judge whether confounds were controlled.
results_feedback: The results section is not compatible with the described design and is largely incomplete/incorrect relative to expected reporting structure. There is no usable Table 1/Table 2 content (the tables are blank/minimal placeholders), the ANOVA dfs appear implausible (e.g., F(2, 10484.21)), and the results do not match what is later claimed in the Discussion (e.g., the Discussion states an increase in waitlist pre-to-post, but the Results only say there was “significant change between pre and post” for all three conditions without reporting the direction/means). The post-hoc p-values are also suspiciously imprecise (e.g., “p = 1.000” at multiple points) and there are no effect sizes, confidence intervals, or descriptives per timepoint in the text. Overall, the statistical reporting is not coherent enough to support the paper’s conclusions.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasis is on correctness/completeness and relevance to stated design/procedure. Reporting should include interpretable descriptive statistics (means/SDs) and a coherent statistical narrative matching the design (condition × time). Strengths: attempts to report ANOVA effects and post-hoc comparisons; mentions assumption checks. Major issues: implausible/incorrect dfs, missing/empty tables, failure to report directionally interpretable means per timepoint, and inconsistency with discussion claims. To reach ‘Adequate/Good’, the section must be internally coherent and include the core descriptive and inferential results in a way that directly addresses the hypotheses.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states main conclusions and attempts to connect outcomes to the inhibitory learning model (variability and safety signals), and it addresses a striking waitlist pattern with critical ideas (sample size, external events, therapist effects). However, it does not demonstrate correct construct-level interpretation of the reported results (given the results/reporting issues), it provides limitations that are somewhat generic and not always tightly linked to specific design/analysis shortcomings, and it misses concrete future research recommendations with sufficient specificity (e.g., how to operationalize safety-signal reduction in self- vs therapist-led VRET; how to control for waitlist-related deterioration). The critical reflection is partially convincing, but not deep enough given the apparent inconsistencies in the Results section.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasis is on: correct main conclusion; convincing integration of findings with theory and prior research; relevant critical reflection and limitations. Strengths: compares self-led/therapist-led and both versus waitlist; attempts to explain unexpected waitlist changes and relates them to sampling/external events. Gaps: the discussion leans on theoretical claims (variability/safety signals) without clearly demonstrating that the specific results test those constructs; limitations are somewhat generic and future research recommendations are not sufficiently concrete and method-linked. For ‘Good’, the discussion must clearly reflect the actual reported results and show deeper study-specific critical reasoning.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is acceptable and scientific tone is maintained, but there are substantial issues with precision and professionalism: incorrect/unfinished table content in the text, odd phrasing (“negative effect,” “wholly unexpected,” “negative effect on social anxiety symptoms” without clear direction), and at least one clear reference formatting/accuracy problem (Wolitzky-Taylor reference in-text has an author formatting error). Paragraph structure is sometimes repetitive and the argument does not consistently follow from what is evidenced in the Results. APA/reference consistency and statistical language (dfs, p-values, reporting) are below an adequate student level.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasis is on: near-faultless scientific language, clear structure (single focus and topical sentences), minimal ambiguity/vagueness, and near-correct APA/statistical formatting. Strengths: generally readable scientific style. Major downgrades: unclear or unprofessional phrasing, missing/placeholder table content, and inconsistencies/incorrectness in statistical reporting and at least one reference formatting/accuracy issue. To reach ‘Good/Excellent’, the text must be polished for clarity and ensure statistical/APA details are accurate and consistently formatted.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a clear societal relevance and a generally coherent storyline: SAD prevalence/impairment and waitlists → inhibitory learning and the role of variability/safety signals → VR exposure and prior VRET trials → gap about self-led vs therapist-led VRET → RQ/hypothesis in prose. However, the theoretical mechanism is used somewhat loosely (e.g., variability is asserted as the driver without clearly linking to what differs between self-led vs therapist-led beyond “safety signals”), and several citations in the narrative do not match the paper’s logic (e.g., Mühlberger is used for phobias broadly rather than directly to SAD). Also, the “current study” justification and design rationale (why these two active conditions and why this specific comparison timing) are not fully argued with precision. Overall: reasonably adequate line of reasoning, but not yet at the level of coherent/convincing argumentation expected for higher grades.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: line of reasoning culminating in RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis; coherence; responsible reference use. This introduction has adequate societal relevance and a reasonably clear RQ/hypothesis in prose, but the theoretical substantiation is not consistently “used again” with tight causal links for each condition difference (variability vs safety signals) and some reference usage is somewhat off-line with the specific SAD/VRET/self-led vs therapist-led comparison. Strengthen argumentation by explicitly stating the constructs, the hypothesized causal mechanism, and how the exact design manipulation tests each construct; ensure prior research is used as building blocks rather than broad background.
methods_feedback: Methods are partially complete: screening procedure, inclusion/exclusion criteria, random assignment to three conditions, basic session structure (8 weekly sessions, 45 minutes), and an instrument (LSAS-SR) are described. Yet key elements required for replication and for evaluating internal validity are missing or incorrect/inconsistent: (1) the experimental design description is internally unclear (the results table suggests 2 timepoints; methods say pre/post only, but earlier text implies waitlist assessed pre/post during the wait and then assigned to VRET—no follow-up outcome is measured after assignment, so the waitlist ethical component is not operationalized), (2) no details are provided about therapist-led vs self-led equivalence controls (e.g., same VR scenarios/number and difficulty of exposures, homework/engagement monitoring, adherence, therapist interaction content, treatment fidelity), (3) participant allocation numbers/demographics in the appendix-like table conflict with the stated final N in the results section (and the dropout statement contradicts baseline recruitment), and (4) the method does not specify reliability/scoring details beyond the general score range, nor does it provide any analysis plan beyond “Factorial Mixed ANOVA” without defining repeated-measures structure, assumptions details, or multiple comparisons procedure. These issues place it below “complete and correct.”
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: coherent design clearly related to RQ/hypotheses; participants/materials/procedures described comprehensibly; methods complete and correct. While many basics are present (screening, cut-off, exclusion criteria, randomization, session length, measure), key design/implementation details are missing (treatment fidelity/adherence, scenario standardization, therapist contact content, equivalence across conditions) and the allocation/N/timepoint logic is inconsistent with results (dropout count vs N, waitlist protocol vs measured outcomes). To score higher: provide complete, consistent participant numbers, clear timeline, operationalization of each intervention condition, and an analysis plan consistent with repeated measures and multiple comparisons.
results_feedback: Results reporting is severely incomplete/incoherent relative to the study description and the sample-results expectations. Major problems: (1) the text claims “There were no dropouts… among the 75 participants” but the study described 86 screened/allocated; the paper later includes an LSAS scoring table with N=25 per condition, but the ANOVA uses extreme dfs (e.g., F(2, 10484.21)) that are not possible with typical participant counts, suggesting either fabricated/incorrect statistics or severe reporting errors; (2) the dependent-variable scoring basis and actual descriptive statistics are missing—Table 1 is empty/placeholder (“Per condition the descriptive statistics…”), Table 2 is referenced but not provided with values; (3) assumption checks are said to be ‘met’ “as shown in Table 1” yet Table 1 is empty; (4) p-values and comparisons are inconsistent with typical ANOVA/post-hoc reporting (e.g., p < .002, then multiple comparisons claimed without correction, while later narrative mentions Figure 1 but figure content is absent). Because of these statistical/reporting errors, results fall into “insufficient / incorrect and/or incomplete.”
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: data analysis and reporting correct/complete/coherent/relevant, and demonstrating appropriate statistical reasoning. The results do not meet this level: statistics (df/F values) appear inconsistent with sample size, key descriptive tables/figures are missing, assumption checks are referenced but not shown, and post-hoc reporting lacks clarity/correction. Higher scoring requires correct ANOVA structure consistent with design (N and repeated measures), complete descriptive tables, consistent dfs, and clear reporting of effect sizes and multiple-comparison adjustments where appropriate.
discussion_feedback: Discussion attempts to interpret the pattern and connect it to inhibitory learning (variability and safety signals) and to prior VR literature. It also contains a critical reflection with several plausible limitations (waitlist increase, sample size, single therapist, restricting to VRET only). However, the core interpretation is not well grounded because the results section is not clearly reported (no actual effect sizes, no clear directionality for the waitlist pattern, no means/SDs). The discussion also makes claims that mirror what would be expected from hypothesis testing (e.g., therapist-led stronger) without demonstrating them from reported numbers. The waitlist explanation is speculative and uses concepts/citations that were not established in the introduction (e.g., negative life events reasoning), and it lacks at least two concrete future research suggestions that directly follow from the methodological/statistical problems (it stays at a general “use larger sample sizes / more therapists / add CBT conditions” level). Hence: partially relevant critical reflection and some linkage to theory, but overall not convincingly supported.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: reasoning culminating in the main conclusion correct/complete/coherent; critical reflection relevant and convincingly argued; responsible reference use. The discussion engages theory and includes limitations and future directions, but because the results are not clearly/accurately reported, the interpretation cannot be convincingly supported. Improve by grounding each claim in reported outcomes (means/SEs/effect sizes), explicitly separating expected vs unexpected findings, and providing at least two more concrete future research steps tied to the specific limitations (e.g., measurement timing issues for waitlist, treatment fidelity, therapist variability, adherence monitoring).
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and scientific, with a recognizable structure (intro/methods/results/discussion). APA style is partially inconsistent: some reference formatting issues (e.g., missing capitalization/formatting consistency, stray punctuation/typos like “new to the new technology”), and one malformed reference entry in discussion (Wolitzky-Taylor & LeBeau citation appears in-text with a parenthesis and malformed year/article format). There are also some unclear or ambiguous statements (e.g., “negative effect” wording, confusing descriptions of what the waitlist does after 8 weeks with no measurement). Paragraphing sometimes supports single-topic flow, but there is redundancy and occasional awkward phrasing (e.g., repeated “negative effect on social anxiety symptoms” and “mirroring findings”). Overall: mid-range clarity.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: near faultless scientific language, clear structure with single-focus paragraphs/topical sentences, minimal vague statements, and correct APA referencing/layout. The paper is mostly readable, but APA/reference formatting has noticeable errors and there are some unclear/awkward phrases and ambiguous statements that affect precision. To score higher: ensure references are formatted consistently (including correct years/authors/italics where required), avoid malformed citations, reduce redundancy, and use more precise construct language (e.g., specify whether change is decrease/increase by timepoint and condition).

intro_feedback: The introduction provides clear societal relevance and a generally coherent line toward the research question (self-led vs therapist-led VRET vs waitlist) and a hypothesis grounded in the inhibitory learning model (variability/safety signals). It also includes several relevant core experimental studies and an explicit rationale for the comparison. However, the argumentation is sometimes vague/inaccurate: the mapping from the inhibitory learning model to the specific operational contrast (self-home vs therapist presence) is asserted rather than tightly argued, and the section about “variability” and its link to the self-led vs therapist-led difference is not clearly developed. Additionally, some referenced study details do not align well with the stated design needs (e.g., which comparisons answer which gap), and the transition to a crisp, testable RQ/hypotheses is present but not fully crystallized (hypothesis is described, but the exact directional expectations for time/course and the primary contrast are not tightly specified).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric-aligned strengths: societal relevance is present early; the topic is appropriate; key concepts (SAD, avoidance, exposure, inhibitory learning; safety signals/variability) are introduced; prior experimental studies are used to motivate the gap (self-led vs therapist-led; waitlist comparison). Rubric-aligned weaknesses: the line of reasoning toward the hypothesis is not always “convincing” because (a) the theoretical mechanism is mapped to the self-led vs therapist-led difference with limited argumentation and sometimes conflates variability with safety signals; (b) the research question and hypotheses are not crisply operationalized as primary comparisons/time course expectations; (c) some prior research is summarized in ways that are not fully integrated into a step-by-step storyline (some transition points read like listing rather than building).
methods_feedback: The Methods section contains many required elements (participants recruited via screening; inclusion/exclusion criteria; group allocation; LSAS-SR description; overall procedure with 8 weekly sessions; session length; where VRET occurred). However, critical replication details and internal consistency issues prevent it from reaching “adequate”. Key problems: (1) the randomization scheme is unclear (how exactly randomization is done at which stage; whether the screening cut-off participants are randomized immediately; whether waitlist assignment to VRET after 8 weeks affects blinding/expectancy); (2) the design logic is unclear for a mixed ANOVA because the waitlist group appears to receive no intervention during the measurement interval (but results are inconsistent with that in the paper); (3) materials and intervention standardization are under-specified (what exactly differs between self-led and therapist-led beyond presence; what supervision entails; whether exposure content is identical); (4) the LSAS-SR scoring details are partially correct but some details are inconsistent or too imprecise (e.g., “scores range from 0 to 144” but only a brief item example is given; no scoring procedure beyond ranges); (5) “randomly distributed” is said, but there is no allocation concealment/blinding mention (not required, but the lack of other design clarity makes it worse).
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric-aligned strengths: participant selection (screening via SCID-5-CV; cut-off; in/exclusion criteria) is described; intervention dose (8 weekly sessions; 45 minutes) is given; condition allocation is described; primary instrument (LSAS-SR) is specified with items/subscales and a plausible scoring range; procedure chronology is present. Rubric-aligned weaknesses: details required for “complete and correct” are missing: (1) intervention standardization between self-led and therapist-led is insufficiently specified (what therapist does in sessions 2–7; what is identical/controlled); (2) randomization and assignment timing are unclear (especially for waitlist and the post-waitlist crossover to VRET); (3) measurement points and what “post-assessment” captures in waitlist vs active are not aligned with the later statistical narrative; (4) reporting of attrition is inconsistent with Results narrative; (5) the statistical-analysis plan is not described in Methods (ANOVA type assumptions, use of sphericity correction, handling of missingness/outliers) even if some is mentioned in Results.
results_feedback: The Results section is not sufficiently reliable for the rubric because the statistical reporting and consistency with the described design are seriously flawed/incomplete. Major issues include: (1) impossible/incorrect ANOVA degrees of freedom and test statistics (e.g., F(2, 10484.21) and F(1, 4139.63) suggest computation/typing errors); (2) the paper states n=75 with “no dropouts” but the Methods describe 11 dropouts and a final N=75—then the Results also mention inclusion of all participants, yet the table references do not match the earlier counts; (3) key descriptive tables/values are missing or empty placeholders (Table 1 shown as blank, Table 2 mentioned but not provided in-text, figure without plotted values); (4) no effect sizes are reported; (5) post-hoc p-values are given without specifying which correction (and there’s contradictory claim about outlier handling—“not excluded”); (6) there is a narrative claim in Discussion about an increase in waitlist, but Results text does not provide the specific means/pre-post changes to support that. These problems justify an “insufficient” level for Results due to incorrect/incoherent reporting relative to the stated design.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric-aligned strengths: attempts to present main effects, an interaction, post-hoc comparisons, and references to assumption checks/boxplots. Rubric-aligned weaknesses: the statistical reporting is not correct/complete: degrees of freedom/test statistics appear erroneous; key descriptive statistics/means are missing; no effect sizes/CI are provided; post-hoc correction procedure and which comparisons are used relative to hypotheses are not clearly reported; consistency between sample size/dropout narrative and the ANOVA outputs is not established. Because the Results do not coherently and correctly report the analysis in relation to the described design, they cannot reach “adequate” or higher.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion attempts to interpret findings via the inhibitory learning model and compares active conditions to prior VRET RCTs, and it includes a critical reflection section with several plausible limitations (sample size; single therapist; lack of CBT components; unexpected waitlist change). However, the reasoning is undermined by mismatches with the Results: the Results section does not provide the concrete pattern (especially for the waitlist pre-to post change) and the Discussion claims “negative effect” and a specific superiority pattern without presenting corresponding descriptive results. Critical reflection is present but not convincingly grounded because the paper cannot refute/justify the direction of effects with the reported statistics. Also, some statements are overly categorical (e.g., concluding VRET “has a negative effect” rather than “reduces symptoms” depending on scale direction) and some references to CBT superiority are not integrated with the inhibitory learning framework or the exact design limitations. Overall, the critical reflection exists but is not convincingly argued for using the paper’s own results.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric-aligned strengths: main conclusion is attempted; interpretation references the inhibitory learning framework; comparisons to prior VRET studies are made; critical reflection includes more than one limitation category (limitations in waitlist change, sample size, therapist variability, intervention scope). Rubric-aligned weaknesses: the discussion does not convincingly connect the theoretical interpretation to the actual data because Results are not sufficiently clear/credible for the claimed effect patterns; the critical reflection is somewhat speculative (“negative life events” type explanations) without measurements; implications are not always consistent with the scale direction and the study’s actual findings. This prevents the Discussion from reaching “good/excellent” coherence and convincing argumentation.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is moderate, with generally understandable paragraphs and an academic tone. However, there are recurring issues: informal word choices (“negative effect” wording confusion), grammatical problems and punctuation, and some unclear phrasing (“waitlist inspired … as close to a true control condition as ethically possible” without precision). APA/reference formatting is mostly acceptable, but there are noticeable inconsistencies and errors (e.g., incomplete/incorrect citation formatting in places, a reference with parentheses/formatting issues, and an on-page citation missing proper author/year in one place). Scientific style is not consistently precise (single-focus topical sentences are sometimes violated; connections between claims and evidence are occasionally unclear).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric-aligned strengths: overall structure is present (Introduction/Methods/Results/Discussion) and many sentences are understandable. Rubric-aligned weaknesses: scientific phrasing is sometimes imprecise (e.g., wording that could invert meaning of findings); there are grammar/wording issues that sometimes reduce clarity; APA/reference formatting has inconsistencies; some paragraphs are not strictly single-focus with clear topical sentences. Improvements would mainly be tightening wording for construct-level precision, correcting reference formatting, and ensuring clear sentence-to-evidence links.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a clear topic, substantial societal relevance, and a generally coherent storyline from social anxiety → exposure/inhibitory learning → VR variability/safety signals → gap (self-led vs therapist-led) → research expectation. The RQ/hypotheses are implied but not explicitly stated as a precise research question and testable hypotheses tied to the design. Prior research is used mostly appropriately, but there are content/accuracy issues (e.g., describing Kampmann et al. and exclusion criteria in a way that is not fully supported, and mixing publication years/expectations). Overall, the line of reasoning is present and sensible, but not yet fully “argument-complete” for the rubric’s higher levels.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric emphasizes (i) a line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, (ii) coherent linkage of theory to expectations, and (iii) responsible use of existing empirical/theoretical work. This paper scores well on broad relevance and an overall coherent build-up, but does not reach higher bands because the RQ/hypothesis are largely implicit (not explicitly stated as a testable research question + hypotheses) and several prior-research claims are not tightly/accurately integrated, which weakens “convincingly argued for” at the top level.
methods_feedback: Methods are described in many places, but key elements needed to replicate and to evaluate scientific coherence are missing or unclear. Major issues: (1) participants are said to be randomized after screening, but the flow is inconsistent (86 screened eligible, 11 drop out, final equal 25 per group = 75; results later claim 75 with ANOVA dfs that look inconsistent). (2) allocation procedure for the waitlist condition is conceptually odd/unclear (waitlist is assigned to receive either VRET type immediately after 8 weeks, which affects interpretation). (3) No details on session content standardization, therapist role equivalence, fidelity checks, adherence/compliance, or how much guidance differs beyond “supervised”. (4) Materials lack essential scoring specifics (items per subscale, scoring procedure, and scale anchors for fear/avoidance). (5) Procedure includes psychoeducation/evaluation but does not specify what exactly happens in “evaluation of thoughts” for the self-led condition versus therapist-led, nor timing/duration of exposure within sessions. These problems prevent the Methods from reaching “complete and correct”.
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric requires the design to be coherent and clearly related to the stated RQ/hypotheses, and participants/materials/procedures to be described completely and correctly. Here, the main design is described, but important replication-critical details are missing (standardization/fidelity, adherence, exact differences between self-led vs therapist-led beyond supervision, scoring specifics), and the participant flow/randomization is inconsistent with later reported sample/analysis details. Also, inclusion/exclusion and timing are not fully operationalized in a way that supports “complete and correct.”
results_feedback: Results reporting is not aligned with the described design/statistical approach and contains major omissions/inconsistencies. The ANOVA specification is not credible (e.g., F(df) values: F(2, 10484.21) is implausible; F(1, 4139.63) similarly; and the stated N/df logic does not fit the sample size). The paper references “assumptions… met, as shown in Table 1”, but Table 1 is blank. The results section does not provide actual LSAS-SR means/SDs in Table 2 (Table 2 is not shown), yet claims are made. Post-hoc p-values are given without specifying which correction method was used and without reporting effect sizes or whether tests match the interaction pattern. The discussion later claims a waitlist increase, but the provided results text does not include that pattern (and with only pre/post measurement stated, the waitlist interaction is not clearly reported). Overall, the analysis/reporting is incomplete and not dependable.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric requires correct, complete, coherent reporting of data analysis relevant to the stated design/procedure. The paper does not meet this because the ANOVA degrees of freedom/statistics appear implausible, tables are missing/empty, descriptive statistics are not provided, post-hoc comparisons are incomplete (no correction method/effect sizes), and the waitlist pattern referenced in Discussion is not clearly supported by the presented Results text. Overall, this aligns with the “incomplete/incorrect” levels.
discussion_feedback: The discussion attempts to connect findings to inhibitory learning (variability, safety signals) and to prior VRET RCTs, and it provides several limitations. However, it does not convincingly reconcile the results with the hypothesized pattern in a disciplined way: the waitlist is described as increasing significantly, but the results section as provided does not clearly report that pattern (and the ANOVA/reporting is inconsistent). The critical reflection is somewhat relevant (sample size, therapist variability, lack of CBT comparators, unexpected waitlist change), but the arguments rely on speculative causal explanations without connecting to concrete design details or actual statistical outcomes (and there is confusion around whether the study demonstrates “negative effect” vs symptom change relative to control). Theoretical integration is present, yet the “main conclusion” and its evidence are weakened by the unreliable/unclear results reporting.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric expects correct/complete main conclusion reasoning and a relevant, convincing critical reflection connected to the actual results and theory. While limitations are identified (sample size, therapist effects, scope limited to VRET, unexpected waitlist change), the discussion does not convincingly ground its main claims because the results reporting is inconsistent/insufficiently specified. Theoretical linkage is attempted but not fully convincing given the evidential weaknesses.
lang_style_feedback: Generally readable and often uses scientific tone, with some paragraphing and signposting. However, there are multiple issues that reduce clarity: run-on/overlong sentences, occasional imprecise wording (“negative effect on symptoms” phrasing), and several APA/statistical/formatting problems (incorrect/odd table references; missing tables; inconsistent reporting; reference formatting issues such as punctuation/abbreviation inconsistencies; a malformed in-text citation in Wolitzky-Taylor entry). Content clarity is also affected by the absence of key numeric results in the Results section. This places it around developing/adequate for style-language requirements rather than good/excellent.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric rewards scientific clarity, structured paragraphs with clear topical focus, low grammar/spelling/APA errors, and minimal vague or ambiguous statements. The paper is generally understandable, but clarity is harmed by missing tables/numbers, some imprecise phrasing, and multiple APA/statistical formatting issues (including incomplete/incorrect table content and inconsistent reference details). This fits an intermediate band rather than good/excellent.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a broadly relevant societal and clinical problem, and it sets up an inhibitory learning account (Craske et al., 2014) to motivate variability and safety signals. However, the line of reasoning culminates in an RQ/hypothesis only implicitly (it states an expectation, but the RQ/hypothesis are not stated with sufficient clarity and scientific focus at the end of the introduction). Theoretical constructs (variability, safety signals) are used, but the mapping from these constructs to *self-led vs therapist-led* (and what differs operationally) is sometimes asserted rather than tightly argued. Prior research is summarized, but the synthesis is not fully structured into a clear building-block storyline from the 4 required experimental core studies to the exact design and novelty of the current study. References are mostly used responsibly, but there are notable mismatches with course requirements (e.g., missing/unclear explicit integration of the mandated core studies and some reference choices that are used as justification without being clearly connected to the constructs/hypotheses).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: clarity of the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis, and use of theory to argue toward it. This paper has relevant content and a partial storyline, but the culminating RQ/hypothesis is not clearly and explicitly framed at the end of the introduction, and the argumentation sometimes relies on assertions rather than careful construct-to-design linking. Prior research is summarized but not consistently synthesized into building blocks that logically justify the exact comparison (self-led vs therapist-led vs waitlist) with explicit stated expectations.
methods_feedback: Method is incomplete relative to the rubric: participants, materials, and procedure are only partly described, and key replicability details are missing or inconsistent with the reported sample and design. Examples: (1) The report says a cut-off screening determined eligibility, but it does not specify the exact procedure for random allocation to conditions (e.g., allocation concealment/stratification) beyond a general randomizer. (2) The final number of participants is inconsistent throughout the paper (108 recruited → 86 eligible; then “11 dropped out”; later Results state “no dropouts among 75 participants,” and ANOVA df values look implausible), so the participant flow is not coherent. (3) The design description in Methods is minimal: waitlist participants were randomly assigned to VRET after 8 weeks, but it is unclear whether they are analyzed as waitlist (pre/post only) and whether they crossover affects analysis/ethics. (4) Materials section provides some detail about the VR program but lacks specifics needed for replication (exact exposure hierarchy/order, VR session content differences across conditions, therapist role/session structure beyond “overseen,” psychoeducation content, adherence/monitoring, and how treatment fidelity was ensured). (5) The LSAS-SR description is somewhat general; it lacks scoring details like fear/avoidance scoring procedure and scoring range interpretation beyond 0–144, and it does not state administration conditions (where/when, supervised vs unsupervised). (6) Planned analyses are not described (no mention of main effects/interactions expected, assumption checks specifics, correction for multiple comparisons, effect sizes). Overall, the study design is not described with “complete and correct” information and is too inconsistent to allow replication.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: coherence of study design with RQ/hypotheses, and complete/correct description of participants, materials, and procedures. The paper does not meet this standard because participant flow and numbers are inconsistent across sections, key methodological details needed for replication are missing (treatment fidelity, exposure content standardization, adherence, therapist procedures), and the analysis plan/handling of crossover and missing data is not fully specified. This prevents the Methods from being considered “complete and correct.”
results_feedback: Results are largely not reportable in a way that matches the course expectations and provided sample template: key numerical reporting is missing (Table 1 and Table 2 contain almost no usable content in the provided text; there are no means/SDs per time/condition in a proper table/figure form; Figure 1 is blank). Statistical reporting has major problems: degrees of freedom appear impossible (e.g., F(2, 10484.21) and F(1, 4139.63)), and the ANOVA structure is inconsistent with the stated factors/time points (it says two levels of time, but then post-hoc/figures suggest more complexity). Also, the Results claim that assumptions were tested and met “as shown in Table 1,” but Tables shown are not sufficient to verify this. The post-hoc reporting provides p-values but no test statistics, correction procedure, or effect sizes. Additionally, there is a critical inconsistency between Results and Discussion: Discussion states that waitlist increased significantly, but Results section text indicates “all three conditions showed a significant change,” without giving the direction/magnitude; the later claim of a “significant increase in waitlist” is not supported by reported means. Overall, data analysis/reporting is not coherent or complete and does not demonstrate statistical reasoning depth.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: correct, complete, coherent data analysis and reporting relevant to the design and procedure, with appropriate statistical reasoning. The paper does not meet this because the statistical reporting contains implausible degrees of freedom, critical descriptive statistics are missing/incomplete, assumption checks are not verifiable from the provided tables, and direction/effect estimates are not clearly presented. The results section therefore cannot convincingly connect to the hypotheses.
discussion_feedback: Discussion attempts to interpret the pattern using inhibitory learning (variability/safety signals) and compares with prior VRET studies, but the scientific reasoning is weakened by misalignment with the (insufficient) results reporting and by gaps in critical reflection. Main conclusion is stated broadly, but the specific hypothesis is not clearly restated and connected to the actual statistics that would support it (e.g., effect sizes, direction for each condition, and whether differences are at post only or across time). The waitlist finding is discussed as “wholly unexpected,” yet the Results section does not clearly report the direction and magnitude for waitlist, making the argument difficult to substantiate. Critical reflection provides several limitations (small sample, single therapist, VRET-only vs CBT), which is good in principle, but some limitations are speculative and not clearly tied to what the study actually did or measured (e.g., suggesting a life-event mechanism without any data collection). The discussion lacks at least two concrete future research ideas that directly follow from the specific design/results issues and would be testable (it mentions larger sample sizes and more therapists, but does not clearly propose a structured future design, measures, or hypotheses). References are generally used responsibly, but there are some questionable citation/format issues (e.g., a 2023 Wolitzky-Taylor & LeBeau reference used without full formatting and potentially not aligned with the course’s argumentation needs).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: correct main conclusion, relevant critical reflection convincingly argued for. While limitations and theoretical framing are present, the discussion’s interpretations are not convincingly anchored in clearly reported results (especially for the waitlist change), and the critique relies on speculative mechanisms without supporting data. Future recommendations are somewhat present but not sufficiently concrete and testable, and the mapping from theory constructs to the obtained pattern is not demonstrated with adequate evidential grounding.
lang_style_feedback: Language is understandable overall, but style and precision problems are frequent. There are several unclear or unscientific phrasings (e.g., “negative effect” used without operational definition, some informal causal explanations in Discussion), and a number of sentence-level issues that reduce clarity (long sentences, missing connectors, and occasional grammar/spelling problems such as “the person being aware of the fact”). APA/statistical formatting is also inconsistent (missing/odd tables/figures, incomplete or incorrect capitalization, and reference formatting inconsistencies—e.g., in-text use vs reference list for some sources). Paragraph structure is sometimes not strictly single-focus, especially in Discussion where multiple limitations are bundled. Overall, it is below the “good” threshold for clear academic scientific writing.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: near-faultless scientific language/structure and minimal APA/layout/reference issues. The paper has understandable content but contains recurring clarity/stylistic problems, some vague/ambiguous statements, and multiple APA/statistical presentation issues (notably figures/tables and formatting). Paragraphing is sometimes multi-focus, reducing the single-focus clarity expected at this level.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a generally understandable line of reasoning from SAD prevalence/treatment barriers to inhibitory learning, variability, and why VR (and then VRET, plus therapist-led vs self-led) matters. The RQ/hypotheses are present and mostly coherent. However, several argumentation issues prevent a higher score: (1) the rationale for variability and the inhibitory-learning link is somewhat asserted rather than tightly reasoned for the specific comparison (self-led vs therapist-led), (2) prior research is sometimes described in a way that is not always aligned/accurate to the paper’s own design (e.g., the “failed to include exclusion criteria” claim for Kampmann is stated without evidence and the core set required by the course is not used as prescribed in the narrative), and (3) the societal/scientific “gap” and the specific contribution of *this* study are present but not strongly argued in a structured “punchline” way. References are often relevant, but there are also issues with reference currency/appropriateness (e.g., Freeman 2017 is a general VR review rather than a direct experimental anchor for the comparison argument).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria: the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis should be correct, coherent, and convincingly argued with an appropriate RQ supported by relevant, up-to-date empirical/theoretical work. Current issues to address for a higher band: strengthen the explicit gap/punchline (why this exact comparison matters now), tighten the theoretical-to-hypothesis link for *self-led vs therapist-led* (what constructs differ and how they predict the measured outcome), and ensure prior research is integrated as building blocks rather than listed; also keep references strictly relevant to the argument (avoid overly general sources when they don’t directly support claims).
methods_feedback: The design is mostly coherent (3-arm condition, pre/post, 8-week VRET vs waitlist, random allocation), and key inclusion/exclusion criteria and sample demographics are provided. Still, important Method details are missing or unclear relative to the course guide: (1) participants are said to be allocated to conditions and to have 25 per group, but earlier it says 86 were allocated and 11 dropped out—yet results claim 75 participants with “no dropouts”; this inconsistency undermines clarity. (2) The randomization unit and timing for the waitlist condition are described, but the exact post-8-week crossover/extent for the waitlist is vague. (3) The VRET intervention description lacks crucial comparability details (exact variability manipulation, session content structure, therapist contact time for therapist-led vs any interaction for self-led, therapist training/protocol, adherence/fidelity checks). (4) Materials section does not give a proper LSAS-SR scoring description beyond total range and general item examples; it also does not clearly specify subscale scoring/what is used in analysis (total score vs subscales). (5) Planned analyses (e.g., mixed ANOVA specification, correction for sphericity, handling of outliers) are not fully reported (and results/assumptions claims appear inconsistent with the sample/design). Overall, methods are not “complete and correct.”
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria: study design coherent and clearly related to RQ/hypotheses; participants, materials, and procedures described comprehensibly; methods complete and correct with no major inconsistencies. Key rubric gaps here are internal consistency (sample size/dropout reporting), missing core intervention protocol details for replication (session structure, therapist contact/fidelity, variability manipulation, duration/content across conditions), and insufficient operational detail for measurement/scoring and analysis plan (what LSAS-SR score exactly, how analyses handle assumptions/outliers, and what exact post-hoc framework/corrections are planned).
results_feedback: Results are not sufficiently aligned with the stated design and sample size, and several reporting elements are missing. Major issues: (1) the paper reports “no dropouts among the 75 participants,” but methods described dropouts and the allocation numbers are inconsistent. (2) Table 1 is empty and Table 2 is not actually provided; Figure 1 is also not meaningfully presented (no axis values, condition/time means). (3) The ANOVA degrees of freedom are implausible/inconsistent with the design: for a 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed ANOVA with N≈75, the reported F tests (e.g., F(2, 10484.21), F(1, 4139.63), F(2, 8921.25)) are not credible. (4) Assumption checks are stated as “met,” but the relevant tests/values are not shown. (5) The post-hoc p-values are reported without specifying which correction was used (e.g., Holm) and without specifying which comparisons were run (pairwise condition comparisons at post, and change scores). Because the correctness/complete reporting criterion is not met, this sits in the developing range.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria: data analysis/reporting correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to stated design, including demonstrating in-depth knowledge of statistical reasoning. To reach a higher band, results should include complete descriptive tables (baseline and post means/SDs), the actual mixed-model ANOVA results with plausible dfs for the given design and sample size, assumption check outputs (or clearly stated tests with statistics), and a transparent post-hoc procedure (which comparisons, which correction). Tables/figures must not be empty placeholders; p-values must match the reported analyses.
discussion_feedback: The discussion attempts to interpret the findings through inhibitory learning (variability and safety signals) and compares to prior VRET studies, and it includes limitations. However, the main conclusion and interpretation are not fully justified by the presented results: (1) the claim that the waitlist increased significantly is not supported because the results section as written is internally inconsistent/insufficient (no means, no actual waitlist change information provided in text/table/figure). (2) The interpretation that therapist-led “has a stronger negative effect” than self-led is asserted without reported effect sizes/means. (3) Prior research is referenced, but the discussion does not systematically compare specific expectations vs actual patterns (e.g., what exactly happened to each condition across time). (4) Critical reflection exists, but it is largely generic and speculative (negative life events, therapist event, sample degradation) rather than tied to concrete design factors and actual data patterns. (5) Future directions are present only partially; they are more “recommendations” than specific future research questions that follow from the limitations and would be testable (e.g., measurement of adherence, therapist fidelity, longer follow-up, better waitlist control, inclusion of CBT components or additional active controls).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria: reasoning culminating in the main conclusion correct/complete/coherent, critical reflection relevant and convincingly argued, and strong use of the theory/literature from the Introduction. Improvements needed: anchor every interpretation in the actual reported results (construct-level patterns), explicitly handle unexpected effects (especially waitlist change) with data-supported reasoning, compare expectation-by-expectation to prior studies, and provide at least two concrete, testable future research directions tied to the specific limitations of this study.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria: near-faultless scientific language and structure, minimal APA/grammar/spelling errors, and no vague/ambiguous statements in crucial parts. To improve: maintain consistent terminology (e.g., VRET vs VR program; self-led vs therapist-led; waitlist as stable vs not), reduce ambiguous phrasing in key claims (e.g., dropout/no dropout), ensure tables/figures are fully described rather than placeholders, and improve APA/statistical formatting consistency (and remove formatting artifacts that disrupt readability).

intro_feedback: The paper provides a clear societal relevance (waitlists, costs, impact) and a coherent general idea (VRET vs self-led/therapist-led, variability and safety signals from inhibitory learning). However, the argumentation has important issues: (1) the introduction culminates in a hypothesis but the causal/logic chain is sometimes asserted rather than carefully supported (e.g., “removing safety signals” logic is partly speculative and not operationalized); (2) “RQ/hypothesis” are not explicitly stated as such in a clean, unambiguous sentence; (3) several references are used that do not match the course’s core requirement (Anderson 2013, Kampmann 2016, Bouchard should be 2017 per the guide, but the text references Bouchard 2018; also multiple citations that appear in the intro are not among the required core set). Overall, the storyline is present but not strong enough to reach the “good/excellent” argumentation band.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: line of reasoning culminating in RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis; clarity and relevance of RQ; coherence; and proportion of relevant/up-to-date references. You have a clear societal relevance and a generally coherent theoretical framing leading toward a hypothesis, so you land in the “developing/adequate” transition. To reach higher: (1) state the RQ/hypotheses explicitly and precisely; (2) tighten the argument so safety-signal variability claims are directly tied to what the study manipulates and measures; (3) ensure core required references are used correctly and consistently with the course guide (and avoid introducing mismatched/incorrect years or missing required core trials).
methods_feedback: The methods include many required elements (sampling route, SCID-5-CV screening, inclusion/exclusion criteria, random allocation, sample size flow to 3 arms, LSAS-SR description, intervention duration, session structure, pre/post timing). Still, important replication-critical details are missing or incorrect: (1) participant allocation and final N are inconsistent with the results (you state 86 allocated, 11 dropped, yet results use ~75 and reports 'no dropouts among 75'); (2) the Results mention an outlier but the Methods do not describe outlier handling; (3) the waitlist design is unclear/odd: participants are assigned to two forms after 8 weeks, but the analysis appears to compare pre vs post only—this assignment after 8 weeks is not described as part of the design logic and could create confusion; (4) no therapist details (number of therapists, training adherence, fidelity) are provided; (5) the materials description lacks key procedural/instruction details (e.g., how exposures are selected/titrated, how “tweaked” scenarios are standardized across conditions, adherence monitoring for self-led). Because these are substantial for internal validity and replication, the Methods cannot score “adequate+”.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: study design coherence with RQ/hypotheses; completeness and correctness of participants/materials/procedure. The study design is broadly coherent (screening, randomization into 3 arms, pre/post LSAS-SR). However, the rubric requires methods described comprehensibly and complete/correct. Missing replication-critical details (adherence monitoring, exposure standardization, therapist/fidelity), plus internal inconsistencies (allocation/dropout/N mismatch; ambiguous waitlist procedure) prevent a higher band.
results_feedback: Results are presented in a roughly “structured” way (ANOVA, main effects, interaction, post-hoc comparisons, figure placeholder). However, multiple reporting problems prevent a passing level: (1) statistical degrees of freedom and F values are implausible/incorrect for the stated design (e.g., F(2, 10484.21)); (2) critical descriptive statistics tables (Table 1/2) are incomplete/blank in the provided text, and Figure 1 has no values; (3) the pre/post means and the post-hoc p-values (e.g., p = .002 vs p < .002) are inconsistent/unclear; (4) the dropout statement conflicts with the Methods (Methods mention 11 dropped; Results say none among 75). Also, assumptions are claimed “met” without showing the actual tests/values (and you should report what exactly was violated/checked). There is some alignment with the planned ANOVA (condition × time), but the statistical reporting completeness/accuracy is insufficient.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: data analysis/reporting correctness, completeness, coherence, and relevance to stated design; and demonstrating in-depth knowledge of statistics. Results are structured but do not meet the rubric’s correctness bar: implausible DF/F reporting, missing actual descriptive data/figures, inconsistent dropout statements, and insufficient assumption-check reporting. To score higher: report accurate ANOVA degrees of freedom/values consistent with N and design, include full Table(s) and Figure(s), clearly report outlier handling, and ensure congruence with methods.
discussion_feedback: The discussion identifies a main conclusion consistent with the (intended) results: both VRET conditions outperform waitlist and therapist-led is stronger than self-led, framed within inhibitory learning (variability/safety signals). It also attempts to address an unexpected waitlist increase with limitations. However, the discussion has issues that limit scientific reasoning quality: (1) the “overall conclusion is that VRET has a negative effect” is the opposite of what is argued earlier (negative effect vs negative improvement is unclear wording, and it undermines clarity); (2) several interpretations are speculative without linking back to specific data patterns (especially given that the Results are incomplete/possibly incorrect); (3) critical reflection is present but mostly generic and sometimes statistically weak (e.g., “naturally degraded in symptoms” without considering regression to the mean, measurement error, or waiting effects); (4) future research ideas exist but are not very concrete and not systematically derived from each limitation; (5) theoretical implications are mentioned but not deeply integrated with how the manipulation (safety signals) was tested. This keeps the discussion around the lower-middle band.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: correct and complete reasoning culminating in the main conclusion; critical reflection that is relevant and convincingly argued; responsible reference use; outstanding insight into limitations/future directions for higher bands. The discussion connects back to inhibitory learning and attempts to interpret an unexpected waitlist pattern, and it gives some limitations/future ideas. To reach higher: (1) ensure the main conclusion and wording are unambiguous and consistent with results; (2) interpret only patterns that are clearly supported by the reported statistics; (3) give at least two limitations tied to specific methodological features and propose concrete future studies that directly test the limitations/theoretical claims.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and scientific vocabulary is used appropriately. Paragraphing is mostly present, and citations are included. Still, there are notable issues: (1) many APA/reference consistency problems (e.g., inconsistent year for Bouchard; LSAS-SR scale description errors like “wait an average of upwards of 3 months, ranging up to 6 months” is wordy but okay; in Discussion there is a major clarity error “negative effect”); (2) Results section formatting is incomplete (tables/figure missing content), which also affects clarity; (3) some sentences are long and sometimes unclear (a few vague/ambiguous statements). These issues prevent higher than “adequate/developing” on the language & style criterion.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: clarity of scientific language, structured components/paragraphs, grammar/spelling, and APA/reference accuracy. You are mostly readable and use scientific tone, but there are clarity-breaking wording issues (e.g., “negative effect” in the final conclusion), incomplete/placeholder content in results visuals/tables, and reference/year inconsistencies. Fewer ambiguous/vague statements and higher APA consistency would be needed for higher bands.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides clear societal relevance, defines key constructs (SAD, exposure/inhibitory learning, variability/safety signals, VRET), and culminates in a coherent RQ/hypothesis about therapist-led vs self-led VRET vs waitlist. The line of reasoning is mostly sound and references are generally relevant. Main weaknesses are (a) some imprecisions/overgeneralizations (e.g., causality wording around waitlists and outcomes, and the link from inhibitory learning to the specific “safety signals” manipulation without showing explicit operationalization), (b) reliance on a few older/general claims without tighter alignment to the exact study design/population, and (c) limited explicit statement of the research question/hypotheses in one crisp form at the end of the intro.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric is satisfied at a “Good/Adequate” level: the RQ/hypothesis is present and the line of reasoning culminates in it, but it is not as crisp and explicitly argued as required for the top bands. Societal relevance and key theoretical constructs are included. To reach “Excellent,” the intro should (1) state the research question and hypotheses very explicitly in the final transition sentences, (2) tighten how the theoretical elements (variability and safety signals) translate into your specific operational differences between self-led and therapist-led VRET, and (3) ensure all cited empirical work is directly used as building blocks for the argument (some mentions feel more general than tightly linked to the exact design).
methods_feedback: Methods are broadly coherent with the stated design (screening, allocation to 3 conditions, 8-week VRET with pre/post LSAS-SR). Participants/inclusion/exclusion and session structure are described. However, several required replication-level details are missing or inconsistent: randomization/sequence generation details are thin beyond “randomizer tool”; the drop-out description is internally inconsistent (11 dropped before completion but the results report no dropouts among 75); the final sample is unclear (86 allocated minus dropouts vs later 75/no dropouts); key therapist-led vs self-led differences are under-specified (e.g., exactly what therapist involvement occurs during sessions, and how much standardization there is); waitlist timing is described but not clarified (waitlist getting no intervention vs receiving VRET immediately after post). In Materials, the LSAS-SR description is somewhat correct, but the scoring explanation and item wording are not sufficient for full completeness (no number of items per subscale or an item example is fine, but scoring details are minimal). Overall: mostly correct at a high level, but not complete/certain enough for passing a next-year bachelor thesis.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s “Adequate” band is nearly met: the design is coherent and described, participants and inclusion/exclusion are mostly given, and interventions/procedure timing are broadly clear. However, it does not reach “Good/Excellent” because several important details are missing or inconsistent: the final N/sample size is unclear due to inconsistent dropout reporting; therapist-led vs self-led procedures lack enough operationalization to replicate (exact therapist behaviors, standardization, monitoring); randomization and allocation concealment details are minimal; and key outcomes of the design (waitlist receiving intervention immediately after post, and session structure consistency) are not fully specified. These prevent “complete and correct” methods.
results_feedback: Results reporting is insufficient relative to the paper’s own design and the course expectations. There is no Table 2 or the means/SDs actually filled for pre/post by condition (Table 1 is mostly blank; Table 2 is referenced but not provided in the text). The key ANOVA degrees of freedom values appear implausible/mistyped (e.g., F(2, 10484.21) and F(1, 4139.63)), suggesting reporting or calculation/format issues; assumption checks are claimed “met” but no test statistics/figures are provided. The mixed ANOVA and post-hoc p-values are also presented without the multiple-comparison correction approach (despite multiple pairwise comparisons). Figure 1 is blank. Additionally, the narrative contradicts the Methods (no dropouts “to report” in Results vs earlier drop-out statement). This places the paper around “at least 50% correct, nearly complete” rather than “mostly correct/complete.”
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric is not met beyond the lowest band because reporting is incomplete/insufficiently correct for the stated analysis: crucial tables/figures are missing or blank, ANOVA reporting includes likely errors in df values, assumption checks are claimed without evidence, and the post-hoc comparison approach/corrections are not clearly specified. The results do not sufficiently demonstrate in-depth knowledge of statistical reasoning/accurate reporting as required even for the middle bands.
discussion_feedback: Discussion links results to the inhibitory learning model (variability and safety signals) and to prior VRET studies, and it addresses an unexpected pattern in the waitlist condition. However, the argument quality and critical reflection are uneven: the main conclusion is overstated (“VRET has a negative effect” is an incorrect phrasing; it is presumably intended as a positive/beneficial effect on symptoms). The waitlist explanation is speculative and not grounded in measured covariates (e.g., “naturally degraded”/negative life events) without proposing how future work could test this concretely beyond “larger sample size.” Limitations include some relevant points (single therapist, exclusion of CBT components), but it does not clearly connect them to specific results/possible mechanisms, and it misses at least two study-specific improvements/future directions that are tightly derived from the study’s design (e.g., fidelity/monitoring, adherence in self-led VR, expectancy effects, blinding, long-term follow-up). Despite that, it is reasonably coherent and uses responsible references.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric is met at about the “Developing/Adequate” level: the main conclusion is mostly correct in direction and is connected to theory/prior research, and there is critical reflection on an unexpected waitlist finding. However, it falls short of higher bands because the critical reflection is not strongly tied to concrete limitations/alternative explanations that are testable, and the discussion includes at least one major correctness/wording issue about the direction of effects. It also needs clearer, study-specific future recommendations derived from measured/operational constraints (e.g., adherence/fidelity/expectancy, attrition handling).
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and scientific, but there are frequent issues affecting quality: awkward/incorrect phrasing (“negative effect on social anxiety symptoms”), occasional confusing wording (e.g., “negative effect” vs “decrease symptoms”), and some informal/unclear statements. Structure is mostly present by paragraphs, but topical focus/single-argument clarity is sometimes weakened by long sentences and digressions. APA/statistical formatting is poor in Results (implausible/unclear F dfs, missing tables/figures content) and in references some items look inconsistent (e.g., “Bouchard et al., 2018” mentioned in intro/discussion but reference list shows 2017; “Wolitzky-Taylor, K., & LeBeau, R. (2023).” is present but not clearly used; minor APA inconsistencies like “DSM-V” vs “DSM-5” appear). Overall this prevents a “good” band.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric is limited to around “Developing/Adequate”: overall structure and readability are present, but there are multiple vague/incorrect statements and substantial problems in statistical/APA presentation. To reach the next band, the paper needs (1) near-faultless correctness in key interpretation language (e.g., direction of effects), (2) consistent and correct statistical reporting (figures/tables filled, plausible df, appropriate formatting), and (3) fewer APA inconsistencies (matching citation years/author lists, consistent DSM numbering, clearer reference-to-text alignment).

intro_feedback: The societal relevance and the general rationale are present early, and the paper develops a plausible theoretical motivation (inhibitory learning/variability and safety signals) that culminates in clear expectations about comparing self-led vs therapist-led VRET and both vs waitlist. However, the line of reasoning is sometimes under-justified or internally imprecise (e.g., some claims about “in vivo” vs VR variability are asserted without clearly linking to the exact constructs/operationalisations used). The research gap is present, but the exact RQ/hypotheses are not crisply formulated as a testable statement tied to the design (e.g., no explicit hypothesis language like direction/magnitude for condition×time). Prior research is used, but several references listed do not align with the course requirement for the prior-research core articles (and some years/IDs differ from what the guide specifies). Overall: competent and mostly coherent narrative, but not consistently argumentatively “tight” and not fully aligned with required course structure.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric criteria emphasize a clear, coherent line of reasoning culminating in a correct RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis, with up-to-date and relevant empirical/theoretical work. This paper meets the societal relevance and uses an interesting theory, but does not make the RQ/hypotheses sufficiently explicit and testable in a way that clearly “culminates” the argument. The literature is used but some references appear mismatched to the required core set/recency, and several argumentation links are asserted rather than carefully derived from constructs and design operationalisations. For higher scoring, the introduction would need (a) explicit RQ and hypothesis sentences in the final part, (b) tighter PEEL-like logic transitions between studies and theory, and (c) stronger alignment between how the theory constructs map onto the actual VR/self-led/therapist-led manipulations.
methods_feedback: Design logic is coherent (screening, random assignment to three conditions, pre/post, same measurement timing; 8 weekly sessions; therapist-led vs self-led distinction). Participants are described with inclusion/exclusion criteria and basic recruitment. Materials describe the primary measure (LSAS-SR) and basic VR features. Procedure gives an understandable timeline at a high level. However, several important replication-level details are missing or unclear: key intervention content/structure (exact exposure hierarchy, session specifics, how variability is implemented), therapist involvement details (frequency, interaction content, therapist fidelity/competence), what differs between conditions beyond leadership, and how the waitlist “wait” period was handled ethically (e.g., did waitlist receive any minimal support/contact, and how long after baseline were they measured again). Also, the analysis plan is not reported in Results (and consequentially Method doesn’t specify the statistical approach), and there are inconsistencies between narrative and the Results section (dropouts and final N). These issues keep it from a “complete and correct” Methods rating, though it is clearly not incoherent.
methods_rubric_feedback: Method rubric criteria require a coherent design related to the RQ/hypotheses, and participants/materials/procedure described comprehensibly with key replication details. This draft has a coherent high-level design and inclusion/exclusion criteria, but lacks multiple important details that are needed to understand exactly what participants did (the exposure/variability implementation, session content, therapist role specifics, ethical/operational handling of waitlist, and condition equivalence beyond leadership). Also, cross-section consistency (Methods vs Results sample size/dropouts) is essential; inconsistencies lower the Methods rating because the study cannot be assessed or replicated reliably.
results_feedback: The Results section is not sufficiently correct/complete relative to the stated design and the reporting expectations. Major issues: (1) The first sentences contradict the Methods (it says no dropouts and then later describes outliers, but Methods reports 11 dropouts and also lists 75 final participants in a table). (2) The ANOVA degrees of freedom and F-statistics appear nonsensical for the sample size and design (e.g., F(2, 10484.21)), suggesting a reporting or calculation error. (3) The assumption checks are claimed as “met” but the actual tests/values are not provided (and a Table 1 is essentially empty). (4) The post-hoc p-values are not consistent with the overall pattern described (and are given without correction details). (5) There is no actual Table 2 content in the text, no numeric means for pre and post across conditions/time, and Figure 1 is not actually presented. Because of these problems, the analysis/reporting is far below the required “mostly correct/complete” level for PT:PR.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric criteria require correct and (nearly) complete reporting of data analysis in terms of the stated design/procedure, including appropriate statistics, assumption checks, and presentation of descriptive outcomes (tables/figures) tied to the hypotheses. This submission does not meet those requirements: key tables are missing/empty, figures are not provided, and the inferential statistics (df/F values) appear inconsistent with the sample and design. Therefore it falls into the band where reporting is incorrect/incomplete and not reliably interpretable in terms of the study design.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion addresses the main conclusion (VRET conditions better than waitlist; therapist-led better than self-led) and links to the inhibitory learning model/safety signals. It also attempts to handle an unexpected waitlist increase with plausible limitations and offers some future recommendations (larger samples, more therapists, broaden active controls). However, the interpretation is weakened by the earlier incorrect/incomplete Results: the Discussion treats the waitlist outcome and the magnitude/patterns as certain, but the Results reporting is not reliable enough to support those claims. Additionally, critical reflection remains somewhat generic in places (e.g., therapist personal life event) and lacks at least two concrete, study-specific limitations tied explicitly to measurable aspects (e.g., measurement timing, expectancy effects, therapist non-blinding, attrition handling—even though the paper reports conflicting dropout information). Future directions are present, but not as specific design-level proposals (e.g., what exact control condition, longer follow-up, or how to test safety-signal mechanism). Overall: reasonable attempt at theorizing and critique, but not convincingly grounded due to earlier reporting weaknesses.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric criteria emphasize correct/complete reasoning leading to the main conclusion, responsible use of references, relevant critical reflection, and (ideally) at least two concrete study-specific limitations plus future directions. This paper provides a plausible theoretical interpretation and attempts at critical reflection with future ideas. However, the discussion is not sufficiently convincing because it relies on results that are not presented reliably, and some limitations are speculative/generic rather than tightly grounded in the study’s actual design, measurement, and analysis choices.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language/style rubric criteria reward near faultless scientific wording, clear paragraph structure, low redundancy, and accurate APA/statistical formatting with few vague/ambiguous statements. Here, readability is mostly fine, but there are serious formatting and content completeness issues in Results (tables/figures missing), several imprecise statements, and reference/APA coherence problems.
