These keep the score below “good/excellent” despite generally understandable prose.

intro_feedback: You provide clear societal relevance, then a generally coherent theoretical justification (inhibitory learning, variability, safety signals) leading to a concrete RQ/hypothesis about comparing self-led vs therapist-led VRET vs waitlist. However, the argumentation has gaps: the transition from “inhibitory learning + variability” to the specific safety-signal reasoning is plausible but not tightly integrated with the earlier variability claim, and several claims are not well constrained to your design/population. The prior-research section is somewhat descriptive and sometimes weak in tying each study’s design/results directly to your specific comparison and to what is novel here. References are mostly relevant, but there are inconsistencies (e.g., an “Anderson et al., 2013; Kampmann et al., 2016; Bouchard et al., 2018” reference pattern vs a 2017 Bouchard paper is listed) and some citations look outdated or unmatched to exact claims.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis; references should be relevant/up-to-date; argumentation should be mostly convincing. Strengths: societal relevance early; theoretical framework is introduced and an explicit expectation/hypothesis is stated. What limits the score: the argumentation becomes more narrative than argumentative in places; the link between earlier claims (variability) and the later safety-signal mechanism is not fully integrated; novelty/gap and why your design is the needed test are sometimes only implied. Reference relevance/up-to-dateness is mostly good but weakened by citation/year mismatches and occasional loose justification of claims.
methods_feedback: The method includes many required elements (setting, recruitment, inclusion/exclusion, allocation to three conditions, session structure, and an identified primary outcome LSAS-SR). But key replication-critical details are missing or unclear: you never specify the exact cut-off score definition for “social anxiety symptoms” beyond the number, you don’t report demographics/statistics in a structured way in Methods, you don’t describe therapist qualifications/standardization of VRET protocol sufficiently (what is standardized across therapist vs self-led beyond “overseen”), and the randomization process for waitlist-to-VRET after 8 weeks is described but not operationalized (is there crossover, and how is it prevented from contaminating post-measures?). In Results you also claim 75 participants with “no dropouts,” which conflicts with Methods where 11 drop out before completion and with the final stated N. These inconsistencies lower the Methods score substantially.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: study design coherence with RQ/hypotheses, and comprehensibility/completeness of participants/materials/procedure. Strengths: inclusion/exclusion criteria are listed; allocation to three conditions is described; session duration and broad protocol differences are provided; outcome measure is identified. What limits the score: several replication-critical details are missing (e.g., exact randomization procedure details, therapist-led protocol standardization, what exactly differs in self-led vs therapist-led beyond therapist oversight, crossover handling and whether waitlist is truly control at post), and there is an inconsistency between Methods and Results regarding N and dropouts. These issues prevent “complete and correct” methods.
results_feedback: Results reporting is not aligned with the stated design and sample: you report “no dropouts… among the 75 participants” but Method states 86 allocated and 11 dropped out (final 75). Later you present ANOVA degrees of freedom numbers that do not match a plausible N (e.g., F(2, 10484.21)), and the assumption checks are claimed “met” without showing the tests/values. Table 1 is empty and Table 2/Figure 1 are not properly presented (and Figure 1 contains no data). Also, you only report pre vs post, yet state a mixed ANOVA without providing the correct structure/output. Post-hoc p-values are reported without stating correction method (despite mentioning none). Overall, the data analysis/reporting is largely incorrect/incomplete, even if the general narrative is interpretable.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: data analysis and reporting correctness, completeness, and relevance to stated design; demonstrate statistical reasoning. Strengths: attempts to report ANOVA structure, main/interaction effects, and post-hoc comparisons. What limits the score: reporting is incomplete (empty Table 1, missing numeric Table 2 values in text, Figure 1 not interpretable), assumption checks are asserted without evidence, ANOVA statistics/df values appear implausible given sample size, and the narrative conflicts with earlier described sample/dropout counts. Post-hoc procedures/corrections are not clearly specified.
discussion_feedback: The discussion has an understandable main conclusion and it connects to the inhibitory learning model/safety signals, and you appropriately address one striking pattern (waitlist symptom increase) with possible explanations and some limitation discussion. You also link results back to prior VR VRET findings at a broad level. However, the reasoning is partly undermined by the weaknesses in Results (since key numbers/figures are missing or inconsistent, several interpretations become less grounded). Critical reflection is present but often generic and not always tightly tied to your actual manipulation/measurement (e.g., waitlist explanation is speculative and could be better connected to timing, regression to the mean, measurement/recontact effects, or regression). Future directions are provided, but they could be more concrete and explicitly derived from specific limitations in your setup (protocol fidelity, therapist effects, crossover contamination, baseline equivalence, and improved control conditions).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: reasoning culminating in the main conclusion should be correct/complete/coherent; critical reflection should be relevant and convincingly argued; references used responsibly; demonstrate insight into limitations/future directions. Strengths: clear attempt to interpret findings on construct level; explicitly discusses an unexpected waitlist pattern; connects back to inhibitory learning model and mentions prior VR studies. What limits the score: interpretations rely on under-specified/possibly incorrect results reporting; limitations are discussed but sometimes generic/speculative rather than tightly tied to design/measurement; future directions are present but not always sufficiently specific to what was actually done.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is acceptable and scientific tone is used. Paragraphing and topic structure are mostly understandable (especially in Discussion), but there are several clarity/accuracy issues: some sentences are overly long, and there are a few awkward/unclear phrases (e.g., “negative effect… have a negative effect” style repetition; safety-signal explanation could be written more precisely). APA issues exist: some citations in-text vary in year/paper matching (e.g., Bouchard 2018 vs listed 2017; Anderson/Kampmann/Bouchard year mismatches), and there are inconsistent capitalization/hyphenation in terms (e.g., VRET vs V R E T), plus incomplete/empty tables/figures hurt clarity. Style is not severely broken, but it is not near-faultless.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: near-faultless scientific language and structure; minimal APA/grammar/spelling issues; few vague/ambiguous statements; no redundancy at paragraph/sentence level. Strengths: generally understandable scientific writing and mostly coherent paragraphs. What limits the score: incomplete/placeholder tables/figures reduce clarity; some citation/year mismatches and formatting inconsistencies; some sentences are repetitive or slightly unclear; overall not “near faultless” and not consistently APA-accurate.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a clear attempt at societal relevance and provides a line toward an RQ/hypotheses (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist). However, the scientific reasoning is not sufficiently coherent: key constructs (self-efficacy/interaction theory) are introduced but not clearly and consistently linked to specific predictions beyond general expectations; several claims are asserted without proper integration with the cited evidence. The previous research section is more like a sequence of summaries than an argument built toward the exact research gap, and some referenced studies in the text (e.g., Anderson et al. 2016; Kampmann et al. 2016; Lindner et al. 2018) are not consistently aligned with the required core-articles logic (and there are citation-year/author inconsistencies: e.g., Anderson et al. 2016 vs required Anderson et al. 2013). Definitions are present for some concepts, but important operational/construct definitions (e.g., what exactly distinguishes SVRET and TVRET in your design, what “self-led” entails as an independent variable, and how SAD diagnosis/threshold maps onto the inclusion criteria) are insufficiently specified. Overall: RQ relevance is present, but the hypothesis substantiation via theory + prior experiments is incomplete/inconsistent.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: line of reasoning culminating in RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis; coherence and convincing argumentation; responsible references. Improvements needed to reach a higher band: (1) make the RQ explicit early and ensure the hypotheses follow transparently from the theoretical framework and prior experimental evidence; (2) build a clearer ‘story’ that uses the cited studies as building blocks (what they show + how they lead to your exact design gap), instead of largely summarizing each study; (3) ensure all referenced studies match the required design/population/variables logic and resolve citation inconsistencies; (4) connect self-efficacy and interaction theory to *specific* predicted patterns (not just general expectations) and acknowledge what your study can/cannot test.
methods_feedback: The Methods section provides the basic elements (participants, materials, procedure) and includes some key details (n=99, age range, inclusion/exclusion criteria, 10-week intervention, LSAS measure). But replication-level completeness is weak and several crucial details are missing or unclear. Random allocation is claimed but the allocation procedure/stratification is not described. Inclusion criteria are internally inconsistent/misaligned: participants are said to meet criteria for the social phobia scale, yet the main outcomes use LSAS; the “comorbidities” exclusion is vague (which ones, how assessed). “Any comorbidities” is too broad to justify without clarification. Procedures mix procedural language with materials; also the between-condition manipulation is not described precisely enough (e.g., what therapist “on call” means for SVRET vs TVRET, how much tailoring occurs in TVRET, whether both have the same number/duration of VR sessions, how reminders work). The analytic plan is not reported in a methods-appropriate way (a factorial mixed ANOVA is stated in Results only; no reporting of assumptions checks beyond normality/homoscedasticity; no mention of sphericity, covariates, handling of outliers beyond ‘boxplots’; no alpha correction details). These issues keep the Methods in the Developing band.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: study design coherence with stated RQ/hypotheses; completeness and correctness of participants/materials/procedures. To move up: (1) specify allocation method and any stratification/randomization details; (2) clarify operationalization of SVRET vs TVRET (exact differences in therapist involvement, timing, tailoring, adherence support); (3) make inclusion/exclusion criteria precise and justified (what comorbidities, how assessed, how language sufficiency determined, how ‘social phobia scale criteria’ relates to diagnosis); (4) ensure materials descriptions include relevant standardized details (session duration, number of VR exposures, exact procedure of scenario reactions) and remove ambiguous wording; (5) include the analysis plan in methods (primary DV, model, correction approach), not only in Results.
results_feedback: Results report the overall ANOVA logic (time within-subject; condition between-subject) and provide some effect statements and post-hoc p-values. However, several required reporting aspects are missing or inconsistent relative to the sample-results expectations: no descriptive table with means/SDs is actually provided (Table 1/2 are referenced but not shown with values), the statistics use inconsistent df (F(1, 99) for a mixed design) and there is no reporting of the omnibus ANOVA for the mixed structure consistent with df expectations; “Boxplot revealed two outliers” is stated but no analysis of how they were treated (e.g., sensitivity analysis) is given. Post-hoc comparisons are partially reported but the correction approach (“Holm-corrected”) is not clearly matched to the set of tests. The most striking discrepancy is that normality/homogeneity are claimed to be tested with q-q plot/Levene but the actual test outcomes are not reported. The results are somewhat coherent, but statistically and reporting-wise they are not complete/correct enough for a higher grade.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: data analysis/reporting correctness, completeness, coherence, and relevance to stated design. To move up: (1) provide complete descriptive statistics (means/SDs by condition and time) in tables; (2) ensure statistical model reporting is correct for a mixed design (appropriate df structure) and consistently reported; (3) clearly report all relevant tests (omnibus effects and interaction) with correct df and effect sizes where expected; (4) report assumption checks with results (test statistics/p-values) rather than only describing that they were met; (5) ensure post-hoc comparisons and multiplicity correction are transparent and match the claimed correction.
discussion_feedback: The discussion reiterates the main conclusion and attempts to relate findings to prior research and to theory (self-efficacy vs interaction theory). Yet the theoretical reasoning is not executed convincingly: the claim that results specifically confirm Bandura’s self-efficacy theory and disconfirm Vygotsky/interaction theory is not substantiated with direct measures of self-efficacy/interaction processes (manipulation checks are only suggested for future research). Alternative explanations are mentioned (motivation) but are not integrated into a clear argument addressing why motivation/self-efficacy would or would not explain the pattern, and the logic remains somewhat speculative. Critical reflection is present (age range, lack of follow-up, measurement only pre/post, untested theories, and fear of negative evaluation suggestion), but the limitations are not always tied back to how they could change interpretation of the SVRET>TVRET effect. Some references are used in ways that are not clearly connected to the current design (e.g., technology access claims, “no fading” implications). Also, the discussion does not adequately compare effect sizes/magnitude or address whether the post-test superiority of SVRET over TVRET could be due to differences in adherence, contact time, measurement timing, or tailoring—key issues that would be expected at this stage. Overall: discussion is relevant, but argumentation quality for theory-testing and critical interpretation is insufficient.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: reasoning culminating in the main conclusion and quality/relevance of critical reflection. To move up: (1) connect each key finding to theoretical constructs *in a way the data can actually support* (avoid claiming direct theory tests without manipulation checks/measures); (2) compare more explicitly with the studies summarized in the introduction using the same constructs/operationalizations; (3) interpret unexpected findings with plausible, theory-grounded mechanisms and discuss alternative explanations that follow from design differences; (4) limitations should be specific and followed by concrete implications for interpretation and future research design; (5) broaden implications should follow from the evidence strength and acknowledge uncertainty.
lang_style_feedback: Writing is generally understandable, but there are many grammar/wording issues (e.g., 'people their', 'telephoning in public' wording, 'TVRET into in vivo', inconsistent capitalization like 'TVRET condition into in vivo exposure'). Scientific style is sometimes undermined by colloquial phrasing and imprecise terms ('interaction-based therapy', 'z.d.'), and APA/statistical notation has noticeable inconsistencies. APA reference list formatting is mostly present, but in-text citations have inconsistencies and some sources appear to be used inconsistently with the rubric’s expectations (and one source appears possibly incorrect/inconsistent with required core articles). Paragraph structure is mostly present but single-focus/topical sentences are not consistently achieved.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: scientific language correctness, structure (single focus/topical sentences), minimal errors in grammar/spelling and APA/style, and low ambiguity. To move up: (1) improve grammar and phrasing consistency ('people their' -> 'people’s'; correct tense and article use); (2) ensure proper statistical and methodological language; (3) reduce ambiguous phrases and fix inconsistent citation strings and terminology (SVRET/TVRET, in-vivo/VR, 'fear of negative evaluation' distinction); (4) improve APA/statistical formatting consistency and proofread for capitalization/typos. Paragraphing should more consistently reflect single argumentative steps.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a clear topic, and it generally builds from societal/clinical relevance to the rationale for exposure/VRET, self-led vs therapist-led delivery, and links to theories (self-efficacy/interaction). However, the theoretical storyline is not consistently developed into a logically tight set of construct-level expectations: the interaction theory is introduced but not fully integrated into the hypothesis/interpretation, and there are some imprecise causal claims. References are mostly used responsibly, but several citations are to non-core/website sources for key claims, and the literature synthesis does not always explicitly lead to the specific research gap and design choice (SVRET vs TVRET without mixed in-vivo phases). The RQ/hypotheses are present and relevant, but argumentation quality is uneven and sometimes too descriptive.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects a clear line of reasoning culminating in an RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, with theory used as an argument engine (not only introduced). Here, the RQ/hypotheses are present and relevant, but the theoretical substantiation is partly inconsistent: the interaction theory is mentioned yet not tightly tied to specific measurable expectations and the surprising SVRET>TVRET result is not clearly anticipated from the theory. The synthesis of prior research sometimes remains descriptive instead of explicitly building toward the design choice (SVRET vs TVRET without in-vivo crossover). References are mostly relevant and responsibly used, but overreliance on non-peer-reviewed/website-style sources for key claims weakens “given existing empirical and theoretical work” strength.
methods_feedback: Design is coherent with the stated aim (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist; pre/post; LSAS as outcome). Participants are described with basic inclusion/exclusion and demographics, and the VR intervention is described in enough detail to understand the general manipulation. However, important method details are missing or unclear for replication and for scientific reasoning: random allocation process is not described (e.g., how randomization was implemented), the comorbidity exclusion is underspecified, and the procedure lacks key elements (e.g., whether assessments were administered identically across groups, whether blinding/expectation control was attempted, adherence/compliance and what counted as “completed sessions,” therapist qualifications and standardization, whether waitlist received any minimal contact). The analysis description is incomplete (e.g., mixed ANOVA structure, assumption checks are mentioned but not correctly aligned with the reported model; outliers handling is vague). The statistical reasoning is therefore not fully “complete and correct.”
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric requires a study design that is coherent and clearly related to stated RQ/hypotheses, and methods that are complete and correct: participants (including allocation), materials/intervention (precise differences), and procedure (chronological replication details) plus appropriate analysis reporting. While the core manipulation and outcome measure are described, key replication-relevant details are missing or underspecified (randomization procedure, therapist standardization, adherence/compliance definition, expectation control, assessment conditions, outlier handling rules). Analysis description is also incomplete/inconsistent with the reported mixed-model structure, preventing “complete and correct.”
results_feedback: Results reporting is partly aligned with the sample template (main ANOVA effects and interaction are reported, with post-hoc comparisons and a figure described). Yet several issues limit adequacy: the ANOVA degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with the sample and described design (e.g., using F(1, 99) for both condition and interaction without clearly matching the two/three-level condition and the pre/post within-subject factor); effect direction/magnitude is not quantified (no partial eta-squared/CIs), making the interpretation less grounded. Table/figure content is incomplete (the paper references Table 1/2, but only later demographic content appears; Figure 1 is not substantiated with actual values). Assumption checks are mentioned but not consistently reported with the reported analysis, and outlier handling is not specified (only “Boxplot revealed two outliers” and “all data used”). Overall: mostly understandable direction, but reporting is not complete or fully correct for the stated model.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects data analysis and reporting to be correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to the stated design/procedure. The report includes the main inferential pieces (main effects and interaction) and post-hoc comparisons, which is good. However, the statistical model/df values appear inconsistent with the described 3-condition mixed design (with a within-subject pre/post factor), and effect sizes/precision are missing. Table/figure reporting is incomplete (no means/SDs shown for outcomes in the provided text) and outlier handling is not specified beyond “all data used.” This keeps the results from reaching “Good/Excellent.”
discussion_feedback: Discussion reaches the main conclusion (both exposure conditions reduced LSAS vs waitlist; SVRET outperformed TVRET). It compares with prior work and attempts theoretical interpretation (self-efficacy favored over interaction theory), and it provides several study-specific limitations and concrete future research suggestions (elderly sample, follow-up timepoints, measuring mechanisms, testing FNE). However, the discussion has several scientific-reasoning weaknesses: it asserts causal mechanism (self-efficacy) without direct manipulation checks and sometimes treats theory confirmation as more certain than the data allow; it does not critically reconcile the surprising SVRET>TVRET result with alternative explanations (e.g., differential engagement, therapist contact, adherence, expectancy effects) beyond a single motivation vs self-efficacy contrast. The limitations are present but vary in specificity and completeness, and some links to prior research are asserted more than rigorously argued (e.g., “not in line because in vivo changed” without providing a tight argument). Still, the overall reasoning culminates correctly in a coherent set of implications and future directions.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects the reasoning culminating in the main conclusion to be correct and the critical reflection to be relevant and convincingly argued. This discussion correctly interprets the main pattern and provides multiple study-specific limitations and future directions. It falls short of the top band because mechanism claims are not convincingly constrained by what the study measured (no direct self-efficacy/interaction manipulation checks), and alternative explanations for SVRET>TVRET are not fully explored. Some comparison to prior studies could be more explicitly argued in relation to design differences (e.g., in vivo crossover).
lang_style_feedback: Scientific language is generally understandable, but there are noticeable grammar/spelling issues (e.g., word order, agreement, punctuation) and several vague or awkward phrases (e.g., “zone of proximal development… This is known as the interaction theory,” “TVRET condition this helped the therapist choose…”). APA referencing has inconsistencies (e.g., author/year/year formatting; some citations in the text differ from the reference list; Bandura (1997) in references but Bandura (1979) in text). Paragraph structure is mostly present by section, but within paragraphs there is sometimes multiple ideas at once, and some sentences are discontinuous/unclear. Overall: below “good” organization and near “developing/adequate” clarity.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric rewards near faultless scientific language, clear paragraph/sentence structure with single topical focus, and hardly any APA/style errors. This paper is generally readable, but there are frequent grammar/spelling and phrasing errors, and APA referencing/in-text consistency issues. Some paragraphs contain multiple ideas, reducing single-focus structure, and there are vague/awkward statements in critical places (especially around intervention/adherence and theoretical claims).

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a generally understandable topic and attempts to build toward an RQ/hypothesis comparing SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist. However, the scientific relevance is sometimes stated generally (e.g., “threshold for seeking help”) without tight linkage to the exact study gap and population/design. The theoretical framework (e.g., interaction theory, self-efficacy, exposure principles) is introduced but not consistently developed into a clear, logically used set of constructs/expected mechanisms for the specific design comparison. The prior research section summarizes multiple studies but the line of reasoning is not consistently organized as building blocks that culminate in the specific RQ; transitions between studies and why each one is essential are often implicit. References are mostly used responsibly, but some references are less appropriate/unclear for the claims they are used to support (e.g., non-core sources for core constructs; some citation/year mismatches). Overall, the RQ/hypothesis is present, but the argumentation culminating in it is only somewhat coherent and not very convincing at this level.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasize: (1) correct, coherent argumentation culminating in a clear RQ/hypothesis; (2) explicit and convincing scientific relevance grounded in existing empirical/theoretical work; (3) responsible reference use. For higher scores, require a more explicit “hourglass” line: narrow the societal problem to a precise research gap tied to the exact population and design, then justify the specific comparison (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist) with a mechanism-based theoretical framework that is later reused. Ensure prior research is used as building blocks: each study should be linked to what it adds/does not answer for the student’s RQ, and transitions should make the logical leap explicit (rather than summarizing aims/results).
methods_feedback: The method section has the core elements (participants, materials, procedure, basic analysis description) and provides some intervention and measurement details. Yet several necessary replication-relevant details are missing or unclear: random allocation procedure is described only broadly; the inclusion/exclusion criteria are incomplete (e.g., “comorbidities” is vague; no diagnostic instrument/criteria beyond LSAS/SPS mention); group sizes are given but demographics in the text are inconsistent with the later table (female/male split). In the materials section, several constructs are conflated or imprecisely described (e.g., VR headset and scenario set-up are described, but fidelity/standardization between SVRET/TVRET is not sufficiently specified; “anxiety situations used before by Kan et al.” is not enough to operationalize the IV). Questionnaire scoring description is mostly present but not fully consistent with the actual LSAS scoring structure (and key scoring details like exact scoring ranges/interpretation are stated in a way that raises concerns). The analysis/reporting in results is partially handled in Results rather than Methods; planned contrasts/corrections are not described in Methods. Because participants/materials/procedure cannot be reconstructed confidently from the description, the methods quality is below the “complete and correct” level.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasize: study design clearly coherent and related to RQ/hypotheses; participants/materials/procedure described comprehensibly; methods complete and correct for replication at this student level. For higher scores, the Methods should include: clear operationalization of the independent variable(s) (exact differences between SVRET and TVRET, standardization, therapist role/time/feedback boundaries, session structure, and VR content delivery); explicit participant inclusion/exclusion criteria with diagnostic basis; allocation/randomization procedure details; complete procedure timeline including assessment moments (pre/post at specific times) and what happens to the waitlist; and analysis plan details either in Methods or with clear linkage (e.g., ANOVA model, factors, contrasts, corrections).
results_feedback: Results report an appropriate overall analysis idea (2×2 mixed ANOVA-like logic: condition × time) and includes assumption checks (normality via Q-Q, homogeneity via Levene), an omnibus pattern (main effects and interaction), and some post-hoc comparisons aligned to the stated hypothesis. However, the reporting is incomplete/unclear in a few places: the degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with N=99 (and the ANOVA is described as F(1,99) rather than with the correct df structure). The assumption statement is vague (Q-Q plot/homogeneity “met” without test statistics). The post-hoc p-values are inconsistent in formatting and contain at least one apparent typo (e.g., p = <.001). The results also lacks the descriptive statistics in-text (the promised Table 2 content is absent from the submission text shown) and effect sizes are not reported, which limits interpretability. Still, the main pattern (improvement in both exposure conditions vs waitlist; SVRET > TVRET at post-test) is presented clearly enough to reach an adequate-to-good level for this course.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasize correctness/appropriateness/completeness of data analysis and reporting, and relevance to the stated design/procedure. For higher scores: report accurate ANOVA df structure matching N and model; include the promised descriptive statistics table(s) and ensure consistency between text and tables; present assumption check statistics if stated; use correct p-value formatting; report effect sizes (at least partial eta-squared) and degrees-of-freedom correctly; and ensure post-hoc tests are described with which comparisons and correction method, matching what was stated in Methods/analysis plan.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states a main conclusion and interprets the findings in relation to the hypothesis. It appropriately addresses that SVRET outperformed TVRET, and it compares to prior work (especially Lindner et al. on the online-to-in-vivo transition confound). It also provides a theoretical interpretation using self-efficacy vs interaction theory. However, the theoretical reasoning is not executed in a fully convincing way: the interaction theory is invoked, but the discussion does not demonstrate specific tested predictions about interaction mechanisms, nor does it clearly reconcile why the interaction-theory expectation would be weaker in this specific implementation. Critical reflection is present (age range, lack of follow-up, only LSAS without FNE), but limitations remain somewhat general and sometimes speculative (e.g., relapse mechanism and “fadeout” tied to social-emotional/cognitive processes without direct support in the study design). The future research suggestions are generally relevant, yet the discussion does not clearly separate what is warranted by the data vs by assumptions. Overall, reasoning is somewhat coherent but not very convincing, and critical engagement is not at the “relevant and convincingly argued” level.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasize: correct main conclusion reasoning; critical reflection that is relevant and convincingly argued; and responsible reference use. For higher scores: (1) connect each major finding to specific expectations and previous studies one-by-one; (2) interpret results at construct/theory level, showing whether theory elements are supported or need adjustment; (3) provide at least two study-specific limitations tied to internal/external validity and show why they might change the interpretation; (4) propose concrete future directions that logically follow from limitations/results, not from broad speculation; and (5) distinguish expected vs unexpected findings clearly.
lang_style_feedback: Language and scientific style are generally understandable, but there are recurring issues: grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (“people their personal relationships,” “TVRET to treat SAD,” “The theory stated that…”). There is inconsistent terminology (SVRET/TVRET vs VRET; sometimes “TVRET into in vivo” wording; “TVRET condition every week…”). APA/style is weak-to-inconsistent: some reference formatting issues and missing/incorrect citation details (e.g., APA year/citation style inconsistency; some website citations used as key evidence). Statistical reporting has formatting problems (p-value typos like “p=<.001”; unclear df reporting). Paragraphing is mostly present but sometimes multiple ideas are packed into single paragraphs, reducing the single-focus structure. Hence this is mid-range rather than adequate-to-good.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasize near faultless scientific language, clear structure (single focus/topical sentences), and minimal grammar/APA errors. For higher scores: reduce grammar issues and awkward phrasing, ensure consistent terminology and abbreviations (SVRET/TVRET/VRET), correct statistical formatting (p-values, df, test names), ensure APA-consistent in-text citations and reference formatting, and maintain clear paragraph-level topical sentences that align with the argumentation (no paragraph containing multiple disconnected subclaims).

intro_feedback: The introduction provides some societal relevance and an overall rationale for comparing SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist. However, the theoretical constructs (e.g., self-efficacy/interaction theory) are not integrated into a clear, coherent argument that culminates in a well-justified, unambiguous RQ/hypotheses; transitions rely on assertions rather than a logically connected build-up. Prior research is summarized in ways that sometimes go beyond what the paper needs for the specific hypotheses, and some article-to-article links are weak or somewhat speculative. References are used but include issues of citation precision and some unclear mapping between claims and the listed sources.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric emphasizes: (1) a correct line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ and justified hypothesis, (2) clear societal relevance and relevance of existing empirical/theoretical work, and (3) responsible use of references. In this rubric, introduce stronger guidance on: ensuring the RQ/hypotheses are explicitly stated near the end of the introduction (not only implicitly), requiring construct-level “why this study” transitions, and penalizing speculative causal claims that are not grounded in the cited literature. Consider adding a checklist item about whether each theory construct reappears later in a testable expectation.
methods_feedback: The methods section includes key elements (participants, materials/intervention, procedure, and a basic description of the analysis approach), and the design is broadly coherent with the stated aim (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist; pre/post). Nonetheless, several required details are missing or insufficiently specified for replication and for evaluating internal validity: recruitment/randomization procedures are described loosely ("randomly allocated" without method), comorbidity/exclusion criteria are vague, intervention standardization and therapist involvement (time, content, adherence/fidelity) are not operationalized clearly, and the outcome measurement/scoring details are partially incorrect or inconsistent (e.g., LSAS scoring description does not match the instrument’s standard structure). Additionally, the results-reported analysis does not clearly align with a factor structure described in the results vs what is tested (and effect size/assumption checks are inconsistently reported).
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric targets coherence of design to RQ/hypotheses and completeness/correctness of participants/materials/procedures. It could be strengthened by explicitly calling out: randomization/allocation method, intervention fidelity/standardization (therapist involvement, time, adherence), and instrument scoring accuracy (including subscales/item counts where relevant). Also clarify that analysis plan should match the described design (factor structure, within/between factors) and that sample size justification or handling of missing data/outliers should be briefly reported.
results_feedback: The results report the general idea of a factorial mixed ANOVA and reports F-tests and some post-hoc p-values, which helps readability. However, the reporting is incomplete/unclear in key places: there is no actual Table 1 content or descriptive statistics shown, the ANOVA degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with N and the factor structure, outliers are mentioned without specifying criteria/handling, and the assumption checks are stated without the relevant test statistics (and the statement that both normality and homogeneity were met is not supported with reported values). The post-hoc section also contains inconsistencies (e.g., p-values written as "p = <.001") and does not clearly report which comparisons were adjusted and how many tests were conducted. There is also limited evidence of statistical reasoning beyond reporting p-values.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric focuses on correctness/completeness/coherence/relevance of data analysis and reporting, and recognizes deeper statistical knowledge for higher levels. To improve grading consistency, specify minimum acceptable reporting elements at this course level (e.g., N per condition, means/SDs in a table, full ANOVA specification with correct dfs/terms, assumption test statistics or explicit statement of what was checked, and corrected post-hoc strategy). The rubric could also mention that p-value formatting errors and inconsistent dfs are not minor issues because they undermine correctness/interpretability.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states the main conclusion and addresses unexpected vs expected findings. It attempts to connect findings to Bandura/self-efficacy and interaction theory, and it provides multiple limitations and future research suggestions. However, the critical reflection remains somewhat general and sometimes overreaches: the theoretical interpretation is not tightly constrained by what the study actually measured/manipulated (no manipulation checks, no measures of self-efficacy/interaction mechanisms), and some interpretations rely on claims that are not well supported by the reported data. Limitations are present (age range, no follow-up, no theory-specific measures), but the discussion does not convincingly evaluate alternative explanations beyond asserting them. Overall, the construct-level reasoning does not consistently and rigorously link results to theory and to the operationalization used in the study.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric emphasizes: correct main conclusion reasoning, relevant and convincingly argued critical reflection, and responsible referencing. It could further operationalize “convincingly argued” by requiring: (a) at least one paragraph that explicitly maps each result pattern onto theory and (b) at least two alternative explanations with partial refutation. Consider clarifying that theoretical claims should be constrained by what was measured/manipulated, and that mechanism claims without direct measures should be framed as tentative.
lang_style_feedback: Writing is mostly understandable and has a generally scientific tone. There are frequent grammar/spelling issues and some unclear phrasing. Paragraph structure and signposting are present, but a number of sentences are ambiguous or contain logical/wording problems (e.g., awkward causal statements; imprecise instrument descriptions). APA issues appear in reference formatting and in-text citation consistency. Overall, language and style do not seriously impede understanding, but clarity and accuracy are not consistently high.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric currently covers overall scientific language quality, structure (single focus/topical sentences), grammar/spelling, and APA accuracy. For reliability, consider adding specific sub-criteria weighting: (1) whether ambiguous sentences impede comprehension, and (2) whether instrument/scoring descriptions contain factual inaccuracies due to phrasing. Also, clarify that reference-list completeness/APA consistency is part of ‘APA style referencing/layout’ but that minor formatting differences should not dominate when content and reasoning are strong.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a general societal/clinical relevance for SAD and builds a plausible rationale for comparing SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist, including a stated hypothesis. However, the argumentation line culminating in the RQ/hypotheses is not sufficiently clear and “hourglass-coherent”: key theoretical constructs (habituation/extinction/emotional processing, interaction theory, self-efficacy) are introduced but not consistently integrated into specific expectations for the pattern of results beyond broad statements. Prior research is summarized rather than used as building blocks with a clear mapping to the design and the added value of this study. Several references appear to be used for broad claims without being tightly connected to the exact constructs/variables and the specific research gap (e.g., online accessibility, waitlist/job impacts, “interaction theory” for therapist-ledness) and the transition to the final hypotheses is somewhat abrupt.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: correctness and clarity of the line of reasoning culminating in RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis; whether the RQ is clear and relevant; and whether references are used responsibly. The rubric would reward an introduction that (1) explicitly frames the gap (why SVRET vs TVRET specifically, and why waitlist), (2) connects each theoretical construct to an expected directional outcome and to the specific intervention differences, and (3) uses prior research as building blocks with clear implications for the stated hypotheses. To improve, the argument needs stronger “linking” from societal relevance → theoretical framework → prior research → precise RQ/hypotheses, with fewer broad or tangential reference uses.
methods_feedback: The design is broadly coherent (3 groups, pre/post, VR-based self-led vs therapist-led exposure, LSAS as outcome) and the study has a defined sample with basic demographics and exclusion criteria. However, the methods are not complete/correct enough for replication: key details are missing or unclear, especially (a) allocation procedure (“chosen randomly” after registration) lacks description of randomization and allocation concealment, (b) participants/comorbidities exclusion is vague, (c) operationalization of the intervention is incomplete (e.g., exact differences between SVRET and TVRET beyond “therapist on call” and “scenarios can be altered”; what therapist does in each session; fidelity/standardization; how “on call” works; whether participants in SVRET received feedback/adaptation), (d) measurement timing beyond pre/post is not fully specified and there is no description of follow-up handling, (e) dependent variable operationalization is inconsistent/unclear (LSAS scoring described in a way that does not match what LSAS actually measures; also mentions multiple regression but results report an ANOVA), and (f) data-analysis plan/assumption checks and outlier handling are described in a way that leaves important decisions unspecified. These gaps keep the methods at “developing/insufficient” rather than adequate-good for this course level.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: study design coherence with RQ/hypotheses and comprehensiveness/correctness of participants, materials, and procedures. The rubric expects enough operational detail for replication: recruitment and random allocation description, clear inclusion/exclusion criteria, fully specified intervention differences (what participants do in each condition and how standardized), complete questionnaire/instrument scoring description aligned with the actual scale, and a transparent, matching statistical analysis plan. Missing or inconsistent operationalization and incomplete procedural/material details prevent reaching higher bands.
results_feedback: Results include the main analysis concept (factorial mixed ANOVA with condition × time), some assumption checks (QQ plots, Levene), identification of outliers, and a basic reporting of F-tests and p-values including post-hoc comparisons. The main conclusion is therefore supported statistically in broad strokes. However, reporting is incomplete/unclear versus the expectations from the course sample structure: there is no actual Table 1/2 content in the provided text (only headings), the outlier statement is not followed by what was done (removed or retained) in a way consistent with the claim “all data was used”, effect sizes are not reported, and the structure of the post-hoc reporting is difficult to follow (e.g., a pre-test comparison p-values that include inconsistent formatting/accuracy, and the significant SVRET>TVRET result is stated without clarifying whether it is at post only or also tested at change scores). The analysis appears mostly appropriate for the design, but the statistical reporting does not yet demonstrate thorough knowledge/complete transparency.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: correctness/completeness/coherence and relevance of data analysis and reporting to the stated design and procedure; showing adequate statistical reasoning. The rubric expects (at minimum) complete descriptive tables/figures for main variables, clear reporting of the statistical model consistent with the design (including relevant degrees of freedom/terms), transparent assumption/outlier handling, coherent post-hoc structure, and (ideally) effect size reporting. Incomplete tables/figures, unclear outlier handling, and lack of effect sizes reduce the grade band.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states the aim, restates the main findings, and provides interpretations. There is some engagement with prior work (Kampmann, Lindner, Kan) and some critical reflection (age range limitation, no follow-up, untested theoretical mechanisms, suggestion to test FNE). However, several major rubric elements are weak: interpretations rely on theory claims (self-efficacy vs interaction/ZPD) without sufficiently demonstrating that the data directly test those mechanisms; alternative explanations are discussed (motivation) but not convincingly tied to a clear refutation structure. The critical reflection is not detailed enough in a study-specific way: limitations focus on broad issues (age, follow-up, mechanism) but do not address other important design/measurement limitations suggested by the results section (e.g., how therapy adherence/compliance was monitored; potential expectancy effects; measurement scope only via LSAS; equivalence/fidelity of VR scenarios and therapist involvement). Also, the final “conclusion” is overly strong for the evidence presented (small sample, short follow-up period, multiple comparisons without reported effect sizes/robustness). Overall, the reasoning is somewhat coherent but not convincingly argued for to the “good/excellent” level.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: whether the reasoning culminating in the main conclusion is correct and coherent; whether critical reflection is relevant and convincingly argued; and responsible references. Higher bands require (1) construct-level interpretation tied to the theoretical framework used in the introduction, (2) explicit comparison to prior studies on the same outcomes/designs, (3) at least two study-specific, non-generic limitations, and (4) coherent implications/future directions that logically follow from findings and limitations. Over-claiming and weak mechanism testing reduce the grade band.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: clarity and structure of scientific language, paragraph/topical sentence discipline, and APA referencing/layout and statistical clarity. Higher bands require near-faultless grammar/spelling, minimal vague statements, consistent APA reference formatting, and clear, non-redundant paragraphing with strong topical focus. To reach the top band, the writing should also avoid inconsistent terminology and ensure that statistical terms and descriptions match the analysis actually reported.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a generally understandable topic (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist for SAD), includes societal relevance and a literature-based rationale, and ends with a clear aim and testable hypotheses. However, the line of argumentation is not fully coherent throughout: multiple theories are introduced (exposure mechanisms, interaction/ZPD, self-efficacy) but not all are cleanly connected to the specific SVRET vs TVRET manipulation and the precise hypotheses. Some claims are asserted without adequate integration or clear causality, and there are citation/quality issues (e.g., mixing up year/authors in places, and using some references that are not among the required core set). The prior research section mostly summarizes studies, but the “building blocks” linkage to what is novel in this design is sometimes unclear or comes late. Overall, it is on the developing/adequate boundary for the rubric criterion.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric: The criterion focuses on whether the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis is present, correct, coherent, and convincing enough, supported by relevant and up-to-date literature. This paper meets the minimum structure (societal relevance + theory + RQ/hypotheses), but the argumentation is not consistently built using the theories as actual explanatory constructs tied to the SVRET vs TVRET manipulation. Future calibration suggestion: explicitly reward clear “how the theory predicts the differential effect between SVRET and TVRET” and penalize when theories are listed without re-used explanatory work. Also ensure the rubric accounts for citation quality (including whether the required core articles are properly used).
methods_feedback: Design is mostly coherent with the RQ/hypotheses (3-arm condition, pre vs post, LSAS outcome). Participants and some eligibility criteria are given, and the VR materials plus LSAS measurement are described. But several critical method details are missing or insufficiently specified for replication and for evaluating internal validity: randomization procedure is not described; recruitment/diagnostic procedure and how “diagnosed with SAD” is verified is unclear; allocation concealment is not described. The VR manipulation details are incomplete (exact session duration, structure/timing per module, therapist involvement in TVRET vs “on call” vs active guidance, how “altered to participant’s needs” is implemented, and standardization across conditions). The procedure lacks key elements such as adherence/number of sessions completed, handling of dropouts/non-compliance, and what happens to the waitlist after the study. The analysis plan is vague/inconsistent (ANOVA terms/df don’t match the described design well; regression mentioned in Intro is not used in Results).
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric: Focus is on coherence of study design with RQ/hypotheses and completeness/correctness of Participants, Materials, and Procedures. This paper fits the general design idea but is missing several replication-critical details (randomization procedure, verification of diagnostic status, adherence/compliance, standardized intervention components, therapist involvement specifics, dropout handling). Future calibration suggestion: clarify that replication-critical missing details should cap the score even if the general design is coherent; also separate “some details missing” vs “internal validity missing” (e.g., randomization/allocation, adherence, therapist role).
results_feedback: The results section reports a factorial mixed ANOVA, main effects, an interaction, and post-hoc comparisons, which fits the stated design. Reporting is mostly coherent at a basic level and includes p-values, though effect sizes are missing. The section is incomplete relative to the sample template: there is no actual Table 2 content (means/SDs not provided), the demographics table is outside the Results section, and the figure is not properly specified (no axes/values). The assumption checks are mentioned briefly but not consistently. There are also statistical-reporting inconsistencies (e.g., “Boxplot revealed two outliers” while also stating all data used; df values appear odd given N=99 and 3 conditions; post-hoc p-values formatting like “p = <.001”). The analyses are not sufficiently demonstrated as “in-depth” knowledge per the rubric, so it lands around adequate/developing.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric: Evaluate correctness/complete reporting in terms of stated design, and demonstration of statistical reasoning knowledge. The rubric could more explicitly require that key numeric descriptives (means/SDs) and the figure/table referenced in text are actually present, and that df values and omnibus/interaction reporting align with the design and N. Also consider adding a small criterion for effect sizes to reduce ambiguity between “statistically significant” and “substantively meaningful.”
discussion_feedback: The discussion states the main conclusion correctly (both exposure conditions improve vs waitlist; SVRET > TVRET). It compares with prior research and offers explanations based on self-efficacy and interaction/ZPD, and it includes limitations and future directions. However, critical reflection is partly problematic: limitations are present but some are speculative or not well tied to the actual study limitations (e.g., elderly/technology argument is plausible but not grounded in measured variables or sample characteristics beyond age range). The “self-efficacy vs interaction theory” interpretation is not convincingly tested; the paper acknowledges mechanism testing is not done, but still makes strong mechanistic claims. Also, the discussion repeatedly references prior studies, but the connections are sometimes hand-wavy (e.g., attributing differences solely to in vivo vs VR without checking design equivalence). Overall it is coherent enough to be adequate, but not convincing/exact enough for a higher band.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric: Looks for correct/complete main conclusion reasoning, responsible reference use, relevance of critical reflection, and convincingly argued limitations/future directions. This paper provides limitations and future directions, but mechanistic interpretation (self-efficacy/interaction theory) is not fully supported by the measurements collected. Consider clarifying in the rubric that theory-based mechanism claims must be limited to what is directly tested, or explicitly labeled as post-hoc interpretation with weaker certainty.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric: Current bands reward near-faultless grammar/APA and clear paragraph structure with single topical sentences. This paper has frequent grammatical and statistical formatting issues and several clarity problems (especially around p-values and missing numeric table/figure content). Calibration suggestion: consider whether inconsistent p-value formatting and missing referenced tables/figures should be treated as APA/layout errors (affecting the same band) rather than only “language,” because it impacts comprehension similarly.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a generally relevant topic (SAD, VR/self-led vs therapist-led exposure), defines several key constructs (exposure principles, self-efficacy, interaction/zone of proximal development) and ends with an RQ/aim and testable hypotheses. However, the overall “hourglass” logic is only partially convincing: the theoretical framework is mentioned but not tightly operationalized into specific expectations/mechanisms that clearly carry through the entire argument. The prior-research section mixes several studies but does not clearly synthesize them into a coherent gap statement; some claims are asserted rather than argued (e.g., about thresholds/waitlists causing job outcomes), and several references are not aligned to the specific methodological comparisons needed for the SVRET/TVRET vs waitlist design. Also, some cited core requirements are not met in the narrative (you reference Kampmann/Lindner etc., but there is no consistent, accurate build using the exact four core articles as building blocks, and there are at least a few unclear/incorrect citations or mismatches).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: line of reasoning culminating in RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis. Strengths: RQ/aim and hypotheses are present; societal and clinical relevance is introduced early; multiple relevant theories/constructs are named and briefly defined. To move toward “Adequate/Good”: (1) tighten the synthesis of prior research into a clear, explicit gap statement that directly matches the design (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist, and specifically what is being isolated by not using in-vivo components); (2) make the theoretical framework operative by stating how each theory component implies specific, testable hypotheses (and why these differ between SVRET and TVRET); (3) ensure all references used are up-to-date and directly relevant to the exact comparisons you make; (4) improve argument clarity with more precise causal/logical connectors and fewer unsupported factual assertions.
methods_feedback: The methods include the key design elements at a basic level (participants with inclusion/exclusion, allocation to SVRET/TVRET/waitlist, a stated intervention length, and the primary outcome measure LSAS). The procedure provides a chronological overview with therapist-contact details for TVRET and self-scheduling for SVRET. Yet the section lacks several important replicability details: (1) no clear description of randomization procedure/stratification; (2) materials lack specifics about how the VR scenarios were standardized/altered between conditions and how “therapist on call” worked; (3) no reliability/feasible scoring procedure beyond the point range; (4) the analysis plan is under-specified (no exact ANOVA model structure stated beyond “Factorial Mixed ANOVA”, no handling of outliers/assumptions beyond a brief mention, no covariates, and regression language in Materials conflicts with later ANOVA reporting). Overall completeness is around the mid band: coherent enough to understand the study, but not complete/correct for replication at bachelor thesis level.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: study design coherence and completeness/correctness of Participants, Materials, Procedures. Strengths: participants, inclusion/exclusion, allocation to conditions, intervention duration, and primary outcome measure are included; procedure is chronological. To move toward “Adequate/Good”: (1) specify randomization/assignment procedures more clearly; (2) fully and consistently describe intervention differences and what is standardized vs individualized (especially what the therapist does in TVRET and what “on call” entails); (3) resolve inconsistencies in outcome analysis planning (avoid mixing regression language with ANOVA without explanation); (4) include key missing analytic/material details for replication (e.g., VR session duration, number of exposures per scenario, adherence criteria, and how outliers are handled).
results_feedback: Results report the main test (mixed ANOVA), main effects and the interaction, and include post-hoc comparisons aligned with the stated hypotheses. However, key elements are missing or inconsistent: Table 1 (means/SDs by condition/time) is not actually provided; only a demographics snippet appears. Assumption checks are mentioned but not reported (no Levene/F tests values; normality checks not quantified). Outliers are stated, but no decision rule or impact is described. Several statistical reporting issues reduce correctness/clarity: degrees of freedom appear as if both dfs are 1 and 99 (but a mixed design with 3 conditions typically implies different df patterns); some p-values are written oddly (e.g., “p = <.001”), and the post-hoc section includes comparisons but not clearly in a consistent family-wise-correction context (Holm correction mentioned, but the number of comparisons/family is not specified). Also, the figure is referenced but not provided. Because the core pattern (improvement in both exposure groups vs waitlist) is plausible and discussed consistently, the score stays in the adequate band rather than lower, but it is not “complete/correct” per rubric.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: data analysis/reporting correctness, completeness, and statistical reasoning depth. Strengths: reports an appropriate general approach (mixed ANOVA) and includes main effects, interaction, and post-hoc comparisons. To move toward “Adequate/Good”: (1) include the actual descriptive statistics table with means/SDs by condition/time; (2) report assumption check results with sufficient detail; (3) correct inconsistencies in df/p-value formatting and ensure the reported ANOVA df matches the stated design; (4) report outlier handling transparently; (5) ensure the figure/table exist and match the reported tests; (6) clarify the post-hoc correction family and the exact contrasts.
discussion_feedback: The discussion clearly restates the aim and provides an interpretation of the main pattern: SVRET and TVRET both improve SAD symptoms vs waitlist, with SVRET outperforming TVRET. It compares to prior research and gives at least some reasoning for unexpected findings (online-to-in-vivo change in Lindner; hypothetical role of exposure format; suggestion to replicate and test). It also includes critical reflections and multiple future directions (age/generalizability, follow-up needed, manipulation checks for self-efficacy vs interaction mechanisms, testing fear of negative evaluation via FNE). However, the theoretical integration has weaknesses: Bandura and interaction theory are invoked, but the data do not directly test these mechanisms as written, and the discussion sometimes leans on post-hoc storytelling (“therefore the result supports interaction theory/self-efficacy theory”) without a direct test. References are mostly used responsibly, but there are a few questionable causal claims and at least one citation in a way that is not clearly supported by the design (e.g., self-efficacy vs motivation distinction). Critical reflection exists and is relevant, but could be more construct-level and more explicitly tied to the results’ specific pattern and limitations (e.g., treatment adherence, blinding, attrition, multiple comparisons, effect sizes).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: reasoning culminating in main conclusion and critical reflection relevance/convincingness. Strengths: main conclusion aligns with results; comparisons to prior research are present; limitations and future research suggestions are relevant (age range, need for follow-up, mechanism tests, adding FNE). To move toward “Good/Excellent”: (1) more explicitly connect each limitation/future direction to a specific methodological issue in the current study (e.g., adherence measurement, no follow-up, multiple comparisons, mechanism not directly tested); (2) avoid overclaiming theory support—state clearly what the results do and do not test; (3) incorporate effect sizes or at least emphasize practical relevance; (4) include a more explicit theoretical implication paragraph at construct level.
lang_style_feedback: Language is understandable overall, but there are frequent grammar and phrasing issues (e.g., “people their personal relationships”, “TVRET into in vivo”, inconsistent capitalization, occasional missing punctuation), and some scientific-statistical writing is imprecise (“p = <.001”, inconsistent naming and capitalization of conditions). APA is partially followed (citations present, reference list mostly there), but there are noticeable issues in formatting and consistency (some reference formatting inconsistencies; some missing/incorrect in-text details; “Sources:” not an APA section; and no clear APA compliance for tables/figures). Paragraph structure is often less strictly single-focus than required, with some redundancy and long sentences. These issues keep it in the lower-to-mid band for style.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: scientific language, structure clarity, grammar/spelling, and APA accuracy. Strengths: readability is mostly maintained and citations are generally present. To move toward higher bands: (1) improve grammar and reduce awkward phrasing; (2) maintain consistent naming/capitalization of constructs and conditions; (3) correct statistical notation and p-value formatting; (4) ensure APA-consistent table/figure labeling and section headers; (5) reduce redundancy and ensure each paragraph has a single topical focus with clear topical sentences.

intro_feedback: The paper provides a generally understandable line from SAD and access problems to exposure/VRET and then to the SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist comparison. The RQ/hypotheses are stated in prose, and some theoretical constructs (self-efficacy, zone of proximal development) are linked to the comparison. However, the introduction does not clearly build a coherent “theory → predictions” chain throughout: interaction theory is mentioned but not used to derive concrete, testable expectations; several claims are asserted without clear argumentation (e.g., self-efficacy vs self-led motivation) and the gap/punchline appears somewhat late. Referencing is often relevant but not always up-to-date/robust (e.g., some questionable or mis-specified citations like “Anderson et al. 2016” when the allowed core article is 2013; also APA exposure-therapy source is a website-like reference). Overall, coherence and substantiation are “adequate” rather than strong.
intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction rubric rewards a clear, coherent line culminating in RQ + scientific relevance + hypothesis, with appropriate and up-to-date/relevant references. This paper is in the “adequate/developing” range because (1) the societal relevance and problem are present early, but the full argumentative build-up to testable predictions is not fully consistent; (2) the RQ is stated, but the theoretical framework-to-hypothesis link is only partly convincingly argued (especially for interaction theory); (3) references are mostly relevant but not consistently accurate/up-to-date (some core-article mismatch/uncertainty). For a higher band, the introduction should more explicitly define constructs/variables relevant to the exact hypothesis and make explicit predictions derived from the theory, then show a tighter “why this design” transition.
methods_feedback: Design is largely coherent with the stated RQ (three conditions; pre/post; VR exposure manipulation; LSAS as outcome). Participants and basic allocation are described, and there are some inclusion/exclusion criteria (psychotherapy last year, comorbidity, age, Dutch language). Materials and measures are described at a high level, but key missing details prevent near-replication: there is no clear reporting of assignment procedure (randomization method), no detailed description of session duration/timepoint structure beyond “10-week program” and “two scenarios per session,” no operationalization of how therapists modified scenarios in TVRET, and no scoring description for LSAS (items, fear/avoidance scoring ranges) beyond a rough points description. The stated analyses are not fully consistent with the reported design details (e.g., outliers mentioned; ANOVA factors described, but degrees of freedom and model specification are unclear). Methods therefore match the mid-band: not incomplete/incomprehensible, but lacking multiple important details for completeness/accuracy at this stage.
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric emphasizes that the study design must be coherent and clearly related to RQ/hypotheses, and that participants/materials/procedure are described comprehensibly and completely (for this level). This paper meets the “adequate/developing” requirement because key components are present, but important replication details are missing or unclear: random allocation procedure, therapist-led flexibility parameters, session timing/structure, attrition handling, and a precise LSAS scoring/administration description. For a higher band, include all required operational details (what exactly differs between SVRET and TVRET beyond “therapist guidance,” how therapists decide/standardize scenario changes, timing of questionnaires, and full scoring description). Ensure the statistical analysis description matches the design (including correct factor structure and consistent degrees of freedom/reporting).
results_feedback: The results contain the correct broad elements (ANOVA, main effects, interaction, post-hoc comparisons, mentions of assumption checks). However, the reporting is not “complete/correct”: the model details are inconsistent (e.g., factorial mixed ANOVA stated with pre/post as within factor, but ANOVA results use F(1, 99) which is not appropriate for a typical mixed model with three groups; the degrees of freedom don’t match the stated sample size/three conditions). The tables/figures are missing (Table 2 and Figure 1 are referenced but not provided), leaving descriptive results unclear beyond Table 1 age/demographics. Outlier handling is mentioned but not tied to specific actions (e.g., which outliers, how handled). Post-hoc p-value formatting is inconsistent (e.g., p=<.001; also some comparisons reported with limited context). Because of these issues, statistical reporting and coherence are only partly aligned with the stated design.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric requires correct and (for the student level) complete/coherent reporting aligned with the stated design, including descriptive stats and appropriate tests with correct degrees of freedom. This paper is not fully in the higher bands because (1) degrees of freedom/model specification appear inconsistent with a 3-group mixed design; (2) referenced Table 2 and Figure 1 are not actually provided in the submission text; (3) outlier handling is mentioned but not operationalized; and (4) some p-value formatting and context are unclear. To reach “good,” the results should report the means/SDs per condition/time, ensure test statistics (F with correct df) match the model, and clearly show the pattern in a figure/table.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states the main conclusion (SVRET and TVRET reduce SAD vs waitlist; SVRET outperforming TVRET) and attempts to compare to prior research (Kampmann, Lindner, Kan). It also provides interpretation using self-efficacy and interaction theory, and it includes limitations and future directions (age range; lack of follow-up; manipulation checks; measuring broader SAD constructs vs FNE). However, theoretical interpretation remains somewhat assertive and not tightly evidenced by results: interaction theory is said to be “not in line” but the paper does not clearly test interaction-theory-specific mechanisms, nor does it specify what would have supported it. Limitations are present but vary in specificity and sometimes mix design explanations with speculative mechanisms (relapse/self-efficacy decrease) without direct data. Overall it is coherent enough to reach an adequate band, but the argumentation and critical reflection are not yet convincing/grounded at the “good” level.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric rewards correct main conclusion reasoning, responsible use of references, relevant critical reflection, and convincing argumentation connecting results to theory and prior studies. This paper fits an “adequate” band: the main conclusion and comparisons are present, and limitations/future directions are relevant. However, the theory connection is not sufficiently specific/test-driven (interaction theory is claimed unsupported without a clear mechanism test), and some explanations are speculative without direct measures. For a higher band, each theoretical claim in the discussion should be explicitly tied to either (a) what the data directly tests or (b) a justified inference, with clearer refutation of alternatives.
lang_style_feedback: Clarity is mostly acceptable, but there are frequent scientific-language and APA/style issues: many sentences have grammar/word-choice errors (“their personal relationships”; “TVRET into in vivo”; “tasking”; “zone of proximal development” usage). Paragraph structure sometimes lacks single-focus topical sentences. Statistical and scientific notation is inconsistent (p-value formatting, missing spaces/commas, inconsistent variable naming: LSAS vs LSAS scores; LSAS vs LSAS Total). There are also reference issues (e.g., some citation years/targets inconsistent with the earlier listed core articles; at least one likely mis-citation: “Anderson et al. 2016”). These problems reduce the language/APA band into the developing/lower-adequate range.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & style rubric rewards near faultless scientific language, clear paragraph/sentence structure (often single focus with topical sentences), and minimal APA/layout/reference errors. This submission shows comprehension but has recurring grammatical/word-choice errors, inconsistent statistical notation, and at least some citation/reference issues (including possible mismatch of cited prior studies). To score higher, tighten editing (grammar), ensure consistent variable terminology, correct p-value/statistical formatting, and verify all citations against the reference list and the course’s required core articles.

intro_feedback: The societal relevance and broad problem framing are present early, and the paper clearly defines SAD and exposure/VRET and states an aim with an RQ/hypotheses. However, the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/hypothesis is only partly convincing: important constructs (interaction theory, self-efficacy) are introduced but not tightly integrated into specific, testable expectations for SVRET vs TVRET beyond general statements. Several claims are not well substantiated with the provided literature (e.g., “only difference is gradual steps”/mechanisms; threshold-for-seeking-help argument), and some used studies are not perfectly aligned to the design as described (e.g., frequent reliance on sources that do not map cleanly onto “virtual-only” comparisons). References are a mix of relevant and less appropriate/out-of-scope sources (some are generic websites/news and some are mismatched years/study targets), so the “all relevant and up to date” standard is not met.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric rewards a clear and convincing hourglass line of reasoning to the RQ/hypothesis. It currently expects (a) a coherent societal relevance tied tightly to the target population and design, (b) a theoretical framework with constructs and relationships that are then used again to justify hypotheses, and (c) high relevance/up-to-dateness of references. Strengths: clear topic/problem framing and early definitions. Gaps to guide improvement: make the theoretical constructs (self-efficacy, interaction/ZPD) operational in the argument (what exactly differs between SVRET vs TVRET and why that should affect LSAS), and ensure the referenced prior studies logically “build” toward the exact virtual-only comparison (not only general VRET success).
methods_feedback: The method is generally coherent with the stated design (diagnosed SAD; three conditions; 10-week intervention; LSAS pre/post). Participants and basic inclusion/exclusion criteria are provided, as well as core intervention features and the main outcome measure. However, important replication-relevant details are missing or unclear: (1) how random allocation was implemented, (2) missing sample details (no recruitment procedure details, no attrition/compliance, no compensation), (3) materials description contains inaccuracies/ambiguities (e.g., “Ruscio” scenarios but “Kan et al.” program; unclear VR-scenario count and administration specifics), (4) scoring description of LSAS is confusing/inconsistent with known LSAS scoring, and (5) the analysis plan is incomplete (no mention of covariates, sphericity relevance, outlier handling beyond “boxplot,” or exact ANOVA model). Therefore, methods are not “complete and correct” for this level.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric expects participant/inclusion-exclusion details sufficient to replicate, clear allocation procedure (how randomization happened), comprehensive and correct description of interventions and materials per condition, and a complete correct analysis plan (including assumptions/outlier decisions). It also expects instrument description with correct scoring/ranges. Strengths in student work: coherent overall structure and a clear outcome measure (LSAS).
