intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric rewards (i) a clear line of reasoning culminating in the RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, (ii) appropriate/relevant and up-to-date references, and (iii) convincing argumentation. For a top band, the framework should be more explicitly “tight”: define key constructs/assumptions tied to IV vs VR differences, and make the superiority prediction *directly and uniquely derivable* from the theory. References should be consistently appropriate to adolescents and to the specific comparison being justified (VRET vs IVET rather than VRET as part of CBT vs waitlist). Ensure the punchline (why this study, why this design, why now) is early and unequivocal.
methods_feedback: Participants, key inclusion/exclusion criteria, random assignment, intervention dose (10 sessions, twice weekly), and main measurement instrument are described. Materials include adequate detail about VR setup and IVET implementation, and the LSAS-CA description includes items, scoring ranges, and a cut-off (though not the practical use of the cut-off for recruitment/classification). Procedure is chronological at a reasonable level. Missing/unclear elements that limit replication and that likely keep it from “complete and correct”: (1) no explicit handling of the outliers beyond stating they were included; (2) no clear description of what happens at follow-up/randomization of waitlist beyond “then randomly assigned”; (3) the “block of 30 minutes” structure is stated, but session duration/details of therapist involvement in each condition are not fully comparable/standardized; (4) the demographic table is only partially present in the prompt (so completeness can’t be verified); (5) no explicit description of how fidelity/adherence to exposure homework/adjustment rules was ensured.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric expects a coherent design closely related to the RQ/hypotheses and complete/correct description of participants, materials, and procedures. Strong submissions include sufficient replication detail: allocation process, recruitment and eligibility operationalization, session timing and therapist role comparability, standardized intervention content and adjustments, and instrument scoring details. It also expects the description to avoid missing key analysis/inclusion details that matter for interpreting results (e.g., how exclusions/outliers were handled) and to keep materials vs procedure separation clear.
results_feedback: Statistical reporting is mostly understandable and the analysis matches the design at a basic level (mixed ANOVA; time effect, condition effect, interaction). Assumption checking is reported (Shapiro-Wilk, homogeneity via Levene). Reporting of post-hoc comparisons is present and the main interpretation aligns with the pattern (both treatments > waitlist; no VRET vs IVET difference). However, several result-details are problematic for the target “sample results”-style completeness: (1) Table 1 is referenced but not fully formatted in the provided text; (2) the results omit effect sizes and confidence intervals, and there’s no clear correction/approach for multiple comparisons beyond “Holm corrected post-hoc”; (3) interpretation mentions “A 3×2 Factorial Mixed ANOVA” but the degrees of freedom presented are inconsistent-looking (and not cross-checkable without the missing table/group sizes in the text); (4) the results section doesn’t clearly describe whether outliers were checked/robustly analyzed (just that they were included). Hence: mostly correct, but not fully complete/precise in line with an excellent PT:PR Results section.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric rewards correct and complete statistical reporting, coherent alignment with stated design/procedure, and clear relevance to hypotheses. Excellent work includes: descriptive statistics in clear tables, a visualization of the key interaction/pattern, appropriate assumption checks (with enough detail), correct model/test reporting, post-hoc comparisons with stated correction, and (in many cases) effect sizes/CI or otherwise very clear inferential context. It also expects the narrative to be tightly anchored in what the analysis actually shows, without overreaching beyond the reported stats.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states the main conclusion clearly and revisits expectations: both VRET and IVET outperform waitlist, but VRET is not superior to IVET. It connects results to prior research and the theoretical framework (conditioning/exposure mechanisms), and offers plausible explanations for the null VRET>IVET outcome (e.g., CBT overlap in prior work; potential cognitive avoidance/credibility). There is also critical reflection with at least two study-specific limitations (VR dialogue realism/fear of uncertainty; therapeutic alliance differences). Future directions are concrete (more interactive VR; measure therapeutic alliance; test VRET+CBT). Remaining weaknesses that prevent the “excellent” band: limitations and mechanisms are not always sharply tied to the *exact* measures/design (e.g., fear of uncertainty is introduced but not measured), and some claims about why effects happened are somewhat speculative without direct evidence in this study. Still, reasoning and critical reflection are sufficiently relevant and largely convincing for a first research paper.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric rewards correct and coherent main-conclusion reasoning, convincing reflection tied to the study’s data, responsible use of references, and strong insight into limitations/future directions. For a higher band, limitations should be specific and ideally linked to measurable constructs/procedural features, unexpected results should be interpreted using the intro’s theory/literature, and implications should follow from the evidence while acknowledging what remains unknown. Concrete future research should clearly derive from the limitations you identified.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric rewards near-faultless scientific language, clear structure (single-focus paragraphs with topical sentences and good paragraphing), minimal grammar/spelling errors, and minimal APA/reference/layout errors. It also penalizes vague/ambiguous statements in crucial places and unnecessary redundancy. For top marks, keep terminology consistent across sections, use active/precise scientific phrasing, and maintain clean APA-style citations and formatting for tables/figures and statistical reporting.

intro_feedback: The introduction has a clear topic, societal relevance (adolescent prevalence/untreated SAD), a plausible theory (classical/operant conditioning framing), and a coherent rationale for comparing VRET vs IVET. The RQ/hypothesis are clearly stated and aligned with the prior research discussion (adolescents, VRET acceptability, VRET mechanisms). However, the theoretical framework is somewhat broad and occasionally shifts levels (conditioning vs cognition/CBT-like mechanisms) without fully integrating them as a single, testable mechanism for VRET>IVET. Also, the “previous research” section is missing some required core-study coverage in the provided text (the rubric expects Anderson 2013, Kampmann 2016, Bouchard 2017 plus one extra—your intro cites others, but the alignment to the core set is not fully visible), and the justification for choosing exactly the waitlist and only pre/post measurement is not strongly argued. These issues prevent an ‘excellent’ argumentation level.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric expects: (1) a clear line of reasoning culminating in an appropriate RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis; (2) sound, mostly coherent argumentation; (3) relevant/up-to-date references. Your rubric’s ‘Excellent’ band requires very coherent and very convincing argumentation plus all references being relevant and up to date. To make grading more consistent, it could explicitly clarify whether missing/insufficient core-paper coverage (per course requirement) should lower the score even if the general literature is relevant, and it might separate ‘RQ clarity’ from ‘theoretical mechanism/testability’ as distinct dimensions.
methods_feedback: Methods are mostly complete and mostly correct: participants (diagnostic criteria + exclusion criteria + N per group + allocation), materials (VR system and scenario tailoring; IVET general setting; LSAS-CA description and scoring), and procedure (screening/consent/randomization; 10 sessions twice weekly; session structure; timing of LSAS-CA; waitlist thereafter). Still, several important details are missing or insufficiently specified for replication/validity at this course level: group allocation procedure is described, but participant numbers per group in demographics vs randomization are not clearly and consistently presented in-text; recruitment sources and inclusion/exclusion operationalization (e.g., confirmation method for SAD diagnosis beyond ‘online interview’) are vague; therapist training and fidelity/supervision are not described; and key analytic details are not in Methods (though analyses belong to Results, the design description should state the statistical model clearly enough). Some wording also mixes materials/procedure (minor). Overall, it is adequate to good, but not ‘complete and correct’ per the rubric.
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric assesses coherence/fit with RQ/hypotheses and completeness/correctness of participants, materials, and procedures. To improve calibration, consider adding explicit sub-bullets for replication-critical items that commonly get missed (e.g., participant diagnostic confirmation method and timing; therapist training/fidelity; session length and exposure until criteria are met—how anxiety ratings are used; handling of outliers/withdrawals; and clear reporting of N per group at each timepoint). This would reduce ambiguity between ‘some details missing’ and ‘methods incomplete’.
results_feedback: Results reporting is clear and generally coherent with the stated factorial mixed ANOVA design (time and condition), includes assumption checking (normality via Shapiro-Wilk; homogeneity via Levene), provides F-statistics and p-values, and includes post-hoc comparisons plus a figure reference. The pattern in the narrative matches the reported tests (treatment vs waitlist, and no VRET>IVET difference post). However, there are multiple inconsistencies/omissions relative to the course expectations and the sample template: Table 1 is incomplete (missing cell values in the provided text), the ‘interaction’ is reported but the exact degrees of freedom correspond to a mixed design that isn’t fully reconciled with N and group sizes as written, outlier handling is not linked to any sensitivity analysis decision (e.g., whether robust methods were considered), and normality violation is handled via CLT without clarifying whether ANOVA is robust enough given the imbalance/pattern. No effect sizes are reported. These prevent reaching ‘excellent’ results quality even though the core inferential story is understandable.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric includes correctness/completeness/coherence/relevance and ‘in-depth knowledge’ of statistics. For better reliability, you could specify what counts as ‘in-depth knowledge’ at bachelor level (e.g., reporting effect sizes, model terms/assumptions consistent with design, and consistent df reporting) rather than leaving it implicit. Also, given the course’s sample-results template emphasis, it may help to explicitly include ‘presence of key descriptive tables and figures’ as a criterion for higher bands.
discussion_feedback: Discussion reaches the main conclusion correctly (both active conditions improve vs waitlist; VRET not superior to IVET). It links results to the introduction’s theory (conditioning/exposure mechanism) and to prior work (Anderson/Kampmann/Bouchard) in a mostly responsible way. Critical reflection is present with at least two study-specific limitations (VR scenario dialogue realism via semi-structured dialogue; therapeutic alliance differences). It also provides multiple future directions tied to limitations (more interactive VR; measure alliance; compare VRET vs VRET+CBT). The main deduction preventing ‘excellent’ is that some interpretations rely on additions that were not clearly set up in the theoretical framework (e.g., cognitive avoidance explanation and how it specifically operates in VRET vs IVET) and the discussion does not clearly address the waitlist-improvement interpretation (e.g., regression to mean, expectancy/assessment effects) beyond citing other studies. Overall, it is good-to-adequate and convincingly argued for the main findings, but not maximally grounded and not maximally precise for unexpected/non-significant effects.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric balances (1) correctness/completeness/coherence of main conclusion reasoning; (2) relevance and convincingly argued critical reflection; and (3) outstanding insight into limitations/future directions for ‘excellent’. To enhance objectivity, you could define what qualifies as ‘convincingly argued’ limitations (e.g., linking each limitation to a plausible mechanism affecting the specific results) and require at least one discussion of unexpected/non-significant effects tied to either design or theory.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric uses thresholds for errors in grammar/spelling, APA/reference errors, vague statements, and structural clarity. To strengthen consistency, it could specify whether student-level APA reference formatting issues are treated as ‘layout’ errors versus ‘content’ errors, and it could add guidance on how to count vague/ambiguous statements (e.g., examples or whether ‘sometimes’ unsupported interpretations count). This would reduce grader subjectivity.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains clear substantive background on SAD, rationale for exposure/VRET, and a stated comparative purpose and hypothesis. However, the line of reasoning is not sufficiently tight toward the specific RQ/hypothesis: the theoretical constructs are mostly descriptive (e.g., Pavlovian extinction) but not developed as a coherent explanatory framework for why guidance should change trajectories (post vs follow-up). Several concept definitions are either oversimplified or not operationally linked to the study’s exact manipulation (what “therapist guidance” concretely entails). Prior research is summarized, but the synthesis does not consistently build a convincing, step-by-step argument that distinguishes expected short-term vs longer-term effects for guided vs self-guided VRET. References are generally used responsibly, but there are also issues such as inconsistent/possibly incorrect citations (e.g., “Pavlovian extinction model” linkage) and at least one paper-year/title mismatch (Kampmann et al. 2015 vs course-required 2016; use of Kindred et al. 2022 is meta-analytic rather than experimental, and the intro claims effectiveness in a way that is not tightly tied to the required experimental evidence).
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric focus: the line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and a hypothesis that is coherently argued from theory and empirical work. Strengths include a clear topic and a stated comparative aim/hypothesis, plus some societal/scientific relevance. The main gaps relative to the rubric are: the theoretical framework is not used as a real explanatory engine for the expected differential pattern across measurement times; prior research is summarized but not consistently synthesized into a step-by-step argument that justifies the exact hypothesis about post-treatment differences. Some references appear not to match the rubric’s intended core experimental alignment and there are minor issues with citation appropriateness and consistency, which weakens the 'responsible use of references' criterion.
methods_feedback: The Methods section is mostly structured and includes key elements: participant recruitment/diagnosis, randomization, sample size/demographics, the main materials (VR setup), the dependent measure (LSAS-SR) with scoring info, and a chronological procedure with session number and timing. However, it is not yet fully “complete and correct” for replicability: (1) core intervention details are missing or vague (exact exposure scenario content, number/type of exposures per session, progression rule, duration and standardization of guidance in the virtual therapist vs real therapist, treatment fidelity, therapist training/competence, whether therapist involvement was standardized across participants); (2) the comparator is not fully specified as a controlled manipulation (e.g., whether the real therapist also instructs, does the virtual therapist deliver identical content and amount of interaction, are both conditions otherwise matched); (3) timing of at-home measures and survey administration context is not fully specified as part of materials/procedure in a way that would allow consistent replication; (4) analysis plan details are not fully aligned with stated results reporting needs (e.g., whether and how outliers were handled in the final mixed ANOVA and whether baseline equivalence was assessed statistically, and how missing data would have been treated—although attrition is reported as 0).
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric focus: study design coherent and clearly related to RQ/hypotheses; participants/materials/procedures described comprehensibly; methods complete and correct for replicability. Strengths include diagnosis method (SCID-5), demographics, randomization, inclusion/exclusion criteria, reward, VR hardware/software, and LSAS-SR scoring description, plus a chronological timeline. Main reasons it falls short of 'complete and correct' are missing intervention specifics (standardization/fidelity/protocol details, exact content of exposure tasks, how guidance differs minute-by-minute, what constitutes 'progressively more challenging'), incomplete reporting for replicability (e.g., adherence/fidelity monitoring), and limited alignment between analysis/outlier handling description and what was actually reported in the results.
results_feedback: The Results section reports an appropriate overall model (mixed-design ANOVA) and provides key inferential statistics (main effects and interaction) with effect sizes, plus a post-hoc strategy. However, reporting is incomplete/inconsistent: the table for descriptive statistics is not properly presented (and includes placeholders/label issues), the outlier description is provided without the actual resolution clearly stated (they say outliers were kept, but there is no clear consequence for analysis, sensitivity checks, or how those outliers affect assumptions). The assumption checks are summarized, but there is a mismatch between the described outliers and the normality statement (no quantification/where the normality was checked beyond a generic statement). Post-hoc results are plausible but the interpretation is sometimes internally inconsistent with the planned model/time structure (e.g., later claims about “no further decrease” in therapist-guided vs later significant changes at follow-up are not clearly reconciled with the post-hoc p-values reported). Overall, statistical reporting is closer to 'somewhat correct' than 'complete and correct' for the required student-level completeness.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric focus: data analysis and corresponding reporting correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to stated design and procedure. Strengths include using the correct broad design logic (condition × time mixed ANOVA) and reporting main effects, interaction, and post-hoc tests with corrections, plus reporting effect sizes. Main shortcomings: descriptive tables/figures are not properly presented; outlier handling is described without clarity on the impact (no sensitivity/robustness or explicit statement of how analysis treats them beyond 'kept'); assumption-check reporting lacks specificity; and the coherence between reported post-hoc comparisons and subsequent narrative interpretation is not always tight. These prevent reaching 'complete and correct' reporting.
discussion_feedback: The discussion covers main findings, compares them to prior work in general, offers theoretical explanations (human support; self-efficacy; context dependence in extinction), and includes limitations and future directions. However, the link between results and theoretical framework is somewhat underdeveloped: the explanation for the specific interaction pattern (guided > self-guided at post, convergence or continued change at follow-up) is not fully grounded in the earlier stated theory/constructs and could be supported with more direct evidence from the reported statistics (what exactly changed where, for each group). The critical reflection exists but remains partly generic and sometimes repeats what was already stated rather than providing a deeper study-specific critique (e.g., demand characteristics explanation is plausible but not tied to concrete design details and does not consider other plausible explanations such as differential adherence to home VR practice). The discussion also makes claims like “comparable outcomes seen in the long term” without clearly quantifying the magnitude of difference at follow-up beyond a single non-significant p-value; stronger argumentation would discuss effect sizes/confidence intervals and statistical power limitations.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric focus: reasoning culminating in main conclusion correct, complete/coherent; critical reflection relevant and convincingly argued for; responsible use of references. Strengths include stating conclusions consistent with the reported interaction pattern, attempting theory-based interpretation, comparing to some prior work, and offering multiple limitations with concrete future improvements. The main gaps are: theoretical implications are not convincingly tied back to the specific interaction pattern in a construct-level way; unexpected patterns are addressed but may be under-argued; and limitations could be more tightly connected to the specific design elements (measurement context/demand characteristics are plausible but not fully integrated into the quantitative results or alternative explanations).
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and mostly scientific, but there are recurrent clarity/precision problems: several sentences are ungrammatical or have missing words (e.g., 'potentially introduced response bias into the data'; 'due the presence of a therapist'; 'we can not rule out'); there is some imprecise phrasing (e.g., confusing 'significantly decrease' wording without specifying direction comparisons). APA/reference quality has multiple issues: citation formatting inconsistencies, at least one questionable reference entry (e.g., a 'Virtual Reality Exposure Therapy...' placeholder with (n.d.) and 'University of Amsterdam' not a clear journal article), and bibliographic inconsistencies (e.g., course-required core articles not all aligned by year). Statistical notation is sometimes inconsistent (e.g., table content missing; capitalization in variable names; p-values sometimes written with inconsistent spacing). Overall this fits 'developing/adequate' rather than 'good/excellent' style-per-rubric.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric focus: scientific language mostly correct; clear paragraph structure (single focus/topical sentences); few grammar/spelling/APA layout errors; few vague/ambiguous statements; minimal redundancy. Strengths include overall readability and a mostly appropriate academic tone. The paper contains multiple grammatical errors, imprecise phrasing, inconsistent/insufficient presentation of key components (tables/figures), and multiple APA/reference inconsistencies and occasional questionable reference entries. These issues reduce the grade from 'good' to 'adequate' range.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains an overall line from societal relevance (SAD prevalence, help-seeking barriers) to the comparison of therapist-guided vs self-guided VRET, and it states a clear aim and a directional hypothesis. However, the theoretical framework is largely implicit: exposure mechanisms are briefly described, but the specific constructs/relationships that justify *why* guidance should change the trajectory (post vs follow-up) are not fully developed and linked back in a detailed, logically grounded way. The prior-research part summarizes studies but does not consistently synthesize them into a clear build-up with explicit justification of the research gap (e.g., what exactly is missing relative to guided vs self-guided comparisons, and why your design resolves it). Several inconsistencies reduce argument quality (e.g., naming/numbering of core studies, some treatment of claims as established facts without tight referencing, and the hypothesis is stated but without explicit mapping to the previously discussed theory).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: (1) culminates in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis; (2) line of reasoning is correct/coherent and at least somewhat convincing; (3) RQ is clear and relevant given empirical/theoretical work; (4) references are used responsibly. Strengths to look for: early societal relevance connected to the target population and design; explicit conceptual definitions and logical transitions (constructs → theory → gap → RQ → hypothesis). Typical deductions at this level: prior research is summarized without synthesis; theory is mentioned but not used as a mechanism to generate expectations; the gap (“why this study”) is implied rather than explicitly argued; and hypotheses are stated without mapping to theoretical constructs and design choices.
methods_feedback: The Methods section includes participants, key materials, and a chronological procedure with enough detail to understand the broad design (N=60, SAD diagnosis via SCID-5, two conditions, 6 weekly sessions, LSAS-SR at pre/post/follow-up). Randomization and a basic rationale (gender stratification) are mentioned, as well as compensation. However, several required specifics are missing or unclear for replication and for assessing internal validity: what exactly differs across conditions besides the guidance (e.g., identical scenario sets, duration/intensity, therapist script/feedback content, therapist frequency/amount, whether the virtual therapist and human therapist provide comparable coaching, and how “progressively becoming more challenging” is operationalized); in the self-guided at-home headset usage there is ambiguity because the procedure says six weekly sessions and also says the self-guided headset is taken home—yet it does not explain logistics and timing; allocation concealment/blinding is not addressed; demographic reporting is incomplete as there is no table and SD is implausibly small. The LSAS-SR description is generally present, but the method omits the scoring range interpretation consistent with APA style (fine), and does not specify scoring procedure beyond an example.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: (1) study design coherent and clearly related to RQ/hypotheses; (2) participants/materials/procedures described comprehensibly; (3) methods complete and correct. Strengths to look for: clear inclusion/exclusion, allocation procedures, demographics, rewards; detailed intervention description (independent variable operationalization), standardized elements across conditions, accurate description of VR therapist content, timing, session logistics, and what participants actually do in each session; questionnaire details including reference, items, scoring interpretation and range; a chronological procedure only (no duplication of materials). Typical deductions at this level: missing operationalization details; unclear logistics (especially for self-guided/home delivery); insufficient comparability controls; unclear assessor/researcher roles; and missing/blurry details for replication (e.g., randomization checks, blinding).
results_feedback: The Results report includes the main planned mixed ANOVA, reports assumption checks, effect sizes (η²p), a follow-up/post-hoc approach, and describes key pairwise comparisons across time and condition. The overall pattern (both improve; therapist-guided stronger post; convergence by follow-up) is communicated coherently. However, there are major reporting issues that affect trustworthiness and completeness: Table 1 is missing its key content (the paper shows only a header and later provides fragments of means), the text states outliers at multiple timepoints but does not report how outliers were handled other than “decided to keep,” and still claims assumptions are met (unclear given multiple outliers in follow-up). There is an inconsistency/omission in the outlier description and in the reported comparisons (some p-values are given without specifying which tests/marginal means were used). Also, there is no clear reporting of descriptive statistics per time × condition in a consistent way, and no mention of multiple-comparison correction details beyond “Bonferroni.”
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: (1) data analysis and corresponding reporting are correct, complete, coherent, relevant to stated design/procedure. Strengths to look for: clear reporting of descriptive statistics per condition/time; reporting of ANOVA/appropriate tests aligned with design; assumption checks appropriate and consistent with the data/outliers; correct reporting of post-hoc tests and multiplicity corrections; effect sizes and clear mapping from hypotheses to results. Typical deductions at this level: missing or inconsistent tables/figures, unclear marginal vs raw mean reporting, outlier handling that conflicts with assumption statements, and incomplete reporting of the exact post-hoc comparisons performed.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion re-states the main conclusion, interprets expected vs unexpected findings, and addresses limitations and future research. It links the smaller short-term effect in self-guided to the role of human support and offers an explanation for continued improvement via self-efficacy, and it compares results to prior VRET literature. The limitations section includes multiple plausible, study-specific issues (context of post-test, generalizability, context of treatment, unmeasured barrier-related attitudes). However, the critical reflection would be stronger if it more directly challenged alternative interpretations with tighter engagement to the data pattern (e.g., why self-guided improves by follow-up if guidance only matters short-term; whether differential practice/time-on-task is possible; whether regression to the mean/outliers might contribute). Also, the Discussion does not clearly and explicitly evaluate the theoretical framework elements you introduced in the Introduction (e.g., Pavlovian extinction and exposure principles are mentioned earlier but not used to interpret the specific guided vs self-guided differences in a structured way). Future research ideas are present but could be more directly tied to the limitations and to testable predictions for mechanisms.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: (1) reasoning culminating in main conclusion is correct and coherent; (2) critical reflection on current study is relevant and convincingly argued; (3) references are used responsibly. Strengths to look for: main conclusion explicitly answers RQ and ties to each hypothesis; comparisons with prior research per expectation; explicit connection between results and theory (construct/construct level); study-specific limitations (at least two) with mechanistic relevance; at least two concrete future research directions that follow from limitations/findings; implications grounded in actual results. Typical deductions at this level: theorizing not connected back to introduction constructs; unexpected findings not strongly defended with alternative interpretations/refutations; limitations that are plausible but not evaluated against the actual pattern of results; future directions that are not directly testable or not clearly derived from the limitations.
lang_style_feedback: Scientific language is generally understandable, but there are frequent grammar and clarity problems that reduce academic polish (e.g., subject-verb agreement, run-on sentences, awkward phrasing like “post‑treatment LSAS‑SR scores were collected in different contexts…,” and some imprecise or inconsistent terminology). There are also structural issues: Results are missing/garbled table information; Discussion contains redundancy and some unclear causal wording (“could potentially be explained due the presence of a therapist”); and there are multiple punctuation/formatting problems and inconsistencies in capitalization and hyphenation. APA referencing is mostly present and mostly formatted, but there are errors/typos in citations in text (e.g., inconsistent year/author usage, minor inaccuracies), and at least one reference list entry appears incomplete/duplicated. Overall, language and APA are below the expected “Good” level for this course.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric focus: (1) near-faultless scientific language and readability; (2) components and paragraphs clearly structured with single focus/topical sentences; (3) hardly any grammar/spelling errors; (4) hardly any APA referencing/layout errors; (5) hardly any vague/ambiguous statements; (6) hardly any redundancy at paragraph/subsection level. What to aim for: clear signposting and consistent terminology; accurate statistical and methodological wording; correct APA in-text citations and a clean reference list; and table/figure integration so the reader can follow results without ambiguity.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a generally understandable topic, some societal framing, and a plausible rationale for comparing therapist-guided vs self-guided VRET. However, the line of reasoning culminating in a clear, well-substantiated RQ/hypothesis is only partially coherent: theoretical constructs are introduced (e.g., exposure/extinction, barriers to therapist involvement, self-efficacy) but are not fully integrated into a tight mechanism that directly supports the specific expected pattern across time. The prior research synthesis is somewhat descriptive rather than consistently argumentative (less explicit building toward the gap and the comparative prediction). The research design is introduced late and the hypothesis is stated, but the logic for why the post-treatment difference should be larger (and how follow-up is expected) is not strongly substantiated.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric emphasizes: (1) a clear societal/scientific problem, (2) constructs and theory defined and used, (3) a coherent line of reasoning building to the RQ/hypothesis with responsible references. Here, the societal relevance exists and the topic is clear, but the theoretical framework is not developed as a tight set of constructs/relationships that directly supports the specific comparative prediction across time. Prior research is summarized with some linking, but the argumentative chain (how each study builds the case for therapist involvement being the key mechanism vs alternative explanations) is only partially convincing. The RQ and hypothesis are stated, but the rationale for why the post-treatment effect should be stronger and how follow-up is expected is not convincingly argued.
methods_feedback: The methods section includes the core elements (participants, materials/measures, procedure). Participants are described with inclusion/exclusion criteria, demographics, and random allocation, and a clear treatment dose is given (6 weekly sessions of 60 minutes). The LSAS-SR description is detailed enough for replication at a basic level. However, several method-details needed for scientific rigor are missing or unclear/incorrectly specified relative to the rubric’s expectations: the operationalization of the VRET conditions is incomplete (what exactly participants do each session; what guidance content differs; what therapist role/amount of feedback differs; whether session content is standardized across locations); the procedure description mixes procedural and materials-level details somewhat; randomization stratification is stated but the reporting of how stratification was implemented is not provided; the timing of taking LSAS-SR at baseline/post/follow-up is described, but not enough to confirm consistency across conditions. Also, the methods do not clearly specify adherence/compliance, therapist training/competence, and what was controlled to prevent confounds (besides “where” the session happens and headset settings).
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects: (1) coherence between design and stated RQ/hypotheses, (2) comprehensive participant/material/procedure description, (3) correct and complete operationalization of independent variables and measures, and (4) enough detail for someone to replicate. The paper covers participants, LSAS-SR, and a basic exposure dose schedule. However, the rubric would require clearer, more complete operationalization of what differs between conditions (therapist role, guidance content, standardization of session activities, adherence, and assessment timing equivalence), as well as more complete reporting of procedures and controls relevant to confounds introduced by “treatment location” and “survey context.”
results_feedback: Results report the main ANOVA framework and some effect sizes and p-values, plus follow-up comparisons. This partially matches the sample results expectations (mixed ANOVA, interaction, post-hocs). However, the reporting is incomplete and sometimes not consistent/credible at the level of statistical reasoning required: degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with the sample size and the stated outlier handling; the outlier description is mentioned but there is no clear procedure for how outliers were identified/handled (the paper claims they were kept, but then the analysis/assumption checking is not reconciled with that). The results lack the required descriptive table content (the Table 1 is underspecified/garbled; means/SDs are not cleanly presented), and there is no figure or clear presentation of the interaction pattern. Some follow-up statements (e.g., “did significantly decrease in the self-guided condition” at follow-up) are not fully aligned with what the post-hoc p-values would imply, and the narrative does not clearly summarize the pattern at construct level beyond re-mentioning significance.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects that analysis and reporting are correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to the stated design/procedure, ideally demonstrating statistical knowledge commensurate with the course level. While the main mixed-design ANOVA and an interaction explanation are present, the reporting is not complete (missing/unclear table/figure content) and statistical reasoning is not sufficiently coherent (outlier mention without an analysis plan, apparent inconsistencies in df/narrative coherence). The results do not clearly meet the sample-results standard for completeness and clarity of the interaction pattern and assumption checks reporting.
discussion_feedback: The discussion connects to the study aim and largely reiterates the general conclusions (both conditions improve; therapist-guided more immediately; convergence later). It also engages with unexpected findings (self-guided continuing to improve after post). The comparison to prior research is present and generally relevant, and theoretical interpretation (human support; self-efficacy; context-dependent extinction) is attempted. However, the critical reflection remains somewhat limited: while multiple limitations are listed, they are not always tightly tied to the actual statistical findings (e.g., outlier presence, baseline comparability, immediate post context differences) and some limitations overlap without sharpening what they mean for the key inference (post-treatment difference vs follow-up convergence). The discussion also introduces explanations and claims (e.g., self-efficacy mechanism) without explicitly linking them to measured variables or testing them, and it provides fewer concrete, design-specific future directions that clearly follow from the specific weaknesses in the statistical/reporting decisions (e.g., addressing outlier influence, adding behavioral/independent measurement, fully standardizing assessment timing).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects: (1) correct main conclusion, (2) relevant and preferably convincingly argued critical reflection, (3) discussion that ties results back to the theoretical framework, and (4) responsible reference use. The discussion provides an overall correct/mostly coherent interpretation and addresses at least one unexpected pattern with plausible theory. However, critical reflection is somewhat generic/overlapping and not always tightly linked to the specific statistical outcomes and reporting choices (e.g., outlier handling, assessment context differences). The theoretical explanations are plausible but not clearly grounded in measured constructs beyond inference, and future research implications could be more specific and directly derived from the study’s limitations.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is acceptable, but there are frequent grammar and style issues (tense, agreement, phrasing) and several clarity/precision problems that affect scientific communication (e.g., awkward or incorrect sentences, inconsistent terminology such as therapist guidance wording, and places where meaning is ambiguous). APA/reference formatting shows some issues (minor inconsistencies, occasional citation formatting concerns). The presence/absence of tables/figures and their readability also affects clarity, and there are duplicated/garbled table lines in the provided content. Scientific language is mostly used appropriately but not consistently at a near-faultless level.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric values: (1) near-faultless scientific language, (2) clear paragraph structure with single focus/topical sentences, (3) minimal grammar/spelling errors, and (4) minimal APA/referencing/layout errors and ambiguous statements. The paper is understandable, but not consistently well-structured or precise; there are notable grammar and phrasing issues and some unclear/awkward statements that hinder “graceful style.” The provided table content also appears garbled, which is a clarity/layout problem beyond minor APA issues. Overall, the text does not reach the “adequate/good” threshold for clarity and faultlessness.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a generally understandable topic and some societal relevance, and it states a hypothesis and a clear aim (comparing therapist-guided vs self-guided VRET). However, the required “line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/hypothesis” is weakened by: (1) limited explicit theoretical constructs/relationships (the paper mostly describes treatments rather than building a construct-level framework for *why* guidance should change outcomes), (2) some argumentative jumps and vague causality (e.g., “more inclined to seek help” → “barrier removed” → “therefore effectiveness differs”), and (3) only partial integration of the required set of prior research as building blocks—core studies are cited, but the synthesis is not consistently tied to the specific RQ and hypothesis in a PEEL-like way. The RQ and hypothesis are present, but “very relevant given existing empirical and theoretical work” is not fully achieved.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric strengths: societal relevance is present early; SAD is defined; treatments (CBT/exposure/VRET) are introduced; RQ/hypothesis is stated. Rubric gaps: (1) The theoretical framework is not built using constructs + relationships; the paper largely lists mechanisms of CBT/exposure and then proceeds without a tight, construct-level pathway to the specific moderation/causality claim about therapist guidance. (2) Prior research is not consistently used as building blocks to culminate in a well-justified, very coherent hypothesis. (3) Logical connectors and the “why now/why this study” punchline appear late and are not tightly argued as an empirical gap (especially regarding why any difference should be expected specifically at post vs follow-up).
methods_feedback: Methods are mostly coherent and described with many required elements: participants (diagnosis method, demographics, exclusion criteria, randomization), materials (VR setup, LSAS-SR scoring and basic psychometrics), and a chronological procedure (6 weekly sessions, instructions, measurement timing, compensation). Main issues that prevent a higher score include: (1) missing/unclear operational details needed for replication (e.g., what exact exposure scenarios are used, how long sessions are in terms of exposure time, therapist guidance specifics and standardization, what the virtual therapist does at each step, therapist training/monitoring), (2) some wording and design clarity problems (e.g., “participants were able to pick a location of their choosing” but consistency/manipulation control is not fully specified), (3) assumption/reporting of outliers is not integrated with a preregistered/analytic plan in the methods, and (4) inclusion/exclusion and sample representativeness are described but not strongly substantiated for the RQ.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric strengths: design is coherent; participant recruitment/diagnosis and demographics are given; randomization is described; key instrument (LSAS-SR) is described with scoring range; procedure is chronological with session number and measurement timepoints; compensation is mentioned. Rubric gaps preventing higher levels: (1) intervention details lack essential replicability components (VR scenario specifics, therapist guidance content, virtual therapist protocol, treatment fidelity/standardization, session structure and exact exposure parameters). (2) The method for handling outliers is missing from the methods planning portion (outlier criteria/analysis plan), despite being discussed in results. (3) Some design-control details are unclear (e.g., participant ability to choose location and how consistency is ensured; therapist office vs home timing differences).
results_feedback: The results include the correct overall structure elements (sample completeness, descriptive table placeholder, ANOVA type, assumption checks, interaction probing, and p/η² reporting). The analysis choices are broadly plausible for a condition × time design. However, the reporting is not fully complete/consistent with the described sample and design: (1) Table 1 is not properly filled with the reported numbers/labels (placeholders and a missing/unclear table structure), (2) outliers are discussed in the text but there is no corresponding strategy (e.g., how outliers were identified and whether they were included in analyses—stated as ‘decided to keep’, but no descriptive stats or robustness check), (3) several reported p-values and effects are not fully tied to hypothesis-specific claims (e.g., the pattern at follow-up is described but numbers in the table/text don’t clearly support the stated “comparably effective” conclusion), and (4) some statistical phrasing is unclear (e.g., normality “met” without showing which tests/thresholds). Because results are partly understandable but not fully reproducible/complete, the score stays around developing-to-adequate.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric strengths: appropriate analysis family for mixed design (mixed ANOVA) is used; interaction is followed up; effect sizes (η²p) are reported; multiple testing correction is mentioned. Rubric gaps: (1) required reporting completeness is not met because the main table is not properly presented/filled and descriptive numbers are inconsistent/insufficiently clear. (2) Outlier handling is mentioned but not operationalized (identification method, influence checks/robustness). (3) Reporting lacks certain specifics needed for full adequacy (e.g., which normality test outcomes were used for each measurement timepoint/condition in a replicable way).
discussion_feedback: Discussion addresses the main conclusion, compares findings to previous work at a high level, and includes critical reflection with multiple limitations (context of post-test measurement, generalizability, context-dependent extinction, missing data on preference for therapist absence). It also proposes follow-up ideas. Still, the discussion does not consistently reach “convincingly argued” because: (1) the theoretical interpretation is somewhat underdeveloped (self-efficacy and human support are mentioned, but not tightly linked to the exact observed patterns at post vs follow-up), (2) some claims appear stronger than the reported evidence (e.g., “stable at three months” vs the described interaction and mean patterns), (3) it does not clearly re-engage the constructs from the introduction as a structured test of a theoretical framework, and (4) comparisons to specific prior studies are not sufficiently scaffolded to show what is confirmed/added by the present design (e.g., therapist presence without directly contrasting against the most relevant prior guided vs self-guided evidence beyond citing it).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric strengths: main conclusion is stated; both expected and unexpected findings are addressed; limitations are relevant and concrete (response context bias, generalizability, context-dependent extinction, unmeasured attitudes). Future research recommendations are present. Rubric gaps: (1) The interpretation is not tightly “construct-level” back to a defined theoretical framework from the introduction. (2) Some conclusions are phrased as if stronger equivalence/stability is demonstrated than the reported pattern clearly supports. (3) Comparisons to prior studies are somewhat generic rather than structured as “expectation → result → in line/not in line → why → implication for knowledge.”
lang_style_feedback: Scientific language is generally understandable and the paper is readable, with recognizable sectioning and mostly coherent paragraphing. However, there are noticeable issues: (1) multiple grammar/wording problems and imprecise phrasing (e.g., “x and standard deviations for” table title fragment; some sentences with missing articles; “effect were” etc.), (2) occasional ambiguity (e.g., “comparably outcomes” phrasing), (3) statistical/wording inconsistencies (e.g., normality/sphericity described without the specific details), and (4) APA/reference list issues (e.g., a citation mismatch in the core RCT set, and at least one malformed/inconsistent reference detail). Because these issues affect polish and confidence, the style score is in the developing/adequate range.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric strengths: scientific tone and section structure are mostly present; sentences are generally understandable. Rubric gaps: (1) grammar and phrasing errors occur frequently enough to reduce polish; (2) some statistical/table descriptions are incomplete or malformed; (3) APA consistency and reference formatting contain issues (including at least one incorrect/mismatched citation detail and malformed reference entries). These prevent near-faultless language/style.

intro_feedback: The paper provides a mostly coherent narrative from societal relevance to the research gap and a clear comparative question (self-guided vs therapist-guided VRET), and it contains a stated hypothesis. However, the Introduction under-uses the required theoretical framework/construct-level substantiation: key constructs (e.g., what therapist support is expected to change mechanistically) are mentioned, but the line of reasoning is not consistently grounded in a structured theory with clearly linked constructs/relationships. Several references are used in a somewhat generic way, and some claims are asserted without clear conceptual linkage (e.g., CBT/exposure background is present but not tightly integrated into the specific study’s causal mechanism). The RQ/hypothesis is present, but the “why now / why this design / why these groups” argument remains somewhat implicit rather than fully argued.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric rewards a clear, well-argued storyline culminating in an RQ and hypothesis grounded in a coherent theoretical framework. Strengths that meet the developing/adequate boundary: societal relevance is present early, definitions of SAD are included, and an explicit comparative hypothesis is stated. To move toward the top bands: (1) build a construct-and-relationships theoretical framework that directly predicts how therapist vs self-guided support should change the outcome trajectory over time; (2) integrate prior research as building blocks with explicit links to the specific design/manipulation (not only as summaries); (3) tighten the causal/mechanistic claims (what exactly is expected to mediate the effect and why); and (4) ensure references are used responsibly by linking each major claim to specific empirical/theoretical support rather than general background statements.
methods_feedback: The Methods section is largely understandable and includes core elements (participants with inclusion/exclusion, random assignment, brief intervention description, instrument used, and a chronological procedure with measurement times). Nonetheless, some replicability-critical details are missing or unclear: the exact VRET protocol parameters (content of exposures, duration/number of exposure tasks per session, progression rules) are not specified; the exact nature and schedule of the “virtual therapist” guidance is described only at a high level; therapist-guided delivery (what the therapist does, how much feedback/how it is standardized) is not operationalized; and the randomization stratification procedure is stated but not described in implementation detail. Data/assumption handling in Results mentions outliers but Methods does not pre-specify rules for handling them. Overall, it reads as feasible for a student-level paper but not fully complete/correct per the rubric’s standard.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric expects a coherent design clearly tied to the stated RQ/hypotheses, with participants/materials/procedures described comprehensibly and completely. This paper reaches adequacy on the macro-structure (participants, materials, procedure, instrument), but it falls short of “complete and correct” because several replicability-critical intervention details are missing or underspecified (exact exposure tasks, progression criteria, what therapist actions include, what the virtual therapist does and when, and standardization procedures). Also, outlier handling mentioned in Results is not pre-specified in Methods, which reduces completeness. Improving completeness would mean operationalizing the independent variable(s) and standardizing/clarifying measurement and intervention implementation (including any exclusion rules and analytic handling plans).
results_feedback: The Results section reports an appropriate analysis plan in principle (mixed-design ANOVA, main effects and interaction, post-hoc comparisons). It includes assumption checks and effect sizes, and it follows a reasonable narrative structure. However, reporting is incomplete/inconsistent with the provided sample template expectations: the table referenced is not properly specified (and the student does not clearly present condition/time means in a usable way in the text), the outlier description is inconsistent with ANOVA handling (kept, but not reflected in reporting of robustness/impact), and there are missing/unclear details (e.g., the exact F for the condition effect uses df that do not match the stated design as written; the post-hoc comparisons are reported but not clearly tied to specific contrasts/time points in a structured way). Additionally, the “baseline comparable” statement is not supported by explicit numerical evidence in the visible Results text.
results_rubric_feedback: For Results, the rubric values correct, complete, coherent reporting in terms of the stated design and procedure, including understandable descriptive tables/figures and appropriate statistical reporting. This paper is partly aligned: it includes the main ANOVA structure, assumption checks, and post-hoc testing. To better meet the top band: ensure tables/figures are complete and clearly match the reported analyses; report degrees of freedom and statistics consistently with the design; and present outcomes in a structured way that directly supports the narrative (including explicit baseline means and the direction/magnitude of effects). If outliers are kept, reporting should clarify how this was handled analytically (e.g., sensitivity analyses or justification).
discussion_feedback: The Discussion contains a clear main interpretation aligned with the hypothesis, compares outcomes over time, and engages with prior research in a relevant way. It also provides a critical reflection with multiple study-specific limitations (response bias due to survey context, generalizability, context dependence/extinction processes, and uncertainty about participants’ barrier perceptions). However, the theoretical tie-in could be stronger: Bandura/self-efficacy and Bouton context-dependence are introduced, but the paper does not explicitly connect these mechanisms to the exact observed pattern (especially the post-treatment vs follow-up convergence) in a construct-level, argument-driven way. Recommendations for future research are present and plausible, but they could more directly address the specific statistical/measurement issues and the outlier handling. Overall, the reasoning is mostly correct and somewhat convincing, but not fully “complete/coherent” at the rubric’s top levels.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric rewards a correct, coherent main conclusion with a relevant and convincingly argued critical reflection. The paper meets adequacy/good by interpreting the findings relative to the hypothesis and providing multiple specific limitations and future recommendations. To move toward excellence: (1) more explicitly connect the theoretical framework constructs to the observed temporal pattern (post vs follow-up) rather than only naming mechanisms; (2) compare findings more tightly to the specific prior studies introduced in the Introduction (including what differs in population/operationalization and why that matters); and (3) make the critical reflection more “convincingly argued” by linking limitations directly to alternative explanations and the specific statistical outcomes reported.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is acceptable and scientific tone is mostly maintained, but there are noticeable weaknesses in precision and structure: several sentences contain grammatical issues, ambiguous phrasing, and occasional redundancy; some punctuation/spacing errors are present; and statistical writing/wording is sometimes imprecise (e.g., unclear/incorrect references to timepoints, and some statements that are not fully substantiated by the reported numbers). APA/referencing is mixed: several formatting inconsistencies in the reference list, and some citations/claims are not cleanly integrated. These issues prevent reaching the “good/excellent” band for language & style.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric focuses on clarity, scientific register, paragraph-level structure, and correctness/consistency of APA style. The paper shows some structure and scientific tone, but it contains frequent minor grammatical/punctuation issues, imprecise phrasing, and some inconsistencies in statistical/APA presentation. To reach the top band: ensure consistent terminology (e.g., timepoints and condition labels), fewer vague statements, cleaner sentence-level grammar, and a near-flawless APA reference and citation format; also keep topical sentences and single-focus paragraphs to strengthen readability.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a mostly coherent line of reasoning from SAD relevance → VRET vs exposure barriers → therapist-guided vs self-guided rationale → research gap (no direct comparison) → research aim and hypothesis. However, the theoretical framework is underdeveloped (e.g., extinction/Pavlovian extinction is asserted but not clearly built into a construct-level prediction for self- vs therapist-guidance), and the argumentation relies on some weak/incorrect links (e.g., “Pavlovian extinction” used but without using constructs to explain expected timing differences; mismatch with cited studies and some vague causal phrasing). The RQ/hypothesis is clear, but several claims are broad or not tightly justified with up-to-date, directly relevant experimental evidence. Overall it is adequate but not yet convincingly “build-up to RQ” quality.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric emphasizes (1) a clear, relevant RQ with a line of argument culminating in scientific relevance/hypothesis, and (2) a coherent use of empirical and theoretical work. Strengthen the “theory substantiation” part: explicitly define constructs and use them to generate the prediction (not just describe exposure/VRET). Ensure the societal relevance and gap are aligned with the population and design, and tighten links between each prior study and your specific addition (what exactly your comparison tests). Avoid vague causal connectors and ensure the cited evidence directly supports the claims being made.
methods_feedback: Methods include the core components (participants, random assignment/stratification, exclusion criteria, materials, LSAS-SR description, intervention structure, and measurement timepoints). The design is coherent with the stated aim/hypotheses (between-subject condition, repeated measures over time). But several important details are missing or unclear for replication (e.g., exact therapist vs virtual-therapist training/protocol; what “progressively becoming more challenging” concretely means; number/content of exposure exercises per session; therapist characteristics and fidelity; whether self-guided VR therapist feedback is standardized; adherence/contact schedule; where participants took surveys at each timepoint is only partially covered). Also, some demographic/attrition reporting is inconsistent with the Results section (e.g., Tables are incomplete). Thus it is around the “complete/somewhat correct but missing one or two important details” band rather than excellent.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric expects a coherent design tightly matched to RQ/hypotheses and a near-complete, correct description of Participants, Materials, and Procedure (including allocation, relevant demographics, intervention protocol details, and measurement timing). For improvement, add concrete replication-level details: therapist/virtual-therapist protocol and fidelity, session content (number/types of scenarios, exposure hierarchy rule), standardization across conditions (including adherence/contact), exact survey administration context at each timepoint, and any handling of outliers/adherence criteria (even if none were excluded).
results_feedback: Results reporting is only partly aligned with the course/sample template. The mixed ANOVA is described with F, p, and effect sizes, and post-hoc comparisons are reported. However, required reporting elements are missing/insufficiently operationalized: there is no clear Table with means/SD per time and condition (the provided Table 1 text is incomplete/placeholder-like), no figure is shown, and the outlier handling decision is asserted without showing what was done (and without sensitivity analysis). The assumption checks are described, but the normality/outlier statements are inconsistent (outliers mentioned both for pre and multiple timepoints) without impact. The overall statistical reasoning is plausible but the reporting completeness/coherence is below the level expected for “mostly correct and complete.”
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric expects correct and sufficiently complete statistical reporting aligned to the stated design and procedure, plus an in-depth awareness of statistical reasoning. Improve completeness by providing a clear descriptive table with means/SD for each condition and timepoint, include the key figure (if required by the template/assignment), consistently report and justify outlier handling (and whether analyses were repeated without outliers), and ensure that all reported post-hoc comparisons match the ANOVA/assumption structure. Keep assumption checks and their relevance to the analysis explicit and consistent.
discussion_feedback: Discussion has a clear main conclusion, revisits expected vs unexpected findings, compares to prior work (VRET effectiveness and self-guided rationale), and includes study-specific limitations with concrete suggestions for future research. The interpretation of the unexpected pattern (self-guided improving beyond post) is plausible (self-efficacy), though it is not strongly grounded in the stated theoretical framework from the introduction (so the theory-to-results linkage is somewhat weaker than it could be). Limitations are relevant (survey context/demand characteristics, generalisability, context-dependence of extinction, missing assessment of participants’ attitudes to therapist presence). References are mostly used responsibly. Overall this meets a “good/adequate” level but not “excellent insight into limitations” because some interpretations remain speculative and theory integration is incomplete.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric values correct/conclusive reasoning and a convincing critical reflection with study-specific limitations and future directions. Continue to explicitly connect results to the introduction’s theoretical constructs (not only to general explanations like “support matters”); make your interpretive claims more directly testable and less speculative where possible. Ensure each limitation is tied to how it might bias the main pattern, and provide future research recommendations that directly follow from the limitations and your unexpected vs expected findings.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable, but there are frequent clarity/quality issues that match a developing-to-adequate level: grammar and word-choice errors (e.g., punctuation spacing, “could potentially be explained due the…”, “beyond post-treatment” phrasing), some unclear or inconsistent phrasing (e.g., “Table 1 Average scores on x…”), and several inconsistent or incorrect details in reporting (e.g., Results table/means missing; in Methods “n=60 recruited” but SD formatting errors; some reference mismatches in the narrative). APA style is not consistently correct (e.g., author/year citation formatting inconsistencies, some journal/article details, and the reference list appears to include non-PTPR-required extras and an odd/duplicated UvA discovery item). Scientific style is not consistently polished, keeping this at the lower-middle band.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric rewards near-faultless scientific language, clear structure (single focus/topical sentences), and minimal APA/layout and vague/ambiguous statements. For improvement: proofread for grammar/typos, standardize terminology (e.g., consistently “self-guided” vs “self guided”; “therapist-guided” vs “therapist guided”), ensure tables/figures are complete and correctly labeled, and ensure APA consistency in in-text citations and reference formatting. Reduce ambiguous phrasing and avoid placeholders or incomplete table text in the submitted results.

intro_feedback: The paper clearly motivates the societal and clinical relevance, defines SAD and provides a plausible rationale for comparing therapist-guided vs self-guided VRET. The research question and hypothesis are stated. Theoretical/causal reasoning is present (CBT→exposure→VRET→barriers→self-guided), and the design is connected to the hypothesis. However, the theoretical framework and the links from the specific studies to the exact hypothesis are somewhat loose (e.g., several claims are asserted without tight integration; some literature used is not clearly the most directly relevant to the specific “therapist effect” mechanism). References are generally relevant and mostly up to date, but there are some inconsistencies (e.g., some cited sources don’t match the required core set for the course, and a few claims are phrased broadly). Overall the line of reasoning is present and mostly coherent, fitting the “Adequate/Good” band.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasized: (1) line of reasoning culminating in RQ + scientific relevance + hypothesis; (2) RQ clarity/appropriateness based on empirical/theoretical work; (3) how convincingly the reasoning is argued; (4) whether references are relevant and up to date. The paper meets the core requirement of a present, mostly coherent argument and a clear hypothesis, with generally relevant references. To reach higher bands, the rubric would require more explicit use of theory as a tested framework (clearer construct definitions tied to mechanisms and the hypothesis) and more precise integration of cited studies as building blocks rather than as parallel evidence.
methods_feedback: Participants, random assignment, inclusion/exclusion criteria, reward, intervention distinction (therapist-guided vs built-in virtual therapist), and outcome measure (LSAS-SR with scoring range) are described. The procedure contains an adequate chronological description (pre, 6 weekly sessions, post, 3-month follow-up) and basic session structure. For replication, some important method details are missing/unclear: e.g., precise therapist role and fidelity/standardization, what the built-in virtual therapist actually does vs therapist (content, timing, feedback type), exposure protocol specifics (what tasks, duration, criteria for progressing), how homework/video is standardized, whether assessor is blinded, and the exact statistical plan/reporting alignment with stated design (e.g., attrition/invalid data are asserted but no exclusion rules for “invalid” are described). Because methods are mostly correct and fairly complete but not fully replicable, it lands in the Adequate/Good range.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasized: study design coherence with RQ/hypotheses; comprehensibility and completeness of Participants, Materials, and Procedures; methods correct; avoiding missing/incorrect details. The paper likely fits “Adequate/Good” because it includes key components (diagnostic method, demographics, randomization, intervention contrast, LSAS-SR details, session schedule, pre/post/follow-up). To score higher, the rubric would require more details for replication and internal validity: therapist/virtual-therapist operationalization, session protocol progression criteria, exposure stimulus specifics, fidelity/standardization, blinding/assessment procedure, and clearly defined exclusion of invalid data.
results_feedback: Statistical analysis is described (mixed ANOVA with condition between-subject and time within-subject) and key omnibus and interaction effects are reported, along with planned post-hoc comparisons and assumption checks. The pattern is interpretable relative to the hypothesis. However, the Results section is incomplete/unsafe in several ways compared to the expected structure: the paper references Table 1 but the table content is garbled/incomplete (missing numeric details and SE formatting issues), baseline comparability is asserted without actual means/SDs, and the outlier handling is described inconsistently (mentions one pre-test and five follow-up outliers but still claims no removed/invalid data and says they decided to keep outliers—without specifying whether robust methods were used). Also, the reporting of the post-hoc results is partly inconsistent (e.g., narrative says self-guided decreased at follow-up then post-hoc p-values suggest non-significance at some points). These issues reduce “correctness and completeness” and statistical reporting confidence, putting it in the Developing/Adequate boundary.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasized: data analysis/reporting correctness and completeness, coherence with stated design/procedure, and demonstration of in-depth statistical knowledge. The analysis approach is appropriate (mixed ANOVA; reporting F, p, effect sizes; post-hocs). To reach higher bands, the rubric would require complete and consistent tables/figures with readable numeric results, careful consistency between narrative and reported p-values, transparent and appropriate handling/reporting of outliers and any exclusion criteria, and sufficient baseline descriptive reporting to support claims like “comparable at baseline.”
discussion_feedback: The discussion provides a main conclusion consistent with the stated hypothesis and summarizes expected vs unexpected patterns (therapist-guided stronger immediately; self-guided improving to convergence at follow-up). It also connects findings to prior literature and offers plausible mechanisms (self-efficacy; therapist support). Critical reflection includes several concrete limitations (response bias due to different post-test context, generalizability, context dependence of extinction, missing data on participants’ attitudes about therapist presence). However, limitations and future directions are sometimes generic and/or somewhat repetitive, and the discussion uses some references (e.g., Koelen et al. as if directly addressing therapist effects in VRET rather than internet/text support) with limited integration. The unexpected finding explanation is plausible but not strongly anchored in the study’s measures (no self-efficacy measure). Reflection is relevant, but not yet “convincingly argued for” at the highest level.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasized: correctness/completeness/coherence of reasoning leading to the main conclusion; critical reflection relevance and convincing argumentation; responsible references; outstanding insight in limitations/future directions. The discussion meets the minimum of connecting results to RQ and providing relevant limitations with future directions. To score higher, it would need tighter construct-level interpretation linked to the theoretical claims and more evidence-based/refuted alternative interpretations, plus limitations framed more explicitly as threats to specific inferences (internal vs external validity) and future directions that follow more directly from the study’s design and measures.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasized: near-faultless scientific language where possible, clear structure (single focus paragraphs with topical sentences), limited grammar/spelling errors, limited APA/layout errors, and few vague/ambiguous statements. The paper is generally understandable and uses scientific language appropriately, but there are several clarity/style issues (wording such as “increases self-efficacy could explain” without measurement; some grammatical errors/typos; inconsistent capitalization/hyphenation; and table/reference layout issues). To reach higher bands, it would need cleaner APA/layout execution, more consistently crisp sentence-level clarity, and fewer ambiguous statements.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a mostly understandable topic and develops towards a clear comparison (self-guided vs therapist-guided VRET) with a stated hypothesis. However, the theoretical line is somewhat mixed: the Pavlovian extinction model is introduced, but the specific mechanism linking that theory to the guided vs self-guided difference is not fully developed and consistently reused. Prior research is summarized, but the “gap” and what exactly the current design adds is stated only relatively late and sometimes implicitly. Also, several citations are outside the expected set of core experimental studies (and some potentially less direct sources are used for key claims), and there are consistency/accuracy issues (e.g., Kampmann year mismatch; several claims are asserted without tight argumentative linking). Overall, reasoning culminating in the RQ/hypothesis is present but only partially convincing.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric emphasizes (a) a coherent line of reasoning culminating in a clearly relevant RQ/hypothesis, (b) scientific relevance supported by existing empirical/theoretical work, and (c) appropriate, up-to-date relevant references. In this rubric, partial credit applies when the RQ/hypothesis is present and reasonably clear, but the argumentation is only somewhat convincing and/or the theory is not consistently used. To score higher, the introduction should more explicitly link constructs/theory to each step of the argument, make the research gap and novelty (what this study adds) more explicit earlier, and ensure that the most central claims are supported by relevant experimental sources and used consistently (including correct bibliographic details).
methods_feedback: Methods are fairly complete in structure (participants, materials, procedure) and include key details like diagnosis method (SCID-5), sample demographics, randomization/stratification by gender, session schedule, and the primary outcome instrument with scoring range and example items. The LSAS-SR description includes reliability/validity reference and scoring guidance, which is good. Main weaknesses: (1) important intervention details are missing/unclear (e.g., what “virtual therapist” does exactly; what therapist involvement consists of; therapist training/competence; what is standardized across groups vs what differs); (2) the procedure contains some design ambiguities (e.g., participants in self-guided condition “take home” VR headset, but all results/therapy dosage assumptions are not fully operationalized); (3) planned analyses are not described in the Methods section, even though Results rely on ANOVA assumptions/outliers. This keeps it from “complete and correct,” but it is not incoherent.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric rewards a coherent design clearly related to the RQ/hypotheses, and “complete and correct” description of participants, materials, procedures, and allocation/standardization details. Partial credit is given when key elements exist but multiple important operational details are missing (e.g., exact differences between conditions beyond broad labels, therapist role specifics, standardization/controls, timing details). For a top score, the text should enable replication: precise operationalization of the guided vs self-guided manipulations, therapist involvement procedures, consent/debrief timing, and clear match between sample/design and later analyses.
results_feedback: Results reporting is partially aligned with the stated design (mixed ANOVA; main effects and interaction reported; post-hoc tests described).
