However, it is incomplete relative to the sample-results expectations: key descriptive statistics (means/SDs) are not clearly presented in a usable table/format (Table 1 lacks the actual content in the text), and the reported ANOVA degrees of freedom are suspicious/inconsistent with N=60 and the described design (e.g., F(2,116) and F(1,58) suggest mismatch in df specification). The outlier handling is described, but not coherently connected to the analysis plan. There is also some confusion in phrasing of time effects and condition effects (e.g., later statements about both conditions decreasing pre-to-follow-up) without clearly matching to the reported post-hocs. Overall, the statistical reasoning is not demonstrated sufficiently and some reporting is not robust/complete.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric requires reporting that is (1) correct and relevant to the stated design/procedure and (2) sufficiently complete, with in-depth statistical reasoning where applicable. It also expects understandable presentation of descriptive statistics (means/SDs by condition and time) and coherent linking of analyses, assumption checks, and post-hoc comparisons to the hypotheses. A mid score corresponds to partial correctness but incomplete/desynchronized reporting (e.g., missing or unusable tables/figures, inconsistencies in df/statements, insufficient justification of outlier handling).
discussion_feedback: Discussion connects results to the RQ and hypothesis and offers interpretations (therapist presence; self-efficacy explanation) plus comparisons to prior work. It includes multiple limitations and concrete future directions, which is a strength. However, the theoretical framework from the introduction (e.g., Pavlovian extinction) is not meaningfully used to interpret the results; instead, the discussion introduces additional constructs (demand characteristics, self-efficacy) without fully tying them back to the earlier theoretical justification. Also, some claims overreach (e.g., “effects were stable” is asserted while the reported statistics/trajectory are not cleanly evidenced in the Results section). The unexpected finding is identified and explained, but the explanation is not clearly tested against the data beyond narrative plausibility. References are generally used responsibly, but the depth of argumentation and integration with the introduced theory is limited.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric rewards (a) correct, complete, coherent reasoning culminating in the main conclusion, (b) a critical reflection that is relevant and convincing, and (c) responsible reference use. To reach higher bands, the discussion should integrate the theoretical framework and prior studies from the introduction into the interpretation (not introduce new major concepts without setup), clearly distinguish expected vs unexpected findings, and provide limitations plus future research that follow directly and plausibly from the study’s actual weaknesses and pattern of results.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric focuses on scientific clarity and structure at component/paragraph/sentence level, limited grammar/spelling/APA errors, and minimal vague/ambiguous statements. Higher bands require near-faultless APA referencing/layout and clearly structured paragraphs with single topical focus and coherent connections. Scores in the middle range correspond to generally understandable scientific language but with recurring issues such as inconsistent wording, minor APA/layout problems, and/or some vague or overextended statements in crucial parts of the text.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a generally clear storyline: societal relevance of SAD and treatment barriers → why exposure/VRET → gap about therapist vs self-guidance → clear aim and a (partly) clear hypothesis. References are mostly relevant for the topic. However, the theoretical substantiation is somewhat generic (e.g., extinction/Pavlovian model is introduced but not clearly tied to specific therapist vs self-guided predictions; CBT description is not strictly necessary for the RQ). The RQ/hypothesis formulation is clear, but the argument about why outcomes should differ specifically at post-treatment vs follow-up is only partly substantiated.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric emphasizes a coherent line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/relevance/hypothesis and using empirical/theoretical work to substantiate it. Strengths: societal relevance and a plausible treatment pathway toward therapist vs self-guided VRET are present; RQ/hypothesis is stated. To score higher on the rubric’s top band, the theoretical substantiation should more explicitly connect constructs (e.g., extinction mechanism, therapeutic support, feedback/self-efficacy) to *specific* expected differences in post-treatment vs follow-up, and transitions should more clearly argue why the gap requires this design (not just that no direct comparison exists).
methods_feedback: Methods are coherent and mostly aligned with the stated RQ (participants with SAD, random assignment to therapist-guided vs self-guided VRET; LSAS-SR pre/post/follow-up). Participants/inclusion/exclusion, demographics, reward, and allocation/randomization are described. Materials include the VR headset/software and the therapist vs virtual therapist manipulation. The LSAS-SR description includes scale range and scoring. Main weaknesses: (1) Procedure is somewhat descriptive but not fully precise/complete about implementation details (e.g., what the “virtual therapist” does exactly, what therapist guidance entails, therapist role standardization, session duration specifics, exact training/home use timing), and (2) the outcomes timing is stated, but details about when exactly the follow-up context matches the post-test context are not fully clear. Still, overall replicability is reasonable for this level, so it lands in Adequate/Good.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects coherent design aligned with RQ/hypotheses and complete/correct description of participants, materials, and procedures. Strengths: clear sample characteristics, inclusion/exclusion, random assignment, VR manipulation, and LSAS-SR scoring basics. To reach the top band, the method should more comprehensively specify the intervention delivery (what the virtual therapist does minute-by-minute; how therapist guidance is standardized; how challenging exposures are operationalized; exact home/at-home timing for the self-guided condition video; and clearer details of survey administration timing/location to ensure comparability).
results_feedback: Results are partially reported correctly: the mixed-design ANOVA, effect direction, and some post-hoc p-values are mentioned. However, the section is incomplete relative to the course sample template and what the rubric expects for complete reporting: a proper descriptive table is not actually presented (the table shown is inconsistent/empty for the main constructs), there is no figure with the plotted pattern, key descriptive statistics (means/SDs) are missing from the text/table in a usable way, and outlier handling is mentioned without a clear, coherent justification tied to the analysis choices. Also, there is a major inconsistency: the text claims normality “met” but simultaneously mentions outliers while also stating assumptions are met; more importantly, the reported degrees of freedom/pattern do not clearly match the stated N in a way that can be verified from the provided information. Because the analysis/reporting completeness is well below “complete and correct,” the results grade falls to Developing/Adequate borderline.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects correct, complete, coherent reporting in terms of the stated design/procedure and demonstration of in-depth statistical reasoning. Strengths: correct general choice of a mixed-design ANOVA and identification of main effects/interaction with post-hoc testing. To reach higher bands, results should include complete descriptives (means/SDs per condition and time), a clear table/figure, correct/consistent reporting of assumption checks, outlier handling justification tied to the analysis plan, and fully coherent statistics that match the reported N and model. The current reporting is too incomplete to confidently evaluate the claims.
discussion_feedback: Discussion generally engages with the main findings, restates the hypothesis outcome, and compares to prior VRET literature. It also offers a plausible explanation for the unexpected follow-up pattern (self-efficacy) and includes several study-specific limitations (measurement context/demand characteristics; self-report only; generalizability; context dependence of extinction; missing direct assessment of attitudes toward therapist presence). However, the theoretical integration is not fully convincing: extinction/context theory is mentioned only briefly, and the self-efficacy explanation is not clearly linked back to the specific theoretical framework used earlier (extinction/Pavlovian model). Also, future directions are mostly practical replication changes, but at least two limitations could be followed by more concrete hypotheses/analytic implications. Overall, it is relevant and partly convincing, but not “convincing”/“outstanding insight.”
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric rewards correct main conclusion reasoning and a relevant, convincing critical reflection, ideally showing outstanding insight into limitations and/or future directions. Strengths: main conclusion aligns with results (as claimed), comparison to prior VRET literature is present, and limitations are specific and relevant (context/demand characteristics; self-report only; generalizability; context dependence; missing assessment of treatment preference). To score higher, the discussion should more strongly connect interpretations back to the specific theoretical framework used in the introduction, and limitations should lead to clearer, testable future hypotheses/analytic adjustments (beyond suggesting “change the survey context”).
lang_style_feedback: Scientific language is mostly understandable, but there are multiple style/clarity and APA/layout issues: frequent grammar errors and awkward phrasing (e.g., “because the research has decided to keep the outliers…”, “Moreover, after three months… showed a further decrease… reaching comparable outcomes seen in the therapist-guided condition”; “Considering that previous research…”), some unclear/fragmented sentences, and some inconsistencies (e.g., reference to Kampmann year in intro; inconsistent condition naming; results/table formatting). APA referencing is generally present in-text and in the reference list, but there are likely formatting inconsistencies (and at least one clear reference formatting oddity). Paragraphing is generally present, but topical single-focus is not always strict.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects near faultless scientific language, clear structure (single-focus paragraphs with topical sentences), minimal grammar/spelling issues, and very clean APA referencing/layout. Strengths: overall readability is adequate and APA references are included. To reach the top band: reduce grammatical errors/awkward phrasing, enforce consistent terminology and condition naming throughout (including consistent years in citations), ensure results tables/figures are correctly formatted, and minimize vague/ambiguous statements and redundancy.

intro_feedback: You provide a clear societal problem and a broad clinical rationale, then narrow down to the treatment comparison (self-guided vs therapist-guided VRET). The research gap and study aim are present and generally coherent, and the hypothesis is stated. However, the theoretical framing/line of reasoning is somewhat muddled: exposure/VR rationale is described, but the transition to the specific hypothesis is not fully substantiated via a tight constructs-and-relationships story (e.g., why and how guided support should affect post vs follow-up is only partially argued). Also, some claims rely on older/less well-aligned references (e.g., one or two pieces look like internet sources or review-like material rather than the intended experimental building blocks), and some prior-study descriptions are a bit inconsistent (e.g., wrong year/lettering in places). Overall, it’s adequate-but-not-fully-convincing argumentation leading to the RQ/hypothesis.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasis is on a coherent line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, using up-to-date relevant empirical/theoretical work. Here the RQ/hypothesis are present and relevance is justified early, but the theoretical framework and construct-to-hypothesis substantiation are only partly coherent/complete. References are sometimes not fully aligned with the intended evidence for substantiation (some appear outdated, non-core to the argument, or not clearly experimental for the role they play).
methods_feedback: Design is coherent with the RQ (two conditions, repeated measures at pre/post/follow-up; random assignment; stratified by gender). Participants/inclusion criteria, allocation, rewards, and key material descriptions are provided. LSAS-SR scoring and basic item/scale details are included. Procedure has a reasonable chronological flow (instructions, sessions, measurement times). What limits it from “Good/Excellent” is missing or thin information needed for full replicability (e.g., specific therapist-guidance differences/standardization across conditions, details of the built-in virtual therapist, how the VR exposure exercises are operationalized/progression standardized, whether diagnoses/SCID-5 were applied by whom and how confidently, and more on session content/timing). Still, it is largely complete and mostly correct for this level.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric focuses on whether design is coherent with RQ/hypotheses and whether participants/materials/procedure are described comprehensibly and correctly. You meet the “adequate/good” bar: participant recruitment/diagnostic basis, allocation, intervention overview, and measurement timing are described. To reach higher bands, the intervention components need more operational detail and standardization information (what the therapist guidance entails, how exposures progress and are standardized, who delivers and with what fidelity).
results_feedback: You report the main analysis (mixed ANOVA) and the key inferential outcomes (main effect of time, condition, and time×condition interaction) and post-hoc patterns. You also mention assumption checking and effect sizes. However, results reporting is incomplete/inconsistent with the expected structure: the table is not fully presented (missing the actual LSAS-SR means/SDs in the displayed Table 1 text), the presence/handling of outliers is asserted but not clearly integrated into the analysis/reporting, and some degrees of freedom don’t perfectly match the design/context (and the follow-up statements in text aren’t fully aligned with the displayed descriptive table). The Bonferroni wording is inconsistent (you say Bonferroni but only describe its results without clear correction details). Because the core ANOVA pattern is interpretable, the score lands in “Adequate/Developing” but not “Good”.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric checks correctness/completeness/coherence of data analysis and reporting, and in-depth knowledge of statistical reasoning. Your main ANOVA approach and the headline effects match the design, and you report effect sizes and assumption checks. Lowering factors are incomplete/messy reporting (tables/figures not fully readable as presented, unclear outlier handling integration, some inconsistencies in post-hoc correction/df/interpretive statements).
discussion_feedback: The discussion addresses the main conclusion and compares patterns to prior VR/VRET work, and it attempts explanations for expected and unexpected findings (self-efficacy; therapist presence; response bias at immediate post). You also provide multiple study-specific limitations (context of post-treatment measurement; reliance on self-report; generalizability to non-students; context dependence of extinction; missing data about participant attitudes toward therapist presence) and offer concrete future research suggestions (standardize measurement context/time; use more diverse/clinical sample; manipulate context; recruit individuals for whom therapist presence is a barrier). However, the link back to the theoretical framework introduced in the introduction is not fully tight (the extinction/self-efficacy/context ideas are mentioned, but not systematically tied to constructs developed earlier). Also, the writing sometimes over-claims (e.g., “effects were quite stable” without numerical support) and there are a few grammar/clarity issues. Overall: relevant and reasonably convincing, but not outstanding in theoretical integration and precision.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric rewards correct/complete main-conclusion reasoning, responsible reference use, and a critical reflection that is relevant and convincing, ideally with strong theoretical links plus study-specific limitations and concrete future directions. Your discussion generally meets the relevant/convincing level with multiple limitations and future ideas, but theoretical integration with the introduction’s constructs is not fully systematic, and some interpretive precision could be improved.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric emphasizes near-faultless scientific language, clear structure (single focus/topical sentences), few grammar/APA/layout errors, and absence of vague/ambiguous crucial statements. The text is generally understandable and structured, but there are noticeable issues in clarity/precision (occasional vague phrasing and grammar problems), and the APA/statistical presentation is not fully polished (which prevents a top-band score).

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a clear societal problem, relevant disorder definition, and a reasonably coherent theoretical rationale culminating in a study aim and three hypotheses. Emotional Processing Theory and the Inhibitory Learning Model are both introduced, and the argument that therapist guidance may change exposure mechanisms is plausible. However, the theoretical framework is not tightly integrated into the specific RQ/hypothesis for guided vs self-guided delivery (e.g., expectancy violation/corrective feedback are mentioned, but the logic linking guidance to specific mechanisms is still somewhat general). Several claims rely on citations that are not all clearly connected to the exact argument step (and some citations are review/secondary or not obviously aligned with the narrow point being made). The “scientific relevance / gap” is present, but the argument about the novelty of the exact design (guided vs self-guided plus waitlist) is less sharply formulated than it could be, and the expected waitlist change is asserted more than justified.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric focuses on: (1) a clear line of reasoning culminating in RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis; (2) correct use of empirical/theoretical work; (3) responsible referencing; (4) clarity of RQ and relevance. For a higher band, the paper would need tighter causal/construct linkage from theory to the specific guidance manipulation (mechanisms should be explicitly predicted and later recoverable in Discussion), and the “gap” should be stated with sharper, more design-specific justification. Also, references should be consistently aligned to the exact claim (minimize broad or loosely connected citations).
methods_feedback: The method is mostly complete: participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria (including VR safety-related exclusions), random assignment, and core materials/intervention descriptions are provided. The overall design (3 arms, pre-post, LSAS-SR) is clear. However, some required replication details are missing or underspecified for this course level: group size per condition is not stated (90 total is inconsistent with the paper’s later implication of equal n=30, and the analytic plan is not described), the allocation concealment/randomization procedure is described only briefly, and compensation/reward is not mentioned. In Materials, the intervention description for self-guided includes “text- and audio-based instructions within the virtual environment” but does not specify what participants did without the clinician (e.g., adherence monitoring, how progression was enforced, coping/corrective feedback). In Procedure, there is no clear description of what happens during the baseline and post assessment beyond “completed questionnaires,” and there is no mention of clinician training/checks, adherence/fidelity measures, or handling of withdrawals/non-compliance. For the analyses, only a two-way mixed ANOVA is mentioned; no effect sizes, no multiple-comparison logic for the post-hoc structure beyond “Holm corrected,” and some assumption-reporting is incomplete (e.g., homogeneity line is malformed: “p >0,05”).
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric focuses on coherence to RQ/hypotheses and completeness/correctness of Participants, Materials, Procedures, and analyses reporting. To move upward, the paper should (a) state allocation sizes per arm consistently; (b) include essential replication details (rewards/compensation, clinician training/fidelity, adherence monitoring, what participants do moment-to-moment in self-guided vs guided); (c) clearly specify assessment schedule (and if no follow-up exists, ensure consistency with all mentions); (d) provide a complete analysis plan (including effect sizes and correct assumption checks/reporting); and (e) avoid malformed or incorrect assumption reporting statements.
results_feedback: Reporting is generally understandable and includes basic elements: sample size usage, baseline demographic note, a factorial mixed ANOVA with F and p values, a descriptive table, and post-hoc comparisons. However, key elements expected in the sample-results style are missing or under-specified: there is no clear reporting of descriptive means/SDs in Table 2 (it’s implied but not actually present in text), the planned mixed design is inconsistent with the stated figure/table references (no follow-up timepoint though earlier text says “post-treatment and follow-up assessments” in Results), the homogeneity of variances assumption is incorrectly/oddly reported, and the post-hoc section is confusing (e.g., it states all conditions decreased significantly pre to post, but does not provide corresponding within-condition test statistics). Effect sizes (partial eta-squared, Cohen’s d, etc.) are absent. The Results also mention Figure 1 without providing the plotted pattern or values.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric focuses on correctness and completeness of data analysis and reporting, and relevance to stated design/procedure. For a higher band, the results should provide complete descriptive statistics (means/SDs) in tables, match the described timepoints/design throughout the section, report effect sizes (or enough to judge practical relevance), ensure assumption checks are correctly and clearly stated, and provide post-hoc/within-condition test reporting in a consistent manner (with the statistics that justify claims like “all conditions decreased significantly”).
discussion_feedback: The discussion provides a clear main takeaway (therapist-guided > self-guided and waitlist; self-guided not better than waitlist) and compares the key unexpected result to prior work. Limitations are present and include short-term outcomes, reliance on self-report, and cultural mismatch of VR scenarios—this is relevant and improves scientific reasoning. Theoretical interpretation is somewhat present (corrective feedback/expectancy violation), but it is not strongly tied to the specific EPT/Inhibitory Learning constructs and the guidance manipulation in a mechanistic way (it remains somewhat generic). Some arguments are speculative and presented as “most parsimonious interpretation” without direct data support (e.g., that self-guided participants chose less challenging intensities). The section “Unexpected results” is long, but there is limited quantitative integration (e.g., how these mean changes relate to the non-significant condition effect and what statistical comparisons specifically support the claims). Several future research suggestions are thoughtful, but some suggestions introduce methods/terms that are not well grounded in the current study design (e.g., “stochastic frontier estimation” for response shift bias) and some references are not clearly justified. Overall, the critical reflection is relevant, but the strength of argumentation is mixed and the theoretical linkage could be more rigorous.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric focuses on: (1) correct/complete reasoning culminating in the main conclusion; (2) relevant and convincing critical reflection; (3) responsible use of references. To reach a higher band, interpretations should be more firmly connected to the theory constructs introduced in the Introduction and to the actual statistical findings (including how/why guidance could produce the observed interaction patterns). Unexpected findings should be treated carefully with explanations that are traceable to measured variables or plausible mechanisms supported by the literature. Limitations should be paired with concrete, design-linked future research that does not overreach beyond what the study can support.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is adequate, with many sections structured and readable. However, there are several issues that lower the grade: frequent grammatical/style problems (e.g., awkward sentences and phrasing such as “m=-43.33 was a lot larger,” “Homogeneity of variances also met (p >0,05)” formatting), inconsistent punctuation/spacing, and some redundancies. APA/statistical formatting is inconsistent: for example, “χ² (4, N=90)” is unclear because no contingency df context is explained, and the reported ANOVA degrees of freedom are not checked for consistency. The biggest clarity issue is that Results claim follow-up/timepoints but the analysis is only pre-post, and references to Figures/Tables appear without corresponding information. Reference list formatting is mostly acceptable but contains some inconsistencies (e.g., capitalization, stray spacing, occasional citation details varying).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric focuses on: scientific clarity, structured paragraphs with single focus/topical sentences, low grammatical/spelling errors, and clean APA/statistical formatting. To improve, the paper needs more consistent sentence structure, fewer awkward/ambiguous claims, consistent statistical reporting format (df, p formatting, assumption statements), and consistency across sections (timepoints, tables/figures). APA reference formatting should be checked for uniformity, and key statistical interpretations should be written with precise, replicable wording.

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a clear topic, definitions of SAD and several relevant concepts (EPT, inhibitory learning), and it ends with an explicit aim and hypotheses. However, the required hourglass-style “culmination” into a well-argued RQ/hypothesis is weakened by (a) missing explicit operational link between the theoretical mechanisms and the specific guidance manipulation (what exactly in guidance changes the exposure learning mechanism), (b) limited synthesis of the required core exposure-VR RCTs into one coherent storyline about guidance (therapist-guided vs self-guided), and (c) some argumentative leaps (e.g., sudden gains and regression arguments are introduced later but not integrated into the design rationale in the intro). References are generally used responsibly, but there are inconsistencies (e.g., scope shifts to other disorders/guidance literature without clearly connecting back to the VR guidance RQ). Overall this is “adequate” rather than “good”: the line of reasoning is mostly present and correct, but not convincingly argued to the level expected at bachelor thesis-level.
intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction rubric requires (1) a correct, coherent, and convincingly argued line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ/hypotheses, and (2) responsible use of references tied to the argument. The current rubric-adequacy is met because SAD is defined, prevalence and treatment gap are discussed, and EPT/inhibitory learning are presented. What prevents a higher level is the weaker integration: the theoretical mechanisms and prior VR guidance literature are not synthesized into a tight, construct-level prediction about how guidance changes learning processes; transitions and argumentative links sometimes remain implicit. Also, core-study synthesis could be more explicitly used as building blocks to justify why therapist-guided should outperform self-guided for this exact design/population.
methods_feedback: The methods section includes most core elements: recruitment/assignment, sample age range and mean/SD, inclusion/exclusion criteria tied to SAD and VR safety, a clear primary outcome measure (LSAS-SR) with scoring description, a description of the VR hardware/content and number of scenarios, an intervention schedule (12 sessions over 6 weeks; supervised vs unsupervised), randomization procedure, and a basic assessment timing (T1/T2). However, key replication details are missing or under-specified: participant counts per condition are not given (only N=90 overall), allocation ratio is unclear, rewards/compensation are omitted, clinician involvement is described vaguely (e.g., how monitoring/feedback is standardized, session checklists, fidelity), self-guided instruction “within the virtual environment” is not described sufficiently (e.g., how adherence/progress is ensured, troubleshooting, whether pacing is identical), and statistical analysis details are incomplete/unclear (no follow-up period despite the appendix claiming pre/post and boxplots; assumptions reporting has a formatting/typo issue “p>0,05”; no plan for handling missing data; no effect sizes reported). These gaps keep it around developing/adequate.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects a coherent design clearly related to the RQ/hypotheses and complete/correct description of participants, materials, and procedures. This paper is close (inclusion/exclusion, intervention schedule, VR content, LSAS-SR scoring, ethics/randomization, T1/T2 are present). It falls short of the higher rubric levels due to missing replication-critical details: sample allocation per group, compensation, standardized clinician feedback procedures/fidelity, adherence monitoring in the self-guided condition, and incomplete/unclear statistical plan details and follow-up handling. These omissions reduce the 'complete and correct' standard.
results_feedback: Results reporting is only partly aligned with the expected structure. It states the design (3×2 mixed ANOVA), reports some assumption checks, reports the main effects/interaction, and provides post-hoc comparisons in prose. Yet it is incomplete compared to the sample-results expectations: (1) no Table 2 values are provided; only the figure is referenced, but the figure is not numerically/visually specified; (2) the post-hoc section reports multiple p-values without corresponding test statistics/df or a clear description of which comparisons were Holm-corrected across which family; (3) no effect sizes are reported (e.g., partial eta-squared), which limits interpretation; (4) the paper claims follow-up/boxplots in the abstract/appendix language, but the results only analyze pre vs post, so the analysis plan and reporting are inconsistent; (5) a boxplot statement mentions 'post-treatment and follow-up assessments', but no follow-up analysis is shown. Therefore it lands around “developing/adequate”, closer to developing for correctness/completeness.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric requires correct and complete reporting of data analysis relevant to the stated design. The paper includes a basic 3×2 mixed ANOVA description and some p-values, but it is not complete: missing descriptive table values, missing effect sizes, unclear correction family for post-hocs, and inconsistencies between 'follow-up' mentions and the actual analyzed timepoints. The reporting is therefore only 'mostly correct' but not 'complete' at the expected level.
discussion_feedback: The discussion connects the findings to the research aim and provides a main conclusion consistent with the results (therapist-guided > self-guided and waitlist; self-guided ≈ waitlist). It also includes a critical reflection with several limitations (short-term only; response bias; single measure; cultural tailoring of scenarios) and offers future research directions. However, there are major argumentation issues: (1) some interpretations are not tightly constrained by the obtained statistics (e.g., 'large waitlist reduction' is discussed, but the results section does not provide the needed numerical baseline/post means in the results text/tables); (2) several claims introduce constructs/mechanisms not clearly tested or operationalized (e.g., sudden gains explanation assumes the presence of symptom trajectories without follow-up and without specifying criteria to identify sudden gains); (3) the theoretical section (EPT/inhibitory learning) is only partially used in the discussion—mechanisms are invoked generally (“corrective feedback and expectancy violation”) but not clearly mapped to the observed pattern; (4) limitations are somewhat generic and some future suggestions are overly technical (e.g., stochastic frontier estimation for response shift bias) without clear justification for feasibility in the design. Overall: the critical reflection exists and is somewhat relevant, but the depth and coherence are not fully convincing, placing it at “developing/adequate”.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric rewards correct main conclusion reasoning and relevant, convincingly argued critical reflection. The discussion has the correct general conclusion and contains limitations/future work. However, convincing argumentation is reduced because some explanations (e.g., sudden gains) are not justified by the available data/trajectory design, and theoretical mechanisms are not consistently and explicitly tied back to results and constructs from the introduction. Also, several interpretations appear to rely on numerical claims that are not transparently supported by complete results reporting.
lang_style_feedback: Overall language is readable and mostly scientific, with paragraphs that generally have a single focus (especially in Methods and the initial Discussion). There are, however, noticeable issues: typos/formatting errors (e.g., 'm=-' values without consistent reporting; Results has an assumption line with p>0,05 and broken text), inconsistent or incorrect statistical reporting style (commas/spacing, missing dfs in some parts, no effect sizes), and some vague statements or confusing phrasing (e.g., 'follow-up assessments' referenced without analysis). APA reference list formatting is mostly acceptable, but the manuscript includes several small inconsistencies and presentation problems that prevent a “good” score for style/APA cleanliness. Still, it is not severely unreadable, hence around adequate.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects near-faultless scientific language, clear paragraph/sentence structure, minimal APA/style errors, and avoidance of vague/ambiguous statements. The paper is largely readable with generally structured paragraphs, but it has noticeable formatting/typo issues and some statistical/reporting clarity problems (which count as style/clarity under this criterion). Improving consistency in numbers, removing broken text, and ensuring every statistical claim is supported by fully reported results would raise the score.

intro_feedback: The introduction shows a generally clear line of reasoning: societal/clinical relevance → exposure rationale and VR/VRET → theories (EPT and inhibitory learning) → guided vs self-guided gap → RQ, design, and hypotheses. The RQ and hypothesis are present and relevant, and references are mostly used responsibly. However, the theoretical framework is not fully used to generate very specific, mechanism-level expectations for guided vs self-guided VRET (beyond a general “guidance helps adherence/feedback” idea), and transitions sometimes blur causal logic (e.g., moving between EPT/inhibitory learning and guidance without explicitly stating the construct-level pathway). There are also some inconsistencies in claims (e.g., later waitlist magnitude interpreted as sudden gains/regression without setting up measurable moderators/assumptions in the intro).
intro_rubric_feedback: Strengths: The introduction provides clear societal/clinical relevance early, defines SAD, introduces exposure/VRET and major limitations, and culminates in an explicit RQ, study aim, and hypotheses. The references are generally appropriate for key claims. Weaknesses relative to the rubric: the theoretical framework is not consistently leveraged to generate construct-level, mechanism-specific expectations for the guidance manipulation; the causal storyline between theory → guidance mechanism → predicted pattern is sometimes implied rather than convincingly argued. Also, some claims/assumptions later used (e.g., sudden gains) are not sufficiently prepared in the introduction to make the later reasoning maximally coherent.
methods_feedback: Participants and core eligibility criteria are described clearly, and random assignment to three conditions is stated. Materials include the key outcome measure (LSAS-SR) and a reasonable description of the VR hardware/content and the broad difference between therapist-guided and self-guided. Procedure gives a basic timeline (consent, baseline, 12 sessions over 6 weeks, pre/post assessments). But several method details required for a replicable study are missing or insufficiently specified at this level: allocation concealment/blinding is not addressed; key dependent-variable administration details (where/when/how the LSAS-SR is completed, scoring/decision rules for “SAD threshold” vs inclusion threshold) are unclear; the clinician monitoring/standardization (what rating scale, how decisions are made, fidelity/competence checks) is described in broad terms; and the statistical plan is incomplete (e.g., mixed ANOVA rationale stated, but correction procedures, effect sizes, and handling of missing data/dropout are not described).
methods_rubric_feedback: Strengths: Participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria, outcome measure, and broad intervention structure (number and duration of sessions; clinician vs no clinician; identical content/schedule claim) are present. Core randomization statement exists. Weaknesses relative to the rubric: methods are not yet “complete and correct” for replicability: missing key implementation standardization details (clinician monitoring instruments, adherence/fidelity procedures, how intensity was tailored in guided condition), incomplete outcome administration/scoring thresholds clarity, incomplete statistical analysis plan reporting (effect sizes, missing data handling, and consistent use of the stated design—especially the mismatch between mentioning follow-up in results and only reporting pre/post in results).
results_feedback: Results include the main structure (descriptives table, assumption checks, mixed ANOVA, main effect of time, non-significant main effect of condition, significant interaction, and post-hoc comparisons). This is mostly aligned with the stated design and is generally understandable. However, reporting is incomplete relative to the course expectations: the paper mentions “boxplots… and follow-up” in the first sentence but only provides pre vs post (and there is no follow-up analysis/table described in results). The results do not report effect sizes (e.g., partial eta squared) or confidence intervals, and the homogeneity statement is malformed/unclear (“p >0,05” without an exact statistic, and normality phrasing lacks group-wise details but claims all p>.05). The tables/figures are not actually provided numerically for the ANOVA inputs (Table 2/Figure 1 are placeholders) and the post-hoc test reporting is mostly p-values without which comparisons are adjusted beyond “Holm corrected” (and without clear degrees of freedom consistency). This limits scientific reasoning at the results-interpretation interface.
results_rubric_feedback: Strengths: Uses an analysis consistent with a 3 (condition) × 2 (time) design (mixed ANOVA), reports assumption checks, and reports the key interaction and planned post-hoc logic. Weaknesses relative to the rubric: reporting is not fully complete or clear enough—no effect sizes, no clear test statistic/df consistency and malformed homogeneity reporting, and an internal inconsistency where the text implies follow-up but only pre/post results are shown. Additionally, the tables/figures are placeholders rather than complete quantitative outputs, which undermines “correct, complete, coherent” reporting at the required student level.
discussion_feedback: The discussion starts by reiterating the aim and main conclusion consistent with the results pattern (therapist-guided > self-guided and waitlist; self-guided not better than waitlist). It includes comparison to previous RCTs and proposes plausible explanations for the unexpected self-guided null effect (adherence/intensity/feedback). The discussion also attempts to interpret the large waitlist reduction via regression-to-the-mean and sudden gains, and it offers limitations and future directions. However, the integration with the theories from the introduction is partial: EPT/inhibitory learning are mentioned only indirectly, and the proposed mechanisms for “guidance” are not explicitly mapped onto expectancy violation or corrective information incorporation in the same construct-level way. Critical reflection is somewhat relevant but would need more specificity (e.g., whether sudden gains claim is supported by session-level data or prior probability; whether assessment schedule/measurement error could explain large waitlist change; fidelity/adherence measures are not discussed as limitations). Some future research suggestions are quite speculative or methodologically disconnected from the present design (e.g., stochastic frontier estimation for response-shift bias) without explaining feasibility within this study context.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Strengths: Correctly answers the RQ at a high level, compares to prior research, and provides a critical reflection section with multiple limitations and future research ideas. Weaknesses relative to the rubric: the critical reflection is not convincingly grounded in study-specific evidence (e.g., no fidelity/adherence or session-level measures discussed when using those explanations), and theoretical implications from EPT/inhibitory learning are underdeveloped. Some future-method suggestions are not sufficiently argued for or connected to what the current study could realistically support.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is moderate, with generally scientific tone and mostly understandable paragraphing. There are noticeable issues affecting scholarly clarity: formatting/typos and inconsistent scientific writing (e.g., “m=-43.33” without specifying how computed; several incomplete or awkward sentences such as 'At post-treatment, there is no significant difference…' but also prior results claim interaction etc.; normality/homogeneity reporting has formatting problems; references include some inconsistencies and apparent citation errors). There are also problematic content-level language issues (e.g., “Strangely” and some informal phrasing, and some very broad theoretical/mechanistic leaps). APA style is imperfect (in-text citations inconsistent, spacing/punctuation issues, and reference list contains at least one clearly suspicious citation mismatch/typo in the results-discussion sections).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Strengths: Generally readable scientific style with clear headings and a recognizable structure. Weaknesses relative to the rubric: there are multiple formatting/grammar issues and several vague or awkward statements that reduce precision. APA/statistical reporting is not near faultless (inconsistent punctuation, incomplete/malformed reporting, and some questionable specificity), and there are some informal rhetorical words in the discussion that reduce academic polish.

intro_feedback: The paper contains a clear topic, useful societal relevance, a reasonably coherent line from SAD/exposure/VRET to the guidance comparison, and explicit hypotheses. However, the theoretical framework and mechanisms are not fully integrated into the final hypotheses and design in a concrete, testable way (EPT/ILM/expectancy violation are introduced, but later the argument shifts more to general “guidance helps adherence/feedback” without re-linking each theory to the specific outcomes and conditions). Also, several references are not used responsibly for the claims they support (e.g., some citations are general reviews/roadmaps or are used as if directly supporting specific quantitative statements), and there is noticeable terminological/causal slippage (e.g., expectancy violation/habituation/extinction are stated, but not clearly mapped to therapist-guided vs self-guided operational differences).
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric emphasizes a line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypotheses. The introduction generally provides a coherent build-up and a clearly stated RQ/hypotheses. To improve toward higher bands, the theoretical framework should be used more explicitly throughout: specify constructs/relationships as they relate to therapist-guided vs self-guided, state what you expect from each mechanism, and ensure transitions consistently justify the exact design choices (population, timing, outcome). Also, references should be used more responsibly: avoid citing broad sources to support narrow, study-specific claims or quantitative comparisons unless directly substantiated.
methods_feedback: Participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria, randomization, basic demographics (age), intervention dosage (12 sessions over 6 weeks), and the primary outcome measure (LSAS-SR) are described. Strengths include reasonable coherence with the RQ and clear differentiation between therapist-guided and self-guided content. Weaknesses relative to the rubric include: insufficient detail for key replicability items (e.g., group sizes per condition, actual gender distribution in the main text vs appendix vs results, compensation, detailed randomization concealment/allocation procedure, therapist training/standardization of guidance, and how adherence/compliance was monitored or handled). Also, the procedure is incomplete (only T1/T2 described; no mention of whether SCID interview was baseline-only; no debriefing; no description of timing of assessments relative to the end of sessions). Still, the section is mostly complete/correct at a student level, hence not low.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric targets coherence between design and stated RQ/hypotheses and requires that participants, materials, and procedures are described comprehensibly, with methods complete and correct at the bachelor level. The paper meets the general structure (recruitment, criteria, randomization, intervention dosage, outcome measure), but would score higher if it included full group allocation details (n per condition), therapist training/standardization specifics, adherence monitoring/handling, compensation, and a more complete chronological procedure (what happens at SCID baseline, whether debriefing exists, timing of T1/T2 relative to sessions).
results_feedback: Core reporting is present (sample N=90, assumptions mentioned, mixed ANOVA, main effects, interaction, descriptive tables/figure placeholders). However, important elements from the course/sample-model are missing or unclear: Table 2 does not actually provide numeric values in the results text, Figure 1 is not quantifiable (no axis/legend values), and there is inconsistent/underspecified statistical reporting (e.g., homogeneity statement has a corrupted p-value “p >0,05” / unclear formatting; effect sizes/df details are limited; “mixed ANOVA” is described as 3 (condition) x 2 (time) but df/margins are not fully coherent). Additionally, post-hoc results are reported without the full pattern needed to justify conclusions (e.g., no clear reporting of whether the interaction is explored with planned contrasts, and “p-values at different time points” are described in a way that is hard to verify). Thus results are only partially aligned with the required completeness/clarity.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric requires that data analysis and reporting are correct, complete, coherent, and relevant. While the intended analysis (3x2 mixed ANOVA) is plausible and key pieces are present (assumptions, main effects, interaction, post-hocs), reporting is not fully complete/verifyable because numerical values in tables/figures are missing or not shown, some assumption/statistical statements are corrupted, and effect sizes are absent. Improving completeness (tables with numbers; complete post-hoc reporting; clear figure details) and statistical reporting would align better with the rubric’s higher bands.
discussion_feedback: The discussion gives a coherent main conclusion and compares therapist-guided vs self-guided vs waitlist. It also provides a substantive attempt to explain unexpected findings (self-guided not outperforming waitlist) using plausible mechanisms (adherence/intensity/coping/feedback) and addresses sudden gains/regression-to-the-mean. Limitations are relevant (short-term, self-report bias, cultural tailoring). It also proposes future directions beyond generic statements. However, the discussion only partially reconnects to the specific theoretical framework laid out in the introduction (EPT/ILM/expectancy violation are mentioned only at a high level, not used systematically to interpret the pattern). Some claims introduce extra-strong interpretations without enough statistical grounding (e.g., describing sudden gains based on only pre/post with no additional timing data to establish “abrupt reversals”). Overall, it fits the “adequate-good” band.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric rewards correct/complete main-conclusion reasoning and relevant, convincingly argued critical reflection. This discussion reaches an appropriate main conclusion and includes relevant limitations and future research. It should improve by more tightly integrating the introduction’s specific theoretical mechanisms into the interpretation (EPT/ILM/expectancy violation should be clearly mapped to the guidance manipulation and the obtained pattern). Additionally, critical reflection should be careful not to over-interpret phenomena like sudden gains without sufficient temporal resolution beyond pre/post.
lang_style_feedback: Readability is generally understandable, but style and APA precision problems are substantial enough to lower the score: some sentences are unpolished or awkward (“In addition, the clinician…”, “At post-treatment, there is no significant difference…”), there are inconsistencies and formatting issues in Results (missing numerical content, corrupted text), and a number of statements are vague/overgeneralized (e.g., large quantitative interpretations without effect sizes). APA referencing is mostly present, but inconsistencies appear (e.g., some citations in the reference list may have formatting issues; missing/incorrect claims supported by cited sources; some typos/spacing). Appendix inclusion is large and not clearly separated from main paper structure.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric focuses on scientific language use, clear paragraphing/topical sentences, and low error rates in grammar/APA. The paper has generally understandable language, but formatting/reporting glitches in Results and some unpolished/ambiguous statements reduce clarity. Striving for near-faultless APA style (especially statistical formatting and consistency across tables/figures), and ensuring every figure/table referenced in the text contains the needed information, would support higher bands.

intro_feedback: Overall, the introduction contains a plausible topic, substantial background, and a clear aim/RQ with testable hypotheses at the end. The line of reasoning is generally coherent and references are mostly used responsibly. However, the paper does not fully meet the course’s argumentation expectations: the theoretical models (EPT, inhibitory learning) are introduced but not clearly and explicitly linked to the specific design comparison (therapist-guided vs self-guided) in a way that the reader can later see “tested” through the results. The hypotheses are stated, but the “scientific relevance/punchline” appears somewhat late and some causality/assumptions are asserted without enough bridging argumentation. Also, several claims rely on non-core or potentially review/meta sources (not necessarily forbidden, but reduces the strength of the empirical scaffolding for the specific RQ).
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria emphasize a correct, coherent, convincingly argued line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis supported by existing empirical/theoretical work. The paper largely satisfies this (topic relevance, RQ clarity, hypotheses stated), but the theoretical constructs are not consistently carried through to the operational design in a way that strengthens argumentative “tests” of the theory. The “why now/why this study” punchline is present but could be sharper earlier, and some empirical scaffolding relies on sources that are not always tightly aligned to the precise design question. References are generally used responsibly, but the argumentation would be stronger with tighter linking of theory → mechanism → guidance manipulation → predicted pattern.
methods_feedback: Method is fairly complete: participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria, randomization, LSAS-SR as dependent variable, intervention descriptions (12 sessions/6 weeks; guidance difference; waitlist), ethics, and basic assessment timing are provided. The design is coherent with the stated RQ and hypotheses. Nonetheless, several replication-relevant details are missing or under-specified: allocation concealment/stratification (if any), number per condition is implied but not explicitly reported in the main text, no attrition/handling of missing data is reported, and important procedure details are absent (e.g., session attendance/adherence, how clinician guidance/self-guided progression differences were standardized beyond “same content,” debriefing, and what “standardized rating scales” were used by the clinician). Materials section includes key technical details, but scoring thresholds (e.g., “30> indicating SAD, and 60> generalized SAD”) are not consistent/clearly grounded and may be unclear for readers. These issues keep it from the top band.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria require a coherent design clearly related to the stated RQ/hypotheses, with participants/materials/procedure described comprehensibly and methods complete and correct. This submission is coherent and mostly complete, but lacks several key replication and reasoning details: explicit per-condition N, missing attrition/missing-data plan, insufficient standardization details for therapist vs self-guided delivery (what exactly is the same/different beyond “support”), clinician procedures/specific scales unclear, no adherence/fidelity metrics, and assessment timing is inconsistent with Results language mentioning “follow-up.” These gaps prevent reaching the “complete and correct” level.
results_feedback: Results reporting is partially aligned with the sample-results style (tables, main ANOVA, post-hoc statement, figure placeholder), and the core analysis (time × condition mixed ANOVA) matches the design. However, required elements and statistical reporting are incomplete for this course level: there is no clear reporting of descriptive means/SDs in Table 2 (it is referenced but not provided in the Results text), no effect sizes (e.g., partial eta squared) or df formatting consistency beyond a few places, and the assumption checks contain unclear phrasing (e.g., “p >0,05” with a likely typo/incorrect statement). Post-hoc results are reported mostly correctly in prose, but consistency issues remain (e.g., use of p values without clearly stating which comparisons were adjusted and where the interaction pattern is summarized). The inclusion of a follow-up is inconsistent with the methods (the study is only T1/T2), and “post-treatment and follow-up assessments” are mentioned though no follow-up exists in the methods/results. Overall, it is readable but not fully complete/consistent with the sample-results expectations.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria expect data analysis/reporting mostly correct, complete, coherent, and relevant, including clear reporting of main outcomes (means/SDs per condition/time), the correct statistical tests linked to the design, and assumption/post-hoc reporting at an appropriate depth. The submission reports the correct mixed ANOVA framework and key p-values, but it is not fully complete: Table 2 content is missing from the Results text (only referenced), effect sizes are absent, some assumption statements contain likely errors/typos, and there is inconsistency about follow-up. Coherence is moderate but not “complete and correct.”
discussion_feedback: Discussion connects the findings to the main research aim and includes some attempt at explanation for an unexpected pattern (self-guided ≈ waitlist) and a critical reflection section with limitations and future research. The comparison to previous RCTs is present, and there is a reasonable practical implication (guidance matters). However, several scientific reasoning elements are weaker than the rubric expects for an “Adequate/Good” discussion: (1) unexpected findings are addressed, but the rationale for sudden gains/regression-to-the-mean is not tightly linked to the actual design and reported statistics/effects (no baseline severity moderation check, no actual evidence that sudden gains occurred, and no follow-up to show reversal); (2) theoretical models introduced in the introduction (EPT/inhibitory learning) are invoked only minimally and not convincingly used to explain the guidance effect pattern; (3) some statements are speculative or leap beyond the data (e.g., strong claims about expectancy violation without showing mechanisms/measures); (4) limitations are numerous but some are methodologically misaligned/overly specific (e.g., “Stochastic frontier estimation” and “response shift bias” are mentioned though no design/software approach is shown). Because of these issues, the discussion is partly convincing but not firmly grounded.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria require correct main conclusion reasoning and relevant, convincingly argued critical reflection on the current study. This discussion reaches an overall direction (guidance helps short-term outcomes; self-guided not better than waitlist) and includes limitations/future research. However, critical reflection is sometimes not convincingly argued: theoretical mechanisms from the intro are not clearly re-applied to interpret the pattern; sudden gains explanations are not supported with the needed evidence/design elements; and some future-method suggestions are not grounded in the actual study implementation. This keeps it from the top bands.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric criteria reward near-faultless scientific language, clear structure (single focus/topic sentences), and minimal APA/grammar/style errors. The text is generally understandable and scientific in tone, but structure and clarity have issues: the introduction/directions include some awkward phrasing and punctuation, “Hypotheses” and theoretical sections are sometimes long/dense, there are typos/inconsistencies (e.g., p-values/assumption statements), and APA/statistical formatting is not consistently polished. There are also unclear or contradictory elements (follow-up mentioned in Results, while methods show only pre/post), which affects comprehension.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a clear topic and a generally appropriate research aim (effect of guidance mode in VRET), and it provides relevant disorder/population background plus theory (EPT, inhibitory learning) and links to exposure mechanisms. However, the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/hypotheses is only partly convincing: (i) several claims are asserted without being tightly connected back to the specific RQ/design (e.g., how EPT/inhibitory learning differentiates therapist- vs self-guidance), (ii) the transition from “gap” to “this study adds” is present but not very precise about what exactly is novel given the included waitlist and the specific age range, and (iii) there are some problems with reference use/consistency (e.g., repeated/incorrect bibliographic details, and a mix of sources that include potentially generic statements or unclear fit). Overall, it’s coherent enough for bachelor level but not yet at the standard of a “convincingly argued” RQ line.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric criteria emphasize a line of reasoning culminating in a clear, relevant RQ/hypothesis supported by an appropriate synthesis of empirical and theoretical work. To score higher, the rubric expects: (1) stronger logical cohesion and explicit linking from theory/mechanisms to why guidance mode should change outcomes in this exact design; (2) clearer pinpointing of the research gap and why this study’s combination of comparisons (guided vs self-guided vs waitlist) and population (young adults) is necessary; (3) high relevance and up-to-date use of references throughout (avoid outdated/irrelevant entries and ensure bibliographic accuracy).
methods_feedback: Method is mostly coherent and aligned with the stated RQ (three arms; inclusion/exclusion; LSAS-SR; 12 sessions over 6 weeks; therapist-guided vs self-guided; waitlist; random allocation sequence; T1/T2 assessments). Still, several important details are missing or insufficiently specified for replication and for assessing internal validity: no description of group sizes per arm in the main Method section (only in appendix), no details on therapist qualifications/training, clinician monitoring/rating scale specifications, how clinician feedback intensity was standardized, how adherence/number of completed sessions was handled, and no clear description of the exact allocation procedure (e.g., block/randomization stratification) or whether assessors were blinded. Also, the materials section has some APA/statistical inconsistencies (e.g., LSAS-SR cutoffs stated as “30>” and “60>” without clear wording; “follow-up” is referenced nowhere in Methods but appears in Results/Appendix template). These gaps keep it in Developing/Adequate rather than Good.
methods_rubric_feedback: Method rubric criteria reward coherence and alignment with the RQ/hypotheses plus complete, correct, comprehensible reporting of participants, materials, procedures, and analyses. Higher scores require inclusion of key replication details: (1) adequate participant allocation details (group sizes per arm in main text; randomization method); (2) complete intervention description including what is standardized vs individualized, clinician qualifications/training, and feedback/guidance protocol/fidelity for therapist-guided; (3) clear measurement administration and scoring details; (4) study timing and assessment schedule consistent with Results/Appendix; and (5) appropriate reporting of analyses matching the design (and clarity on missing data handling/adherence).
results_feedback: Results reporting is understandable and generally consistent with the design: N=90, mixed ANOVA with time × condition, assumption checks reported, Table 1 and Table 2 referenced, post-hoc testing described, and a figure is included (though the figure itself is not shown in the text). However, it does not fully match the rubric’s “sample-results standard” expectations: the ANOVA degrees of freedom/denominators should be double-checked (e.g., F(1,87) after N=90 with 3 groups is plausible but needs internal consistency with df structure), post-hoc reporting is a bit underspecified (which contrasts were tested and how they were operationalized; Holm correction method details), effect sizes are not reported, and there is confusion around follow-up terminology (the rubric template mentions baseline/post/follow-up, but this study only has pre/post in Methods; your Results/appendix label mentions follow-up-related boxplots). Additionally, the Results section lacks descriptive statistics in Table 2 in the provided text, making the pattern hard to verify from the excerpt. Hence Adequate-to-Good but not excellent.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric criteria focus on correct and complete analysis/reporting, coherence with the stated design/procedure, and demonstration of adequate statistical reasoning. Higher performance requires: (1) reporting that matches the design exactly (timepoints and group structure); (2) clear descriptive statistics (tables/figures) for main outcomes; (3) correct assumption checks and appropriate post-hoc contrasts with correction; (4) inclusion of key statistics (df structure consistency, test statistics) and ideally effect sizes; and (5) coherent narrative that does not introduce analyses or timepoints not present in Methods.
discussion_feedback: Discussion contains a reasonable main conclusion and addresses both expected and unexpected patterns (self-guided not outperforming waitlist; guided outperforming both). It includes some interpretation tied loosely to adherence and guidance, and it attempts to provide theoretical and practical implications. But the argumentation is not yet sufficiently grounded and specific: (i) theoretical mechanisms (EPT/inhibitory learning) are mentioned later but not tightly connected to the actual observed pattern and to why therapist guidance specifically changes expectancy violation/corrective information processes, (ii) the sudden-gains explanation for waitlist improvement is interesting but is introduced without showing that the necessary evidence exists (e.g., no session-level or timing data; “sudden gains often reverse” is asserted), (iii) limitations are numerous and partly generic/repetitive, with some questionable methodological suggestions (e.g., “stochastic frontier estimation” for response shift bias is unlikely to be appropriate/clear at this stage), and (iv) critical reflection lacks at least two clearly study-specific, design-linked limitations that would directly change interpretation (e.g., adherence/compliance data, therapist guidance fidelity, assessor blinding, measurement timing, cultural mismatch concretely affecting validity). The future directions are plausible but sometimes not directly derived from the limitations as operationalized in this specific design. Overall, it’s coherent enough to pass, but not yet convincingly argued for the expected rubric level.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric criteria reward a correct, complete main conclusion tied to the RQ, constructive comparison with prior work, explicit connection to the theoretical framework, and a relevant and convincing critical reflection including study-specific limitations and meaningful future directions. Higher scores require: (1) using the theory introduced in the Introduction rather than adding new theoretical elements late; (2) explaining unexpected results with grounded reasoning and stating what evidence supports each claim; (3) at least two limitations that are clearly specific to the study design and measurement choices; and (4) future directions that follow directly from those limitations/findings.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric criteria reward near-faultless scientific language, clear structure (single focus/topical sentences), minimal grammar/spelling issues, and low redundancy, plus correct/consistent APA referencing and formatting. Higher scores require: (1) consistent terminology (e.g., guidance labels, “pre/post” vs “follow-up”); (2) fewer vague/ambiguous statements and avoiding confusing or overly complex claims that are not clearly operationalized; (3) minimal APA/reference formatting errors (including consistent author/year formatting and correctness of journal/volume info); and (4) strong paragraph-level structure with signposting so the reader can track the argument.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a generally clear topic and constructs (SAD, VRET, guidance) with a coherent rationale leading to the study aim/RQ and hypotheses. Emotional Processing Theory and the Inhibitory Learning Model are introduced, but the line of reasoning does not consistently and explicitly use them to generate/justify the specific guidance comparison (therapist vs self-guided) rather than mostly using general arguments about exposure and accessibility. Several references appear questionable or mismatched (e.g., some cited claims are not clearly anchored to the cited work, and there are also occasional inconsistencies in naming/year expectations). Overall, references are mostly relevant, but the “critical buildup” to the exact RQ/hypotheses is only somewhat convincing rather than very tightly argued and theory-driven.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric emphasizes a line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis that is correct and convincing, plus relevance/up-to-dateness of references. To score higher, the introduction should (a) make the “why this RQ/why this design now” punchline earlier and more explicitly; (b) use the theoretical framework(s) as a mechanism to justify the specific comparison between therapist-guided vs self-guided VRET (not only to justify exposure/VRET generally); and (c) ensure all references used are clearly relevant and up to date for each specific claim.
methods_feedback: The Methods section is partially complete and mostly describes the design and key elements (recruitment, random assignment, age range, diagnostic inclusion/exclusion, key instruments, intervention dose/structure, and basic procedure). However, replication-level details are missing or unclear in important places: (1) number of participants per condition is not stated in the main text; (2) therapist-guided procedures lack specificity about how anxiety was monitored (which scale/timepoints), how progression decisions were made, and what coping strategies were used/standardized; (3) the self-guided condition description is vague regarding what participants saw/heard, adherence monitoring, and how exposure intensity/progression equivalence to therapist-guided was ensured; (4) there is no stated randomization concealment/implementation detail; (5) only T1/T2 assessments are described, yet the Discussion treats this as if longer-term issues could be contrasted. The analysis plan is not described in Methods (no mention of mixed ANOVA factor structure, correction approach, or handling of assumptions/data issues).
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric rewards coherent design aligned with the stated RQ/hypotheses and comprehensive, correct participant/materials/procedure reporting. To score higher, Methods should include all needed allocation details (e.g., n per condition), clearer replication-level intervention steps (especially how therapist-guidance is implemented and standardized; and how self-guided exposure progression/intensity is kept equivalent), and an explicit analysis plan at the level expected for this course (factors tested, corrections, and reporting/handling of assumptions and any missing data/outliers).
results_feedback: Results are generally coherent at a surface level (sample size reported, mixed ANOVA described, significant main effect of time, non-significant condition main effect, significant interaction; post-hoc p-values reported). However, they are incomplete relative to the course’s expected reporting and the study’s theoretical design: (1) only pre vs post is analyzed despite earlier mentions of follow-up/appendix boxplots for follow-up; (2) the description of assumption checks includes some ambiguities (e.g., homogeneity reported but not with the specific test name; the text contains formatting issues like “p >0,05”); (3) the post-hoc reporting is not fully aligned with the reported omnibus interaction approach (which comparisons exactly were tested at which timepoints should be clearer), and effect sizes are absent; (4) the Results section does not demonstrate “in-depth knowledge” (no justification of multiple-comparison strategy beyond “Holm corrected”, no clear presentation of estimated means/SDs across conditions in the text; Figure/Table references are not well integrated). Therefore it lands in the mid/borderline band.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric rewards correct, complete, coherent reporting in terms of the stated design/procedure and demonstrated statistical reasoning. To score higher, Results should match all planned timepoints (e.g., if follow-up exists, report it), present descriptive statistics clearly and consistently (tables/means/SDs aligned with the analyzed model), include essential statistical reporting elements (including effect sizes where expected), and ensure post-hoc comparisons are fully specified relative to the omnibus tests and correction method.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion includes a main conclusion consistent with the results (therapist-guided better than self-guided/waitlist; self-guided not superior to waitlist), and it attempts interpretation for unexpected null self-guided effects and the unexpectedly large waitlist change (sudden gains/regression-to-mean). It also gives theoretical and practical implications and provides multiple limitations and future directions. Still, the argumentation is not consistently grounded in the presented data and design: (1) the Discussion contains several over-confident interpretations without direct evidence (e.g., claims about reduced exposure intensity or failure to persist are speculative); (2) the discussion introduces numerous advanced measurement/analysis claims (e.g., “stochastic frontier estimation” for response-shift bias) that are not well motivated for this study level and are not clearly connected to a realistic methodological plan; (3) some limitation statements are generic or internally inconsistent (e.g., “waitlist improvement typically minimal” but then immediately argues sudden gains—these mechanisms are plausible but not directly tested; also there is no follow-up). Critical reflection is mostly present and somewhat convincing, but not yet at the “excellent” level of targeted, data-driven reasoning.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric rewards correct/main-conclusion reasoning and a critical reflection that is relevant and convincingly argued, ideally linking back to the theoretical framework and prior research. To score higher, the discussion should more directly connect each interpretation to (a) the actual pattern in the data and (b) specific claims from the Introduction theory/literature, while keeping speculative explanations clearly labeled and avoiding over-advanced or poorly justified methodological suggestions.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric rewards near-faultless scientific language, clear paragraph structure with single focus/topical sentences, and minimal grammar/APA/layout errors plus minimal vague statements. To score higher, the paper should improve consistency in statistical formatting (p-value spacing/decimal separators), reduce ambiguous phrasing, and tighten the structure so each paragraph has a clearly indicated single purpose (especially in Results and Discussion).

intro_feedback: You build a generally coherent topic-to-RQ storyline: SAD relevance and treatment need are stated early; exposure mechanisms and VRET are introduced; then you motivate guidance (therapist vs self-guided) and position a gap. The RQ/hypotheses are clearly stated. However, the theoretical framework is not used tightly to generate/structure the specific guidance-by-condition hypotheses; several claims are asserted (e.g., about mechanisms like expectancy violation/corrective feedback) without explicitly deriving distinct predictions from EPT vs I-Learning in the guidance context. Also, some citations appear inaccurate or inconsistent with the course-required core-article set/years (e.g., reference list issues like “Kampmann et al., 2015” while the core article should be 2016), and there are conceptual jumps to adolescent onset even though the sample is 18–30. Overall: sound enough for an Adequate grade, but not fully convincing throughout.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric rewards a clear, convincing line culminating in an RQ with appropriate scientific relevance and hypothesis, backed by relevant, up-to-date empirical/theoretical work. It also expects that the theoretical framework and prior research are used to substantiate the specific hypotheses (i.e., theory-to-prediction link). For improvement, ensure: (1) the societal/scientific gap matches the actual target population/design; (2) each theory you introduce is explicitly used later to justify expected differences between the guidance conditions; (3) claims are not only referenced but logically derived from the cited evidence; (4) references are accurate and up to date—avoid mismatches in core-study details (year/design/population).
methods_feedback: Design is coherent with the RQ (3 conditions, randomized assignment, LSAS-SR pre/post; same exposure duration content described for both guided modes). Participants and inclusion/exclusion criteria are mostly present. Materials include a reasonably detailed LSAS-SR description and VR device/content/sessions. But several key method details are missing or too vague for replication at this stage: group allocation concealment/blinding (if any), compensation, number per condition explicitly (besides N=90 overall), clinician training/competence, how clinician monitoring and progression decisions were operationalized (standardized protocol? fidelity checks?), whether self-guided participants received any minimal oversight/technical help, exact scheduling of assessments beyond T1/T2 (and the paper mentions only pre/post—yet Discussion speaks about follow-up/sudden gains with no follow-up measurements), and the planned vs. executed statistical approach is only partially specified (e.g., no mention of how post-hoc correction was implemented; ANOVA assumptions checks are incomplete given mixed ANOVA context). Hence: mostly adequate but not complete/correct.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric emphasizes a coherent, replicable design tied to the RQ/hypotheses and complete description of participants, materials, and procedures.
