It expects clear operationalization of the independent variable (how therapist guidance differs from self-guidance), participant allocation (including group sizes and any compensation/handling), and a chronological procedure description (T1/T2 timing, order of tasks). For improvement: specify clinician training/protocols and fidelity/monitoring in therapist-guided sessions; clarify what self-guided participants do/receive (instructions, technical support, adherence monitoring); provide the full sample allocation counts; and ensure the analysis plan matches the described design (mixed ANOVA assumptions/terms, post-hoc approach).
results_feedback: The core analysis is appropriate in spirit (factorial mixed ANOVA with time × condition; post-hoc comparisons by time). Reporting is mostly coherent and understandable, with effect of time and the time×condition interaction stated and post-hoc pattern described. Assumption checks are included (normality and homogeneity), and a figure is referenced. However, it departs from the course sample/statistical reporting expectations in important ways: degrees of freedom in the factorial mixed ANOVA are not fully consistent/justified (e.g., uses F(1,87) for time and later indicates a mixed design without explicitly stating the exact error terms), and there is no reporting of F for the interaction itself (it is reported, but not clearly integrated into the degrees of freedom/structure). Also, the Discussion interprets large waitlist change and sudden gains, but results only include pre and post—no follow-up is measured here—so the results section/reporting does not provide the necessary evidence for those specific claims. Thus: around Adequate.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric rewards correct, complete, coherent, and relevant statistical reporting aligned with the stated design/procedure. It expects appropriate reporting of the key tests (main effects/interaction), assumption checks at a sensible level, and a clear descriptive table (means/SDs) and pattern figure that supports the narrative. For improvement: ensure degrees of freedom and model structure are internally consistent for the mixed ANOVA; report interaction statistics and effect sizes where appropriate for clarity; and make sure every interpretation in Discussion can be supported by what is actually reported in Results (e.g., do not discuss sudden gains if only pre/post are measured).
discussion_feedback: You correctly identify the main conclusion (therapist-guided > both other groups; self-guided not better than waitlist) and you compare against prior research. You also attempt to explain unexpected patterns (self-guided not outperforming waitlist; surprisingly large waitlist reduction) and propose mechanisms (adherence/intensity, lack of feedback, regression/sudden gains). The critical reflection is present with multiple limitations, and future directions are provided. However, the discussion contains several reasoning-report mismatches: (1) “sudden gains” is interpreted although only pre/post outcomes are reported; (2) some quantitative mentions (e.g., “m = …”) appear inconsistent with the presented results tables (tables are missing from the provided manuscript excerpt), which undermines trust in the interpretation; (3) several theoretical claims are added in Discussion (developmental self-sufficiency; mechanisms like expectancy violation/corrective feedback) without clearly tying them to the earlier theoretical exposition and without directly testing them. Overall: relevant but not convincingly argued in multiple crucial places.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric values a correct, complete main conclusion, comparison to prior studies, and a critical reflection that is relevant and convincingly argued. It also rewards linking results back to the theoretical framework and providing concrete limitations/future directions tied to the actual design. For improvement: restrict claims to what the results can justify (especially when only short-term pre/post data are collected); more explicitly connect theoretical mechanisms introduced in the Introduction to the pattern of guidance effects; and ensure limitations and future directions are specific and feasible (avoid introducing advanced methods/assumptions without showing how they address the stated limitations in your dataset).
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is acceptable, and the paper is generally structured with clear section headings and paragraphing. Nonetheless, there are frequent APA/precision issues that lower the score: inconsistent citation formatting (commas/spacing, missing spaces like “Ruscio et al.,2008”; “p >0,05”); several references appear inconsistent or potentially incorrect (e.g., “Boeldt et al., 2019” citation formatting; “Kampmann et al.” year mismatch in-text vs reference list); some statistical notation and phrasing are informal/unclear (“see Appendix” without specifying what); and there are several vague/awkward sentences in key parts (e.g., limitations/future research include complex methods terms like “Stochastic frontier estimation” without demonstrating fit and feasibility). Grammar issues and redundancy appear, especially in Discussion. Hence: below-average clarity and style quality.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric focuses on near-faultless scientific language, structured components/paragraphs, minimal grammar/spelling/APA layout errors, and avoidance of vague/ambiguous statements. For improvement: maintain consistent APA formatting (spaces, punctuation, reference year/author accuracy), keep statistical descriptions precise, reduce vague phrasing, and avoid adding unclear or disconnected terminology in crucial parts (e.g., limitations/future research). Ensure that in-text citations and p-value notation are consistently formatted throughout.

intro_feedback: The introduction contains a generally clear topic (SAD), builds a plausible rationale for VRET and for comparing therapist-guided vs self-guided formats, and ends with a clear research aim and hypotheses. However, the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/hypotheses is only partly convincing: key theories (EPT and the Inhibitory Learning Model) are introduced but are not consistently used to derive more specific, testable expectations about guidance mode (e.g., what guidance changes in expectancy violation/corrective information). Several claims are not clearly grounded in the cited evidence (e.g., expected minimal waitlist change is asserted, but the discussion later hinges on multiple competing waitlist explanations). Also, reference choices/recency are mixed (some sources look like modern review/systematic-review items rather than the required experimental core set for that section’s argumentation). Overall, coherence is adequate but not “good” yet for the course level.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric guidance emphasized: a clear, coherent line of reasoning culminating in the RQ/scientific relevance/hypothesis; and using theory plus empirical work to make the RQ/hypothesis convincing. To score higher, the introduction needs (1) tighter linkage from EPT and inhibitory learning to specific expectations about guidance mode (what mechanism changes with therapist involvement), (2) a clearer “punchline” earlier (why this specific comparison, why guidance mode likely matters), and (3) ensuring the cited evidence is directly relevant and up to date for the specific claims being made (especially distinguishing experimental efficacy evidence from reviews/overviews). Also improve logical connectors where the argument shifts from societal need to theoretical mechanism to research design.
methods_feedback: Methods are mostly described clearly: eligibility criteria, random assignment to three conditions, sample size and age range, key measures (LSAS-SR), VR hardware/content (with some scenario detail), and the intervention “dosage” (12 sessions, 30 minutes, 6 weeks, two per week) are provided. However, the Methods section is not fully complete/correct for replication and for the specific course expectations: (1) “Materials” includes scoring information but contains inconsistent threshold statements (e.g., “30> indicating SAD” but LSAS-SR cutoffs are typically interpreted differently; additionally the paper reports both “24 items” and “maximum score 144” without explicitly stating how fear/avoidance are scored per item), (2) the procedure omits important timing details (only pre and post are mentioned; yet the Discussion discusses sudden gains, follow-up is not part of results), (3) clinician guidance details are vague (what exactly is standardized vs personalized; how anxiety is monitored with what scale and how progression rules are implemented), (4) planned/statistical analysis details are under-specified (e.g., no effect sizes, no reporting of which correction(s) were used for multiple timepoint comparisons beyond “Holm corrected post-hoc tests,” and ANOVA df values are given without showing the design table/df rationale).
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric targets a coherent design tied to RQ/hypotheses, and participant/material/procedure completeness so replication is feasible. For higher scores: include all required participant details (including condition sizes in the main Methods text, not only in appendix), clarify intervention components that are standardized vs individualized, specify the clinician monitoring system and progression rules, fix instrument scoring/threshold claims (cutoffs and how max/min scores arise), and ensure procedural timing matches the analysis plan (e.g., if follow-up is mentioned, it must be implemented and analyzed). Also specify analysis plan details (effect sizes, correction strategy, and how the mixed ANOVA is set up).
results_feedback: Results reporting is understandable and generally matches the stated design (3-condition × pre/post mixed ANOVA). The key elements are present: outlier check (brief), descriptive table placeholder, assumption checks (Shapiro-Wilk; homogeneity), main effects and interaction, and post-hoc comparisons with p-values. However, there are notable issues for the course standard: (1) inconsistency with the course sample structure: the paper mentions “post-treatment and follow-up” in the Results intro but only reports pre and post in the analyses; no follow-up results exist, and figures/tables are inconsistent with “follow-up” references. (2) There is no actual Table 2 content (means/SD not shown), no effect sizes, and the figure is not provided (it’s referenced but not actually interpretable here). (3) The post-hoc statements use significant p-values but some comparisons are logically unclear (e.g., whether condition differences were tested at both timepoints using the same model/approach). Statistical reasoning is therefore only adequate.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric expects correct and complete analysis/reporting aligned with the stated design. For a higher band, provide: complete descriptive tables (means/SDs per condition/time), the actual figure/table content, consistent reporting of measurement times (avoid mentioning follow-up if none is analyzed), and more thorough statistical reasoning (effect sizes, and clarity about which comparisons were tested post-hoc). Also ensure p-values and comparison scope match the model and corrections described.
discussion_feedback: The discussion states the aim and a main conclusion that therapist-guided VRET outperformed both self-guided VRET and waitlist at post-treatment, with partial support for hypotheses. It also addresses the “unexpected” finding that self-guided did not outperform waitlist and proposes plausible explanations (adherence/intensity/persistence; feedback absence). It compares with previous research and offers theoretical and practical implications. However, critical reflection is weakened by several problems: (1) the discussion introduces/relies on concepts not firmly set up in the theory section (e.g., developmental “self-sufficiency” and sudden gains/regression are invoked, but the study design does not include follow-up, so some sudden gains discussion is speculative), (2) limitations are lengthy but include some questionable or over-specific future-method suggestions (e.g., stochastic frontier estimation) without clearly justifying suitability for the measurement bias described, and (3) there is no clear, concrete link from EPT/inhibitory learning mechanisms to how guidance mode produced the observed pattern beyond general statements. Still, there are multiple limitations and several future directions, making it adequate rather than developing.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric emphasizes correct main conclusion reasoning and a relevant, convincingly argued critical reflection. For higher scores: explicitly connect results back to the specific theoretical mechanisms from the introduction (EPT/inhibitory learning) and explain how guidance changes those mechanisms; treat unexpected findings carefully without overextending beyond the data (e.g., sudden gains/follow-up claims need the corresponding measurement design); and provide at least two concrete, well-justified limitations plus at least two concrete future directions that directly follow from those limitations/results. Use references responsibly and keep future methods suggestions appropriate to the stated measurement problems.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language/style rubric rewards scientific clarity, consistent structure (single-focus paragraphs), and low error rates in grammar/APA. For a higher band: reduce terminology inconsistencies and formatting issues (spacing in p-values, decimal/comma consistency, capitalization), avoid vague statements, and ensure reference formatting is consistent with APA 7. Ensure every referenced figure/table is actually interpretable and aligns with what was stated in Results (no mismatches like follow-up vs only pre/post).

intro_feedback: The introduction provides a generally coherent storyline: SAD relevance and youth focus → exposure/VRET rationale → mechanisms (EPT, inhibitory learning) → guidance vs self-guidance gap → aim, RQ, hypotheses. The RQ/hypotheses are clearly stated and aligned with the gap. However, the theoretical framework is not fully leveraged: EPT/inhibitory learning are introduced, but later the discussion mostly explains effects via guidance/adherence rather than clearly and directly connecting results to specific theoretical predictions (e.g., expectancy violation/corrective information processes). Several references appear less tightly aligned to the exact claims they support (and some feel out of date/newer alternatives are unclear from the text alone), lowering the “convincing” threshold. Overall, this is adequate rather than good.
intro_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: line of reasoning culminating in RQ, scientific relevance and/or hypothesis. Strengths: problem relevance, population linkage, and a clear pathway to the study aim/RQ/hypotheses. Weaknesses to watch: theoretical framework should be more directly and repeatedly used later in the argument (theory → specific expectations → later results/discussion). Also, ensure all referenced claims are tightly aligned (avoid using references that are only loosely connected) and keep the “punchline” (why this specific design/age group/guidance comparison) strongly explicit and early enough. References should be not only relevant but also clearly current for the exact claims made.
methods_feedback: Methods are mostly present and understandable: participant recruitment/random assignment, inclusion/exclusion criteria, key measures, VR platform/content, therapist- vs self-guided structure, and a basic pre/post assessment schedule. Still, important details are missing or vague relative to what the rubric expects for “complete and correct” methods: (1) group sizes are not stated consistently in the main text (90 total vs 3×30 shown in appendix, but main method lacks per-condition Ns); (2) therapist-guided session details are high-level and do not specify how clinician guidance was standardized (e.g., dosing of coaching, decision rules, therapist fidelity); (3) self-guided adherence/progress tracking is not described (how progress was ensured/monitored); (4) motion sickness safety procedures and debriefing are not mentioned; (5) the analysis plan is not described (no specification of factors/ANOVA structure matching the design, no handling of missing data/dropouts—though none are mentioned). Because of these gaps, it fits the “developing/adequate” range rather than “good/complete”.
methods_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: study design coherence + completeness/correctness of participants, materials, and procedures. Strengths: design is coherent with the RQ; key constructs and main instruments are identified; major inclusion/exclusion criteria are present. To reach “good/excellent”: specify key operational details (group Ns in main text, clinician guidance standardization/fidelity, self-guided progress tracking/adherence procedures, safety/motion sickness management, debriefing), clarify the exact exposure schedule and progression rules, and describe data handling (missing data/dropouts, analysis plan overview).
results_feedback: Results reporting is mostly coherent and aligned with the stated design (3 conditions × pre/post mixed ANOVA). You report main effects, the interaction, post-hoc tests, and provide tables/figure placeholders (Table 2, Figure 1). Assumption checks are mentioned (Shapiro-Wilk, homogeneity), which is good. However: (1) there is an inconsistency in the text/analysis description: the Methods and introduction promise therapist vs self-guided vs waitlist with pre/post only, but in the Results section you mention boxplots for “post-treatment and follow-up assessments” and later only use pre vs post (no follow-up analyses are reported). (2) The post-hoc reporting is not fully consistent/complete: you say “between pre and post decreased significantly in all conditions” but you don’t report the test statistic/adjusted p-values consistently beyond listing p thresholds; (3) no effect sizes are reported, which limits statistical reasoning depth (required by rubric as “in depth knowledge of statistical analyses and reasoning”). Hence, adequate-to-developing.
results_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: correct/complete reporting of data analysis in terms of stated design/procedure and demonstrating in-depth knowledge of statistics. Strengths: the main ANOVA results and interaction are reported; assumption checks are included; post-hoc comparisons are attempted and directionally interpreted. To reach the next band: ensure consistency with the design timeline (no “follow-up” mention if no follow-up analysis exists), report effect sizes (e.g., partial eta-squared, Cohen’s d/standardized contrasts), and present post-hoc results more systematically (including adjusted p-values and test details) with clear linkage to hypotheses.
discussion_feedback: The discussion clearly restates aim and main conclusion, compares findings to prior work, offers explanations for unexpected findings (especially self-guided and waitlist improvements), and includes limitations and future directions. There is some critical reflection (e.g., measurement bias, lack of follow-up, cultural mismatch, etc.). However, theoretical implications are somewhat thin: EPT and inhibitory learning are mentioned earlier, but the discussion later explains mainly via guidance/adherence and speculative developmental arguments rather than linking results back to specific mechanisms (e.g., expectancy violation magnitude, corrective information incorporation) in a structured way. Some future directions are also overly broad or methodologically questionable (e.g., “stochastic frontier estimation” for response-shift bias without clear reasoning in this context). Overall, it reaches adequate rather than good/convincing-in-depth.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: correct main conclusion reasoning and relevant, convincingly argued critical reflection and limitations/future directions. Strengths: main conclusion is coherent with results; unexpected findings are addressed; limitations include multiple aspects. To reach “good”: connect results back more explicitly to the theoretical mechanisms named in the introduction (EPT/inhibitory learning) and ensure future directions are methodologically justified and clearly motivated by the study’s limitations. Also, keep the explanation of effect sizes and waitlist effects grounded in the reported analysis rather than speculative extensions.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally readable, with a mostly scientific tone. Structuring is present (clear headings, paragraphing), but there are several issues affecting the “good” threshold: occasional awkward phrasing and spelling/typography problems (e.g., spacing around references/commas, “m=-43.33” without context, inconsistent symbols like p=.008 vs p = .008 style, some placeholder-like language in figure references). APA referencing is mostly consistent in the reference list, but there are many irregularities (in-text spacing/typos, and at least one apparent formatting/entry issue such as a key publication year/title mismatch risk in the reference list). These problems keep it in the adequate/developing band rather than good/excellent.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Rubric criterion: near-faultless or faultless scientific language, clear structure, minimal errors in grammar/spelling/APA, and few vague/ambiguous statements. Strengths: headings and overall readability are okay. To reach higher bands: maintain consistent statistical/APA formatting (spacing, p-value formatting, italics, dash/en dash conventions), reduce typos and placeholder-like inconsistencies, ensure all figures/tables are referenced correctly, and keep terminology consistent throughout (e.g., “follow-up” vs only pre/post). Minimize vague statements and improve precision in numerical reporting and interpretation.

intro_feedback: No Introduction section was provided, so there is insufficient basis to evaluate the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ, scientific relevance, hypothesis, and responsible use of references.
intro_rubric_feedback: Introduction rubric emphasizes: (1) a clear line of reasoning leading to the RQ, (2) explicit scientific relevance early, (3) correct, coherent hypothesis linked to empirical/theoretical work, and (4) responsible, relevant referencing. To score higher, the Introduction must include these elements in a logically structured storyline (hourglass model), with key constructs and transitions and minimal late/purely implicit “punchline.”
methods_feedback: The Methods contain several required elements (recruitment, inclusion/exclusion criteria, demographics in the table at the end, materials with instrument description and scoring/cut-off, and a substantial procedural description of each condition). However, there are major issues that prevent a high score: (1) core design details are internally inconsistent with the presented analysis (e.g., “three treatment conditions: Waitlist, VRET, and VRET exposure-based CBT homework” but participants are also said to be assigned to “one of the three conditions” without clarifying timing/total study length consistently; also randomization details are minimal), (2) procedural details mix “materials” and “procedure” language (e.g., intervention description repeats across sections), (3) compensations and allocation to conditions are not clearly described as part of assignment in a replicable way, (4) the analysis section in Results mentions assumptions (Levene/Shapiro) without matching the difference-score framing in Results, and (5) there are clarity/redundancy problems and some missing essentials expected at this level (e.g., explicit description of measures administration timing beyond pre/post, and clearer differentiation of waiting-list procedures).
methods_rubric_feedback: Methods rubric emphasizes coherence between design and stated RQ/hypotheses, and completeness/correctness of Participants, Materials, and Procedures. To reach higher levels, the study should be described replicably: clear in-/exclusion criteria with rationale, explicit randomization/allocation procedures, participant compensation details, precise intervention differences (what exactly changes vs stays constant), and clear dependent measure administration/scoring details. Avoid internal inconsistencies (e.g., sample size/df/analysis framing) and ensure procedure vs materials boundaries are respected.
results_feedback: The Results report an overall mixed ANOVA framework and include assumption checks, main effects, an interaction, post-hoc comparisons with Holm correction, a descriptives table, and a figure placeholder. But there are substantial reporting/logical problems relative to the described analysis: (1) The first sentence claims analysis on “180 difference scores” but the design described is a 2x3 repeated-measures structure with pre and post; difference-score wording is inconsistent with the subsequent ANOVA description. (2) The stated ANOVA degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with N=180 and a within-subject factor of 2 (the df reported are unlikely as written). (3) The post-hoc testing description is unclear/incoherent (e.g., conditioning on “time” vs “condition” and listing p-values without the exact comparisons; also an apparent contradiction: interaction is significant but then the text first says “no significant difference between VRET and VRET with homework,” which should be consistent with the post-hoc tests but is not cleanly presented). (4) The Results do not present the means/SDs for pre and post in a coherent table (Table 2 is referenced but not actually shown in the provided text beyond one set of values). (5) Outlier handling is mentioned but not handled analytically (no details on how many, criteria, sensitivity analyses).
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric focuses on whether data analysis and reporting are correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to stated design/procedure. Strong papers provide: accurate test statistics/df, consistent framing with the design (e.g., repeated-measures structure vs difference-score wording), understandable tables/figures for main outcomes, clearly specified post-hoc comparisons, and appropriate reporting of assumption checks/outliers including what was done with them.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion attempts to interpret findings and link to prior studies and theory. However, its scientific reasoning is not yet convincing and contains notable gaps: (1) The main unexpected finding(s) are not handled carefully—e.g., the paper treats “no difference between VRET and VRET+homework” as both expected/partly expected in one place and then “unexpected” or inconsistent in another, without clear alignment to hypotheses. (2) Several interpretations rely on theory claims that were not clearly tested or operationalized (e.g., inhibitory learning transfer, context dependence) without showing how the present design allows that inference (no follow-up, no real-world exposure manipulation details tied to learning transfer). (3) Limitations are included, but they are partly generic and partly speculative; some limitations are also inconsistent with the methods actually reported (e.g., claims about inhibitory learning targeting only behavioral symptoms are not grounded in how homework vs no homework specifically changed mechanisms). (4) There is critical reflection, but not at the depth required: at least two concrete, design-specific limitations and corresponding future directions are partially present, yet the reasoning remains somewhat unstructured and sometimes reverts to high-level statements. (5) The final conclusion overstates “accessible alternative / overcoming barriers” without direct evidence in this study (e.g., no adherence, dropout, or acceptability outcomes).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric emphasizes: (1) correct main conclusion tied to the results, (2) coherent and convincing critical comparison with prior studies, (3) explicit linking of findings back to the theoretical framework, and (4) relevant, well-argued critical reflection with concrete limitations and future research implications. High scores avoid overclaiming beyond the study design (e.g., no acceptability/follow-up outcomes but claiming durability/accessibility).
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is moderate, but there are many clarity and scientific-writing issues: tense/wording shifts, several grammatical problems and typos (e.g., “posttreatment/posttreatment,” “conditioned either on condition or time,” “difference scores” framing), inconsistent terminology (FNE-B vs FNE), and unclear sentence structure in Results and Discussion. APA referencing is mostly present, but there are issues in formatting (e.g., some references look inconsistently formatted; some figure/table captions are incomplete). Scientific style is generally maintained, but argumentative signposting and sentence-level precision need improvement.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric rewards near faultless scientific language, clear component/paragraph structure, topical sentences, minimal vague/ambiguous statements, and correct APA referencing/layout. To improve: use precise statistical and methodological wording (consistent constructs and measurement labels), correct grammar/typos, ensure clear signposting, and maintain consistent terminology and formatting across tables/figures.

intro_feedback: The provided submission contains Methods, Results, and Discussion, but no Introduction (societal relevance, theoretical framework, research question, hypothesis). Without that section, the rubric’s criteria for Introduction cannot be evaluated and correspond to the lowest level.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric requires an Introduction with a clear line of reasoning culminating in a research question, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, supported by responsible empirical/theoretical references. The submission does not include any Introduction content, so the line cannot be assessed.
methods_feedback: Methods include many relevant elements (screening/exclusion criteria, random assignment to three conditions, instrument description with cutoff, VR hardware/materials, and a reasonably detailed intervention protocol including session numbers/duration and homework structure). However, several required aspects are missing or unclear/duplicated: participant allocation after randomization is not fully described (e.g., how waitlist timing was handled), the “three treatment conditions” in the results imply 3 groups but the procedure text also has overlap/inconsistencies (e.g., random distribution to three conditions vs later description of waitlist requirements). Procedure mixes procedural/decision language (some repetition of “as previously stated”), and key method details needed for replicability are incomplete (e.g., therapist qualifications/number, session scheduling/range of time, fidelity/monitoring, exact scoring/scales for other measures are not applicable but questionnaire administration timing is not fully specified beyond pre/post). Still, the design is generally coherent with the stated aims.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric rewards coherence between design and stated RQ/hypotheses, and comprehensive, comprehensible descriptions of participants, materials, and procedures. The submission provides a generally coherent intervention structure and participant screening criteria, but still lacks some replicability-critical details (e.g., allocation logistics for waitlist timing, therapist/fidelity/treatment delivery specifics, scheduling, and some procedural clarity).
results_feedback: Results reporting is partially coherent but has important issues. The analysis description is internally inconsistent: you state a mixed ANOVA with time (2 levels) and condition (3 levels), yet later refer to “180 difference scores” and report F-tests with df that appear inconsistent with N=180 and a 2×3 design. Outlier handling is stated (“two outliers kept”) but no follow-up impact is reported. The post-hoc logic is confused (conditioning on time vs condition) and some p-values are presented redundantly or with formatting errors, including ambiguous statement fragments (e.g., post-hoc comparisons between VRET conditions). Interpretation is plausible, but statistical reporting does not yet reach the rubric’s “mostly correct/nearly complete” level, so the results grade is kept below adequate.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric requires correct, complete, coherent, and relevant analysis/reporting in terms of the stated design. The submission provides key elements (assumption checks, main effects, interaction, post-hoc with correction), but inconsistencies in stated analysis units/df and unclear/fragmented post-hoc reporting reduce correctness completeness.
discussion_feedback: Discussion contains several constructive elements: it summarizes main findings, compares to prior studies, and attempts to connect findings to theory (inhibitory learning/context dependency) and to broader implications for treatment barriers. Critical reflection exists (limitations: no follow-up; VR scenario characteristics; possibility about homework effect; suggests future research). However, several issues prevent reaching a higher band: (1) some statements are theoretically asserted without fully grounding them in the reported data pattern (e.g., VRET predicting no FNE reduction per Kampmann is claimed as “unexpected” but Kampmann is about different operationalizations/symptoms and no direct alignment is demonstrated in the discussion); (2) the “unexpected” finding is discussed as though VRET vs VRET+homework is the key unexpected contrast, but the results section does not clearly and cleanly establish the exact statistical support for each claimed comparison; (3) no effect sizes/CI are discussed, and limitations are somewhat generic in places. References are generally used responsibly, but argument quality is inconsistent.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects reasoning that correctly and coherently leads to the main conclusion, plus relevant, convincing critical reflection. The discussion includes comparison to literature and some limitations/future research, but the link between theory claims and the reported statistical pattern is not fully convincing because the results reporting is unclear/inconsistent.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and written in a mostly scientific register, but there are multiple clarity and style issues: sectioning is inconsistent (“Discussions” heading), there are several grammatical/typo issues (e.g., “posttreatment”, “postassessment”), some sentences are run-on or fragmented in Results (post-hoc section), and some statistical/statements are awkward or unclear. APA referencing in-text is mostly present in Discussion and Methods, and the reference list is extensive; however, there are formatting/consistency problems (e.g., capitalization, journal title casing inconsistencies, odd abbreviations/typography). Structure is present but sometimes undermined by redundancy and missing signposting in Results.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The rubric rewards near faultless scientific language, clear paragraph structure with single focus/topical sentences, and minimal APA/layout errors. The submission is readable overall, but contains noticeable grammar/typos, some unclear/fractured sentences (especially in Results), and some inconsistencies in headings and formatting.

intro_feedback: The paper content provided contains Methods, Results, and Discussion, but no Introduction section. Since the rubric’s Introduction criteria (societal/scientific relevance, theoretical substantiation, clear RQ/hypothesis, responsible references) cannot be evaluated from missing text, this must be graded at the lowest level.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric for Introduction requires: (a) a clear line of reasoning culminating in a specific RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, grounded in empirical/theoretical work; (b) responsible and coherent use of references; (c) alignment between theory/research gap and the proposed design. To meet at least “Adequate,” the RQ and hypothesis must be explicit and logically supported; missing Introduction text prevents evaluation.
methods_feedback: Participants/materials are mostly described at a level that allows a general sense of recruitment and measures (FNE-B, VR headset, general exclusion/inclusion). However, several core Method requirements are missing or incorrect for the claimed design: randomization to three conditions is mentioned but the randomization procedure/allocation concealment is not described; the procedure section includes substantial confusion/duplication (e.g., “Once having gone through screening… assigned randomly” while also repeating SCID/FNE timing and referencing waitlist choice as if they receive a choice during the waitlist). The intervention description is detailed, but timing/duration and what exactly is compared (what “waitlist” entails in practice timewise) is not fully specified. Also, the Methods section is inconsistent about measurement timing (pre/post only) while earlier claims imply a multi-session protocol; details like therapist oversight, therapist training/consistency, adherence to homework/controls, and exact waitlist duration are unclear. These issues fit “Developing/Insufficient” rather than adequate.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric expects a coherent design clearly related to the RQ/hypotheses, plus sufficiently comprehensible Participants, Materials, and Procedure descriptions so replication at the bachelor level is possible. Required elements include allocation/randomization details when relevant, key inclusion/exclusion operationalizations, participant demographics, and clear intervention and outcome-measure administration details. Missing/unclear randomization and incomplete procedural clarity reduce the score toward Developing/Insufficient even if some instrument/intervention details are present.
results_feedback: Results reporting is partially coherent (tables for baseline/pre/post means, stated ANOVA, time/condition effects, some post-hoc testing with Holm correction). However, multiple reporting and statistical-inference problems lower the score: (1) sample description is inconsistent with the text (180 difference scores suggests paired data, yet the stated ANOVA degrees of freedom are unusual/inconsistent with a simple 3-condition × 2-time mixed design); (2) assumption checks are claimed as “not violated” but normality/homoscedasticity tests are not actually reported with the needed statistics; (3) post-hoc strategy is described unclearly (conditioning on time vs condition) and includes contradictory/garbled phrasing; (4) the key claim “no difference between VRET and VRET with homework” is asserted, but the reported post-hoc p-values and the way they are described are not fully consistent/clear for supporting that conclusion; (5) critical outputs are missing or unclear (e.g., effect sizes, exact post-hoc comparisons that establish equivalence/specific non-differences, and alignment between Results and Figure description). This keeps it below adequate on the rubric.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric focuses on whether data analysis and reporting are correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to the design/procedure. For an “Adequate” result section, the expected tests and reporting structure should match the described design, key statistics should be interpretable, assumption checks should be meaningfully reported, and post-hoc logic should clearly support conclusions. Contradictions/unclear reporting and unclear p-value mapping lower the grade.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion contains several components: summary of aim/main finding, some comparison to prior research, and some attempt at explaining an unexpected pattern. However, the critical reflection is not sufficiently well-grounded: limitations are present (scenario characteristics; no follow-up), but they are not fully developed, and some theoretical claims are introduced without a clear connection to what the paper’s theoretical framework/hypotheses would have been (the Introduction is not provided, so this linkage cannot be demonstrated). Also, interpretation overreaches given the statistical reporting issues (e.g., concluding homework is not more effective, despite unclear post-hoc reporting). The study-specific critical reflection and future directions are present but not convincingly argued to the expected “Adequate” level.
discussion_rubric_feedback: For Discussion, the rubric requires correct main conclusion reasoning, plus critical reflection that is relevant and convincingly argued for. It also expects responsible linkage between findings and the theoretical framework/literature introduced earlier, and at least some study-specific limitations and future research that logically follow. Overconfident interpretations or limitations that are not well-justified relative to the presented evidence prevent reaching “Good/Excellent.”
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and mostly scientific, but there are noticeable issues: punctuation/run-on sentences, tense/wording problems, and multiple clarity problems in Results and Discussion (e.g., garbled parenthetical p-values like “p = 0.227; p = 0.561; p = 0.093” without specifying which comparisons; inconsistent spacing; 'Post-hoc analyses… conditioned either on condition or time' described in confusing way; and “Discussions” as a section header). APA issues are also present (e.g., some reference formatting inconsistencies; likely missing italics/stylistic consistency; figure/figure caption mismatch). This matches “Developing/Adequate but not good”.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric rewards scientific clarity, strong paragraphing with single topical focus, near-faultless grammar, minimal APA/layout issues, and no vague/ambiguous statements. Frequent unclear phrasing and garbled statistical reporting reduce readability and argument transparency, preventing a “Good/Excellent” score.

intro_feedback: No Introduction section was provided, so the key rubric components for scientific reasoning, RQ/hypothesis, and argumentation cannot be assessed. With the current information, a proper introduction cannot be credited.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric emphasizes an explicit, clearly argued line of reasoning culminating in a clear RQ and hypothesis, plus alignment with scientific relevance and responsible use of empirical/theoretical work. If no Introduction is provided, the criteria (RQ/hypothesis presence/clarity, coherence of the argument, and evidence substantiation) cannot be met and must be scored at the bottom level.
methods_feedback: Methods are mostly coherent and include several required elements (sample description, inclusion/exclusion criteria, questionnaire used with basic scoring/cut-off, intervention descriptions, and a random assignment statement). However, important methodological details are missing or inconsistent for replication and for judging internal validity: (1) no clear participant allocation description for all outcomes (and some confusion in numbering/timepoints), (2) key design elements are unclear (e.g., whether waitlist gets no treatment for the whole period; treatment duration/number of sessions differs between conditions but the total timeline is not fully reconciled), (3) procedure has redundancy/conflicting statements (e.g., 'fill out after last session' but waitlist also has post-assessment; compensation wording), (4) analyses are not fully aligned with the described design (results section suggests difference scores and two-way mixed ANOVA but methods do not specify the exact analytical model). Overall: adequate but not “complete and correct.”
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric expects a coherent and RQ-aligned design, with participants/materials/procedure described comprehensibly and methods that are complete and correct. The provided content shows several necessary elements (sample criteria, instrument description, intervention outlines, randomization statement), which supports an Adequate–Good band. However, replication-relevant details (allocation handling, timeline consistency, exact procedure endpoints, and full consistency with analytical plan) appear incomplete, preventing a higher grade.
results_feedback: Results reporting contains substantial statistical/reporting issues that prevent a high score. Examples: (1) the paper states “analysis of 180 difference scores,” but then reports an ANOVA with F(df) = (1,177) / (2,177) etc. without consistent degrees of freedom or clarity about how repeated-measures structure was modeled; (2) assumption checks claim normality/homoscedasticity via Shapiro-Wilk/Levene, but the way they are reported does not match the ANOVA structure (and the outlier handling is asserted without reporting how outliers were identified or what impact removing them would have); (3) post-hoc p-value reporting is inconsistent/incorrectly formatted (e.g., 'p = 0.227; p = 0.561; p = 0.093' for one comparison set) and the statement of where comparisons are significant is not fully coherent; (4) effect sizes are not reported; (5) multiple key numerical values are missing (means/SDs are not actually shown in the provided text for Table 2), making interpretation weaker. Therefore, results are incomplete and partly incoherent/inaccurately reported.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric requires that data analysis and reporting are correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to the stated design/procedure. The rubric should penalize inconsistencies between the described analytic approach and the reported ANOVA df structure, unclear handling of difference scores vs repeated-measures factors, incomplete tables/figures, inconsistent post-hoc reporting, and missing effect sizes. These issues place the work below the 'mostly correct' threshold.
discussion_feedback: Discussion connects some results to prior work (Bouchard; Kampmann) and to a theoretical mechanism (FNE, inhibitory learning/context-dependency), but the critical evaluation is not sufficiently grounded in the actual statistical pattern. Main problems: (1) the interpretation claims 'clinically significant reductions' and that outcomes differ as expected for VRET+homework vs VRET, yet the results section does not clearly support those stronger claims; (2) the paper repeatedly states that VRET with homework shows no difference from VRET, but then uses that to argue theoretical implications without careful construct-level mapping; (3) limitations are present (no follow-up; VR scenario characteristics), but they are not tied to specific methodological/statistical weaknesses (e.g., inconsistent analytic model, missing effect sizes, outlier decisions, and possible measurement timing/design mismatch); (4) future research ideas exist, but are somewhat generic rather than directly derived from the demonstrated findings and limitations.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric rewards correct/reasoned interpretation, comparison with prior studies, explicit relation to the theoretical framework, and a relevant, convincingly argued critical reflection (with study-specific limitations and future directions). The rubric should penalize overclaims (e.g., 'clinically significant') that are not clearly supported by the reported statistics, and limitations that do not connect to specific methodological/statistical weaknesses.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable, but there are notable issues affecting academic clarity: some awkward/incorrect phrasing ('analysis of 180 difference scores' without clarity; 'difference scores' mention), inconsistent terminology and formatting ('posttreatment'/'posttreatment'), and several punctuation/parenthesis/typo-like errors in the results narrative. APA/reference formatting is mostly acceptable in entries, but in-text/statistical formatting is not consistently professional. Paragraphing is present but the Results/Discussion sections show some discontinuity (e.g., 'Discussions' heading; missing/unclear Table/Figure details). Overall: adequate readability with multiple errors.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric focuses on scientific clarity, structured paragraphs with topical sentences/single focus, and near-faultless APA/spelling/grammar and minimal vague/ambiguous statements. The provided text includes understandable prose but also multiple statistical/formatting typos and some unclear phrasing that affects precision, so it does not reach the “few errors” band.

intro_feedback: No Introduction section was provided, so the paper cannot be graded against the Introduction rubric criteria (line of reasoning to RQ/hypothesis, scientific relevance, and responsible use of empirical/theoretical work).
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric requires: (1) a clear societal/scientific problem early, linked explicitly to the target population and design; (2) a theoretical framework with constructs and relationships that is used later to justify expectations; (3) a logically coherent line culminating in a clear, relevant RQ and hypotheses; (4) responsible, appropriate use of empirical/theoretical references (no vague claims or unsupported citations). Without an Introduction section, none of these can be verified.
methods_feedback: Study design is broadly coherent for answering whether VRET (with/without CBT-based homework) and a waitlist affect FNE (DVs and conditions are clear). However, key method details are missing or internally inconsistent for a replicable bachelor-level method: (a) randomisation procedure/allocation concealment not described; (b) the statement “assigned randomly to one of the three conditions” appears, but group assignment process is not explained; (c) participant demographics table has only condition group Ns that don’t match the methods section’s 180 total; (d) the outcomes are described with “difference scores” in Results, yet the ANOVA description and df suggest a different structure; (e) the procedure is partly doubled/inconsistent (e.g., waitlist description says participants can choose which treatment they would like to receive, yet no post-waitlist outcome timing is clarified); (f) the Methods use some relevant references but include several non-essential or confusing justifications inside Methods (which is less of a rubric issue than replicability gaps). Overall: adequate partial completeness, but not “complete and correct.”
methods_rubric_feedback: For Methods, the rubric expects: a coherent and RQ-aligned design; participants described with inclusion/exclusion criteria, recruitment/compensation, allocation to conditions; materials must clearly specify instruments (with scoring and range) and the intervention manipulations (what differs/same across conditions); procedure must be chronological without mixing procedural vs materials content; analyses must align with stated design. In this paper, design alignment is partly present, but completeness and correctness are undermined by missing replication-critical details (randomisation procedure; consistency of sample size; outcome timing; clarity of analytical approach).
results_feedback: Results reporting is partially correct in that it states key analysis type, main effects, an interaction, and follow-up comparisons with a multiple-comparison method mention. However, it does not fully meet the expected standard of completeness/accuracy: (a) Table 2 is missing actual means/SDs values in the provided text; (b) the approach to outliers is questionable (“kept in the analyses” because integrity is utmost importance) without a clear analytic rationale or sensitivity analysis; (c) there are multiple inconsistencies/ambiguities: “180 difference scores” while the design appears to be 2 time points (difference-score logic would imply 180 participants → 180 difference scores, but the ANOVA df then must match that structure); (d) post-hoc p-values and the conditioning logic are confusing (e.g., “conditionally either on condition or time” with degrees of freedom inconsistently stated earlier); (e) the results narrative contains typos/formatting errors (e.g., “posttreatment,” “postassessment,” extra parentheses, missing df coherence) which undermines clarity. The statistical reasoning is not clearly demonstrated to the level expected for an Adequate/Good results section.
results_rubric_feedback: Results rubric expects correct and complete statistical analysis and reporting coherent with the stated design/procedure. For an Adequate results section, the reporting should include descriptive statistics (tables/figures), correct test statistics/dfs, assumption checks when relevant, and post-hoc results that are unambiguous and properly matched to hypotheses/design. Here, multiple inconsistencies/omissions (e.g., missing numeric table content, unclear handling of outliers, and confusion about difference-score/ANOVA structure) prevent it from reaching “mostly correct, complete” quality.
discussion_feedback: Discussion engages with prior literature and attempts to interpret expected vs unexpected patterns, but it does not fully and convincingly meet the rubric for coherent, critical, and well-anchored interpretation. Main issues: (a) some claims are not grounded in the presented results (e.g., stating “clinically significant reductions” and interpreting homework as not more effective is plausible, but effect sizes/clinical thresholds are not shown in Results, and the provided Results section does not clearly demonstrate the magnitude); (b) the “unexpected finding” paragraph reverses/contradicts earlier statements about where expectations failed and relies on multiple theoretical claims without properly tying them to the specific results; (c) the critical reflection is incomplete: limitations are mentioned, but not at least two concrete, study-specific limitations that are clearly connected to the design/analysis and result pattern (e.g., lack of follow-up is mentioned, but whether this was a design choice rather than missing measurement is not clearly linked to the Methods; scenario-characteristic limitation is speculative without evidence); (d) future research is present but sometimes repeats limitations rather than deriving precise new predictions from them; (e) the final implications are somewhat assertive without fully acknowledging uncertainty about the strength of the evidence (e.g., no follow-up, possible analytic inconsistencies). Therefore it fits Developing/Adequate-low.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Discussion rubric expects: (1) correct main conclusion tied to the RQ/hypotheses; (2) comparison to prior studies using the introduction’s argumentation; (3) an explicit, grounded link to the theoretical framework; (4) a critical reflection with relevant limitations and convincing reasoning; (5) appropriate, not overconfident, implications. The discussion provides some comparison and limitations, but critical grounding and coherence with the exact results/reporting is not strong enough for higher bands.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable but has frequent formatting/wording problems that affect academic clarity: run-on sentences, typos (“posttreatment,” “postassessment,” “conditioned either on condition or time”), inconsistent capitalization, unclear phrasing, and structural issues (e.g., headings “Discussions” not consistent). APA-style referencing in-text is mostly present, but several references appear in the reference list that are not used in the provided sections (and some sources appear questionable for the exact claims). Statistical reporting formatting is also error-prone (incomplete Table 2 content, inconsistent parentheses and p-value presentation). This places it below the Adequate level for style/APA quality.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language & Style rubric expects near faultless scientific language, clear paragraph structure with topical sentences and single focus, and minimal grammar/APA/layout errors. Adequate writing would maintain clarity and correct statistical/reporting formatting. In this paper, frequent typos, inconsistent headings/formatting, and unclear statistical presentation reduce readability and academic polish, and APA-related issues (formatting and possibly unused references) lower the score.

intro_feedback: No Introduction section is provided, so the grade cannot be assessed against the rubric’s criteria for scientific relevance, research question/hypotheses, and argumentative literature synthesis.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric requires: (1) a clear line of reasoning culminating in a clearly stated, relevant RQ and hypothesis(s), (2) an explicitly argued scientific and societal relevance grounded in the target population and design, and (3) a synthesis of up-to-date empirical and theoretical work (with relevant references). Without an Introduction, these rubric criteria cannot be evaluated.
methods_feedback: Methods are partially described (participants, the core measure FNE-B with a cutoff, VR hardware, and an intervention outline), and the design generally matches an RQ about comparing VRET vs VRET+homework vs waitlist. However, key replication-critical details are missing/unclear: randomization procedure (and allocation concealment), exact waitlist procedure and timing, session scheduling/treatment duration consistency with total number of weeks/sessions for each condition (60 vs 75 mins, but unclear total timeline), therapist qualifications and therapist contact time operationalization, and how homework adherence/compliance is ensured and recorded. Additionally, the participant recruitment statement does not include required demographics (mean age/SD and gender are not given numerically in the text; only implied later in Results), and some wording conflicts/duplication occur (e.g., waiting-list description appears to include choosing treatment “which of the two treatments they would like to receive” while also stating “no new treatment or medications”). Because these omissions affect the coherence and completeness of the method description, Methods fall in the Developing/Adequate range rather than Adequate/Good.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric expects a coherent design linked to the RQ/hypotheses; participants, materials, and procedures must be described comprehensibly and (for an Adequate/Good band) largely complete and correct. Critical elements include recruitment/inclusion-exclusion logic, allocation/rand procedure, clear intervention differentiation (including standardized components), participant demographics (mean age/SD and gender), and clear measurement administration and scoring details. Missing replication-critical operational details lowers the Methods band.
results_feedback: The Results include core elements: descriptive tables, an ANOVA matching the 2×3 design logic (time × condition), and a figure/table reference. However, there are substantial issues: (1) the reported F-statistics are inconsistent with the stated degrees of freedom and design (e.g., interaction reported as F(2,177)=9.78 despite earlier note that the model used 2 measurement times; also baseline assumption statements use tests described but without the required exact p-values); (2) the post-hoc reporting is unclear/possibly incorrect—“conditionally on condition or time” is not standard phrasing, and the pattern of p-values and comparison logic appears inconsistent with the ANOVA and Table 2 (e.g., claiming no differences at pretreatment but also offering multiple sets of p-values without specifying which contrasts); (3) no effect sizes or confidence intervals are reported, which limits proper interpretation at this course level; and (4) participants/outlier handling is described but not aligned with the analysis plan ("two outliers" but no identification/justification, and yet you keep them). Because the statistical reporting is not fully correct/consistent, the grade stays around Developing/Adequate.
results_rubric_feedback: For Results, the rubric expects analysis and reporting to be correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to the stated design. It also expects demonstrating in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses and reasoning. Key requirements at this course level include consistent degrees of freedom, coherent post-hoc contrasts aligned with the ANOVA model, and reporting that matches the design and tables/figures (plus assumption checks). Inconsistencies and unclear post-hoc logic lower the band.
discussion_feedback: The Discussion provides a clear attempt to interpret results, compare to prior studies, and discuss limitations (VR scenario characteristics; lack of follow-up). It also makes an overarching conclusion that both VRET and VRET+homework reduce FNE vs waitlist. However, the interpretation that homework has no added effect is plausible given the results claim, but the Discussion relies on several theoretical claims (e.g., inhibitory learning/generalization/relapse) without explicitly checking whether the data actually address those mechanisms, and it over-extends certain comparisons (e.g., interpreting Kampmann 2016 as “predicts no significant reduction in FNE” without carefully matching outcome measures/contexts). Critical reflection is present but not deeply anchored in the specific limitations of this study’s implemented protocol and analysis choices (e.g., randomization details, adherence/compliance, and the unexplained statistical inconsistencies). There are future directions, but they are somewhat generic (manipulate immersion/stimuli; add follow-up) rather than fully concrete and tied to measured constructs.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric expects the main conclusion to be correct and fully connected to the RQ, with critical reflection relevant and convincingly argued for, and responsible use of references. It also expects coherent links from results to theory and prior research, including explicit handling of unexpected findings and study-specific limitations and future directions.
lang_style_feedback: Language is generally understandable and mostly scientific, but there are several issues: headings are inconsistent (e.g., 'Discussions' instead of 'Discussion'), there is at least one clear structural error in Results (“Discussions” appears inside Results section), several awkward/incorrect scientific phrases (e.g., “180 difference scores” for a 2-time-point design; “conditioned either on condition or time”), mismatched parentheses/typos in statistics (extra parenthesis, punctuation), and some ambiguous statements. APA/reference style is mostly acceptable in the reference list, but in-text usage is inconsistent (some citations appear without clear integration), and the overall reporting has enough clarity problems to prevent an Adequate/Good language score.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric emphasizes scientific language clarity, well-structured components/paragraphs with topical sentences and single-focus paragraphs, and limited errors in grammar/spelling and APA referencing/layout. It also penalizes vague/ambiguous statements and redundancy that harms readability.

intro_feedback: The provided text only contains Methods and Results (and parts of Discussion), but not the Introduction (including societal relevance, theoretical framework, RQ, and hypothesis). Because the rubric’s strongest criteria for scientific reasoning (especially the RQ/hypothesis and argumentation line of reasoning) cannot be assessed, the Introduction grade must be very low.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric assesses the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis; coherence of theoretical and empirical work; and relevance/up-to-dateness of references. To score well, the Introduction needs (a) early societal relevance linked to the target population/design, (b) a theoretical framework with clear constructs and logic connecting to the hypothesis, (c) explicit, appropriate RQ and hypothesis statements, and (d) a coherent synthesis of at least 4 experimental references that builds toward the RQ (minimum of the 3 core RCTs plus 1 added article).
methods_feedback: Study design is broadly coherent with the stated aim (three conditions; pre/post FNE-B; randomized allocation; SCID-5 screening; exclusion criteria). However, Methods are not “complete and correct”: allocation/condition details are confusing (e.g., participants reportedly assigned to one of the three conditions, later also says “randomly distributed … waitlist, VRET, or VRET + homework” but without clarifying exact group sizes/handling), the timeline is inconsistent/unclear (waitlist described as waiting until the study period is over; session numbers and durations differ across conditions; however, the exact total treatment length and when post-measurement occurs relative to treatment end are not clearly specified for all groups), and key replication details are missing for the analysis described (e.g., how the “difference scores” relate to the stated mixed ANOVA). Some questionnaire/instrument description is adequate (items, scale range, cut-off), but the method does not clearly specify administration/scoring timepoints beyond “pre/post” and does not explain how outliers were handled (kept in analyses) in the Methods. Overall this fits the “adequate/developing” range but not “good/excellent.”
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric evaluates whether the study design is coherent and clearly related to the RQ/hypotheses, and whether participants/materials/procedure are comprehensibly and completely described with enough detail to replicate (including allocation, in-/exclusion, demographics, intervention specifics, questionnaire scoring/timepoints, and correct reporting of key procedural steps). It also considers avoiding incompleteness or incoherence (e.g., mismatched N/df timelines) rather than only providing some high-level details.
results_feedback: Results reporting is partially aligned with the described factorial mixed ANOVA (main effects of time and condition; time×condition interaction) but has multiple internal inconsistencies and insufficient reporting for the claims made. The paper says there were “180 difference scores” yet the ANOVA uses F(1,177)/F(2,177); the degrees of freedom do not align with the stated sample structure (N per condition = 60 each implies df patterns inconsistent with those F-tests). The normality/homoscedasticity statement is too vague (no actual test statistics/values for Shapiro-Levene; says “p-values … insignificant” without specifying which analyses were run). Post-hoc logic is unclear: they report “conditioning on time” vs “conditioning on treatment condition” but then give p-values and comparisons that are not clearly mapped to the ANOVA and Holm procedure; the parentheses/brackets also contain at least one obvious formatting error in the results section (extra parenthesis in “p < .001; p < .001)). Additionally, the Results section includes “Discussions” heading, meaning sectioning is inconsistent. Because of these issues, Results cannot reach the “mostly correct/complete” band.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results rubric checks whether data analysis and reporting are correct, complete, coherent, and relevant to the stated design/procedure. It also expects an appropriate level of statistical knowledge demonstration (e.g., consistent df/df patterns, correct linking of post-hoc comparisons to the correction method and the ANOVA structure, and clear baseline/descriptive tables and plots/figures matching the main analyses).
discussion_feedback: Discussion engages with prior research and interpretations (including expected vs unexpected patterns) and offers several plausible limitations and future directions (scenario characteristics; lack of follow-up; context dependency/generalization). However, the Discussion is weakened by the Results problems: it confidently interprets patterns despite unclear/possibly inconsistent statistics, and it also claims “no difference between VRET and VRET with homework” as a key conclusion without showing the exact relevant omnibus or post-hoc effect clearly in the Results. Some limitations are more speculative than evidence-linked (e.g., “VRET predicts no significant reduction in FNE symptoms” from Kampmann et al.—but the student compares this to their finding about difference vs waitlist without explicitly reconciling effect sizes or significance patterns). Critical reflection could be more tightly grounded in the actual reported tests (and should include at least two specific, design-linked limitations; it does, but some are repeated/speculative). Overall this lands in the mid band.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion rubric emphasizes correct/reasoned connection from results to the main conclusion, with a construct-level interpretation. It also requires responsible use of references, a relevant and convincing critical reflection (limitations/omissions) grounded in the study’s design, and broader implications for theory/practice/society that logically follow from the findings while acknowledging weaknesses.
lang_style_feedback: Language is understandable but contains recurring clarity and academic-style issues: incorrect/unclear headings (“Discussions” in Results), some grammatical errors, occasional awkward phrasing, and inconsistent terminology (e.g., “FNE symptoms”/FNE-B; “VRET exposure-based CBT homework” vs “VRET + homework”). APA/statistical formatting is also inconsistent (e.g., missing spaces, inconsistent F degrees of freedom notation, formatting errors like extra parentheses, and some unclear wording around Holm correction). There are also redundancy and vague statements in crucial parts (e.g., assumption checks described generically). These issues prevent a high score but are not so severe that the paper is unreadable.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric evaluates whether scientific language enables proper understanding, whether paragraphs/components are clearly structured with single topical focus, and whether grammar/spelling and APA referencing/layout are mostly correct with few vague or ambiguous statements. It also rewards consistency of terminology and clean statistical/APA formatting.

intro_feedback: No Introduction section was provided to grade. Based on the supplied Methods/Results/Discussion (which do contain some RQ-relevant framing), the likely weakness is that the paper does not demonstrate enough coherent line of argumentation culminating in a clearly substantiated RQ/hypotheses. Since this cannot be verified from the provided text, the grade is set conservatively.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric expects (a) a clear societal/research problem aligned to the chosen population/design, (b) a theoretical framework that defines constructs and builds logical relationships, (c) explicit RQ and hypotheses that culminate from the line of reasoning, (d) a literature base that is relevant and up to date, and (e) convincing synthesis rather than summary. To score well, the line of argument should be easy to follow from relevance → theory → prior experimental findings → precise RQ → testable hypotheses.
methods_feedback: The Methods provide many key elements: participant recruitment/eligibility, main instruments (FNE-B), intervention overview (VR scenarios, therapist contact, session frequency/duration), and a clear random assignment to three conditions including a waitlist. However, multiple critical details are missing or inconsistent: (1) the Participants section states ages but does not provide mean/SD, gender breakdown, or compensation/allocation details; it also states “180 participants” but later tables show 60 per condition without explicitly stating whether all were included. (2) The Procedure section mixes design details with rationale and includes unclear/duplicated assignment statements (e.g., random distribution sentence appears after consent/preassessment). (3) The Materials section describes FNE-B and scoring/cut-off, but does not specify the full treatment of outcome measurement timing (e.g., exact post-treatment time relative to last session) or how waitlist timing aligns with other conditions. (4) The intervention descriptions include many components of homework, but do not specify standardization/adherence monitoring, therapist qualifications, fidelity checks, or what “exposure progression” concretely means across participants. Overall, methods are substantially complete but not “complete and correct” for replication at bachelor-thesis level.
methods_rubric_feedback: For Methods, the rubric expects a coherent design tied to the RQ/hypotheses, with Participants/materials/procedure described comprehensibly and completely correct. Key checklist items include: demographics (mean age/SD, gender distribution), explicit in-/exclusion and justification, allocation/randomization procedure, compensation details, complete intervention descriptions (differences/similarities across conditions, standardization and timing), and full outcome measurement description (instrument name, items, scale, scoring range, and cut-offs). Procedure should be chronological and not mix too much rationale.
results_feedback: Statistical reporting is mostly coherent (mixed ANOVA with time and condition; interaction interpretation; post-hoc with Holm; outliers handling described). But several parts conflict with required reporting clarity: (1) it says “analysis of 180 difference scores” but then describes a 2-time-point mixed ANOVA (difference scores are not used in that design). (2) Degrees of freedom are inconsistent (you have 3 conditions × 2 times with N=180 total; the reported F values use df like (1,177) and (2,177) and for the interaction (F(2,177)=...) which is unusual for a mixed ANOVA and suggests df may be wrong or misreported. (3) Table 1 and Table 2 are not fully consistent with the narrative (e.g., Table 2 shows means around ~52 for both pre and post despite the main text describing significant reductions; also Table 2 lacks SDs in the snippet). (4) Post-hoc comparisons are hard to follow: conditioning “either on condition or time” is not clearly defined, and some p-values are repeated/parenthesis are mismatched. (5) Including/keeping two outliers is stated, but no explanation of influence/sensitivity analyses is given. Because these issues affect correctness/completeness and the alignment between narrative and tables, the results score is below “adequate/good”.
results_rubric_feedback: For Results, the rubric expects correct and complete reporting aligned with the stated design (e.g., mixed ANOVA vs difference-score analyses must match). It also expects appropriate assumption checks, correct df and test statistics, clear post-hoc/interaction reporting (including correction method), and descriptive statistics/figures/tables that match the narrative patterns. In-depth statistical reasoning is rewarded (but only if correct).
discussion_feedback: The discussion interprets findings and attempts to connect to prior studies and theory, and it includes at least two limitations (unexpected VRET-vs-waitlist pattern; no follow-up). However, the discussion section provided appears to include major argumentation issues relative to the Results: the Results narrative suggests a significant main effect of time and condition and an interaction, but also reports no significant pre-post change for the waitlist and significant pre-post changes for VRET and VRET+homework. The discussion then claims “no difference between VRET and VRET with homework,” which could be consistent, but it does not reconcile the reported post-hoc structure clearly. Additionally, some limitations are framed with strong causal language (e.g., “therefore this introduces a limitation … inconclusive”) without showing that the relevant manipulation checks (immersion/evaluation intensity) exist in the study. Future directions are present (scenario characteristics; adding follow-up), but the paper would benefit from more explicit reflection on internal validity (e.g., adherence to homework, therapist effects, whether VR exposure “dose” was equivalent across conditions). Overall: relevant engagement with limitations and some theory linking, but not convincingly integrated and not fully consistent with reported statistical details.
discussion_rubric_feedback: For Discussion, the rubric expects correct main conclusion and coherent reasoning, explicit comparison to prior research and expectations, relation to the theoretical framework (construct level), and a critical reflection that is relevant and convincingly argued. At bachelor level, limitations should be specific and study-specific (e.g., adherence, fidelity, follow-up absence, measurement timing), and future directions should logically follow from limitations/findings. Implications should be grounded in the actual results.
lang_style_feedback: Overall readability is moderate and scientific tone is generally maintained. However, there are multiple clarity and formatting problems: sectioning issues (a heading “Discussions” instead of “Discussion” and missing/abbreviated Results tables/figures), inconsistent terminology and punctuation, and some errors that impede precise understanding (e.g., “analysis of 180 difference scores” inconsistency; mismatched parentheses in p-values; truncated/duplicated narrative fragments like an extra “))” in the Results). APA/layout is also not consistently followed (e.g., table formatting, figure caption, and citation formatting are sometimes irregular). These issues prevent a high style score even if the content is partly coherent.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language/style rubric focuses on (a) scientific language that does not impede understanding, (b) clear paragraph/component structure with single topical focus, (c) grammatical/spelling quality, (d) APA referencing and layout consistency, and (e) absence of vague/ambiguous statements. Minor errors are tolerated, but systematic inconsistencies (especially in statistics and section structure) reduce this score.

intro_feedback: The provided submission contains Methods and Results plus a “Discussions” section, but no Introduction section. Because the rubric weights Introduction heavily (30% of scientific reasoning and argumentation, plus it also drives the line of reasoning into Discussion), the grade must be based on missing content. This results in a Developing/Adequate-level estimate rather than a rubric-maximizing score.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction rubric expects a clear line of reasoning culminating in the RQ, scientific relevance, and hypothesis, supported by a coherent synthesis of up-to-date empirical/theoretical work. It also expects that the RQ/hypothesis are explicitly stated and appropriately grounded in the literature (typically the core trials plus additional supportive evidence). In addition, references must be relevant and current; older/out-of-scope sources weaken the score. Finally, the section should explicitly connect theory → prior findings → research gap → design choice (end of hourglass).
